$$\eta = \dfrac{P_{mech}}{P_{in}} \times 100\% = \dfrac{18}{24} \times 100\% = 75\%$$

#### Final Result Matrix

| Quantity | Symbol | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Input Power | $P_{in}$ | **24** | W |
| Back-EMF | $E_b$ | **9** | V |
| Heat Loss | $P_{heat}$ | **6** | W |
| Mechanical Power | $P_{mech}$ | **18** | W |
| Torque | $\tau$ | **0.10** | N·m |
| Angular Speed | $\omega$ | **180** | rad/s |
| Efficiency | $\eta$ | **75** | % |

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Energy and Cost of a Pneumatic Valve in a Smart Irrigation System

**Problem Context:** A soil moisture sensor (policy-aware smart object) controls a pneumatic valve that opens for **$t = 5\ \text{minutes}$** each day. The valve's compressor draws **$P = 1.2\ \text{kW}$** during operation. (a) Find the daily energy consumption. (b) Find the monthly cost if electricity is **₹8 per kWh**.

#### Step 1 — Convert time to hours

$$t = 5\ \text{min} = \dfrac{5}{60}\ \text{hr} = 0.0833\ \text{hr}$$

#### Step 2 — Compute daily energy

$$E_{day} = P \cdot t = 1.2 \cdot 0.0833 = 0.1\ \text{kWh}$$

#### Step 3 — Compute monthly energy (30 days)

$$E_{month} = 0.1 \cdot 30 = 3\ \text{kWh}$$

#### Step 4 — Compute monthly cost

$$\text{Cost} = E_{month} \cdot \text{Rate} = 3 \cdot 8 = \text{₹}24$$

#### Analytical Matrix

| Parameter | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Daily ON-time | 5 | min |
| Daily energy | **0.1** | kWh |
| Monthly energy | **3** | kWh |
| Monthly cost | **₹24** | INR |

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: SMA Wire Thermal Actuator Stroke Length

**Problem Context:** A **Nitinol (SMA)** wire used as a soft robotic gripper actuator has an original length $L_0 = 100\ \text{mm}$. The coefficient of thermal expansion is $\alpha = 11 \times 10^{-6}\ /\!^{\circ}\text{C}$. The wire is heated by **$\Delta T = 80^{\circ}\text{C}$**. Calculate the linear expansion $\Delta L$ of the wire.

> **Note on SMA:** Real SMA wires exhibit *phase transformation* contractions of up to **4–8%** when transitioning from martensite to austenite, far exceeding classical thermal expansion. The classical formula below gives the baseline linear thermal component, but the *functional* SMA stroke is dominated by phase change.

#### Step 1 — State the linear thermal expansion formula

$$\Delta L = \alpha \cdot L_0 \cdot \Delta T$$

#### Step 2 — Substitute values

$$\Delta L = (11 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot (100\ \text{mm}) \cdot (80)$$

#### Step 3 — Simplify numerically

$$\Delta L = 11 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 8000 = 88 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{mm} = 0.088\ \text{mm}$$

#### Step 4 — Compare with realistic SMA stroke

The realistic functional stroke (phase transformation) for the same wire would be:
$$\Delta L_{SMA} \approx 0.05 \cdot L_0 = 0.05 \cdot 100 = 5\ \text{mm}$$

This is **~57× larger** than pure thermal expansion, which is why SMA is preferred.

#### Comparative Matrix

| Mechanism | Stroke (mm) | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Pure thermal expansion | **0.088** | Atomic lattice vibration only |
| SMA phase change | **~5.0** | Martensite → Austenite transition |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Six-Family Actuator Taxonomy (Mermaid)

<!-- Mermaid block: All node IDs alphanumeric with letter prefix; no markdown inside quoted labels. -->

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1[ACTUATORS] --> B1[Electrical]
    A1 --> B2[Mechanical]
    A1 --> B3[Hydraulic]
    A1 --> B4[Pneumatic]
    A1 --> B5[Thermal]
    A1 --> B6[Chemical Material]

    B1 --> C1[DC Motor]
    B1 --> C2[Servo Motor]
    B1 --> C3[Stepper Motor]
    B1 --> C4[Solenoid]
    B1 --> C5[Relay]

    B2 --> C6[Rack and Pinion]
    B2 --> C7[Lead Screw]
    B2 --> C8[Cam Mechanism]

    B3 --> C9[Hydraulic Cylinder]
    B3 --> C10[Hydraulic Motor]

    B4 --> C11[Pneumatic Cylinder]
    B4 --> C12[Air Control Valve]

    B5 --> C13[Shape Memory Alloy]
    B5 --> C14[Thermistor Heater]
    B5 --> C15[Peltier Module]

    B6 --> C16[Piezoelectric]
    B6 --> C17[Photoactive Polymer]
    B6 --> C18[Hydrogel]
```

### 4.2 Smart Object Architecture: From Passive Tag to Autonomous Agent

```mermaid
graph LR
    S1[Smart Object Core] --> S2[Unique Identifier: RFID IP URI]
    S1 --> S3[Sensing Subsystem]
    S1 --> S4[Actuation Subsystem]
    S1 --> S5[Embedded Processor: MCU SoC]
    S1 --> S6[Communication: BLE WiFi LoRa]

    S3 --> S7[Sensor Array]
    S4 --> S8[Actuator Array]
    S5 --> S9[Local Decision Logic]
    S6 --> S10[Network Gateway]
```

### 4.3 Three-Level Smart Object Intelligence Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TB
    L1[Level 1: Activity Aware] --> L2[Level 2: Policy Aware]
    L2 --> L3[Level 3: Process Aware]

    L1 --> D1[Sense Only]
    L2 --> D2[Sense plus React with Rules]
    L3 --> D3[Sense plus React plus Collaborate]

    D1 --> E1[Example: Smart Thermometer]
    D2 --> E2[Example: Smart Thermostat with Relay]
    D3 --> E3[Example: Smart Grid Demand Response Node]
```

### 4.4 Complete IoT Loop: Sensor → Smart Object → Actuator (Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1[Physical World Phenomenon] --> P2[Sensor: Transduces to Electrical Signal]
    P2 --> P3[ADC: Analog to Digital Conversion]
    P3 --> P4[Embedded Processor: Local Decision]
    P4 --> P5{Policy Check: Threshold Met?}
    P5 --> P6[No Action: Stay Idle]
    P5 --> P7[Trigger Signal to Actuator]
    P7 --> P8[Actuator Driver Circuit]
    P8 --> P9[Actuator: Electrical to Physical Action]
    P9 --> P10[Physical Change in Environment]
    P10 --> P11[Feedback Sensor: Closes Loop]
    P11 --> P2
    P4 --> P12[Network Transceiver: Cloud Sync]
    P12 --> P13[IoT Cloud Dashboard and Analytics]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question]**

**Q1. Differentiate between a sensor and an actuator with a suitable example. Mention the role of an actuator in an IoT system.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A *sensor* is a device that converts a physical quantity (e.g., temperature, pressure) into an electrical signal, acting as the **input** of an IoT system. Example: DHT22 temperature sensor.
An *actuator* converts an electrical control signal into a physical action, acting as the **output** of an IoT system. Example: A relay that switches ON a fan.
The actuator is the **"effecting" component** of an IoT system — it translates the digital decision (e.g., "temperature is too high") into real-world change (e.g., "fan turns ON"). Without actuators, the IoT system is read-only and cannot influence its environment, making it suitable only for monitoring, not control. *(3 marks — 1.5 for definition, 0.5 for example, 1 for role in IoT)*

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**Q2. List any three classifications of smart objects and briefly explain each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Activity-Aware Smart Object:** Can sense its environment and report it. It is "aware" of activities around it. Example: A motion sensor that logs movement events. *(1 mark)*
2. **Policy-Aware Smart Object:** Can sense and react based on pre-defined rules/policies. Example: A smart AC that turns ON when temperature crosses a threshold. *(1 mark)*
3. **Process-Aware Smart Object:** Can sense, react, and interact/collaborate with other smart objects. Example: A smart grid node that participates in demand-response workflows with peer nodes. *(1 mark)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — ESE Model Paper Pattern, Module 2 Choice-Based]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Actuators in IoT**

> *(a)* Classify actuators based on the **energy domain** of their output. Discuss the working principle, advantages, and limitations of **hydraulic** and **pneumatic** actuators with one IoT application each. *(7 marks)*
> *(b)* A DC motor draws **$4\ \text{A}$** from a **$24\ \text{V}$** supply. The armature resistance is **$R_a = 0.8\ \Omega$** and the torque constant is **$K_t = 0.04\ \text{N·m/A}$**. Calculate the (i) input power, (ii) back-EMF, (iii) mechanical power output, (iv) developed torque, and (v) efficiency of the motor. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution for (a) — Classification, Hydraulic and Pneumatic Actuators (7 marks):**

Actuators are classified into six major families based on the **form of energy** they output: Electrical, Mechanical, Hydraulic, Pneumatic, Thermal, and Chemical/Material. *(1 mark)*

**Hydraulic Actuators** *(3 marks for principle, advantages, limitations, application)*

**Working Principle:** Hydraulic actuators use an **incompressible fluid** (typically mineral oil) pressurized by a pump. The pressurized fluid acts on a piston inside a cylinder. According to Pascal's Law, pressure applied to an enclosed fluid is transmitted undiminished in every direction, generating a large linear force $F = P \cdot A$, where $P$ is fluid pressure and $A$ is the piston area.

**Advantages:**
- Generate **very high forces** (up to thousands of kN) suitable for heavy loads.
- Precise positional control with hydraulic servo valves.
- Self-lubricating and durable for harsh industrial environments.

**Limitations:**
- Require **pumps, reservoirs, and piping** — bulky and expensive.
- Risk of **oil leaks**; fluid viscosity changes with temperature.
- Slower response than electrical actuators.

**IoT Application:** Smart factory robotic arms in automotive assembly, where a centralized IoT controller commands hydraulic actuators to press, lift, or stamp car body panels with forces exceeding 50 kN.

**Pneumatic Actuators** *(3 marks for principle, advantages, limitations, application)*

**Working Principle:** Pneumatic actuators use **compressed air or gas** (compressible) as the working fluid. When air is admitted into one side of a cylinder, it pushes the piston, producing linear motion. Force produced is $F = P_{air} \cdot A_{piston}$.

**Advantages:**
- Clean (no oil contamination) — ideal for food/pharma IoT.
- Lightweight, simple, and low-cost.
- Fast response and safe in explosive environments.

**Limitations:**
- Lower force output than hydraulic (because gases are compressible).
- Air is compressible, so positioning is less precise without feedback.
- Requires air compressor and filtration.

**IoT Application:** Smart greenhouses where IoT sensors (temperature, humidity, CO₂) trigger **pneumatic valves** to control ventilation dampers, enabling climate automation with clean, oil-free actuation suitable for food-grade zones.

---

**Model Solution for (b) — DC Motor Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given Data:**
$$V = 24\ \text{V},\quad I = 4\ \text{A},\quad R_a = 0.8\ \Omega,\quad K_t = 0.04\ \text{N·m/A}$$

**(i) Input Power** *(1.4 marks)*
$$P_{in} = V \cdot I = 24 \cdot 4 = 96\ \text{W}$$

**(ii) Back-EMF** *(1.4 marks)*
$$E_b = V - I \cdot R_a = 24 - (4)(0.8) = 24 - 3.2 = 20.8\ \text{V}$$

**(iii) Mechanical Power Output** *(1.4 marks)*
$$P_{mech} = E_b \cdot I = 20.8 \cdot 4 = 83.2\ \text{W}$$

**Verification using $I^2R$ loss:**
$$P_{heat} = I^2 R_a = (4)^2 \cdot 0.8 = 12.8\ \text{W}$$
$$P_{in} = P_{heat} + P_{mech} \Rightarrow 96 = 12.8 + 83.2 = 96\ \text{W}\ \checkmark$$

**(iv) Developed Torque** *(1.4 marks)*
$$\tau = K_t \cdot I = 0.04 \cdot 4 = 0.16\ \text{N·m}$$

**(v) Efficiency** *(1.4 marks)*
$$\eta = \dfrac{P_{mech}}{P_{in}} \times 100\% = \dfrac{83.2}{96} \times 100\% = 86.67\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit Errors (–1 mark):** Students often forget to convert minutes to hours in energy problems. Always state units in the final answer.
> 2. **Back-EMF Confusion (–2 marks):** A *very common* error is computing $P_{mech} = V \cdot I$ (input power) instead of $E_b \cdot I$. Remember: **mechanical power is computed from back-EMF, not supply voltage**.
> 3. **Missing the $I^2R$ verification step (–1 mark):** KTU examiners award partial credit if you verify $P_{in} = P_{heat} + P_{mech}$. Always include this cross-check.
> 4. **Forgetting $K_t$ vs. $K_v$ distinction (–1 mark):** In SI units, $K_t$ (numerically) = $K_v$ (numerically), but their *units* differ. State units explicitly.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Smart Objects in IoT**

> *(a)* Define a smart object. Explain the **three levels of smart object intelligence** (activity-aware, policy-aware, process-aware) with an IoT example for each. *(7 marks)*
> *(b)* Design a **smart irrigation system** as a policy-aware smart object. Specify the **sensors, actuators, embedded processor, communication module, and decision policy** used. Draw a labelled block diagram. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution for (a) — Smart Object Definition and Three Levels (7 marks):**

**Definition** *(2 marks):*
A smart object is a physical or virtual entity that uniquely identifies itself, **senses** its environment, **actuates** changes, and **communicates** with other entities using an embedded processor and network interface. The four pillars of a smart object are: *Identity + Sensing + Actuation + Computation/Communication*.

**Level 1 — Activity-Aware** *(1.5 marks):*
A smart object that is aware of *what is happening* around it. It can detect and report changes but does not react autonomously. Example: A **smart water meter** that measures consumption and uploads the reading to the cloud every hour. The meter *senses* but does *not* take any action.

**Level 2 — Policy-Aware** *(1.5 marks):*
A smart object that can *react* to environmental changes based on a pre-defined rule/policy. Example: A **smart AC thermostat** that turns ON a compressor relay when the room temperature exceeds 26°C. The decision is local and pre-programmed.

**Level 3 — Process-Aware** *(2 marks):*
A smart object that *collaborates* with other smart objects in a workflow, supports process automation, and self-optimizes. Example: A **smart grid demand-response node** in a peer-to-peer energy trading system. The node negotiates with neighboring nodes to shift loads during peak hours, balancing the grid collectively. The node exhibits autonomy, learning, and inter-object collaboration.

---

**Model Solution for (b) — Smart Irrigation System Design (7 marks):**

**System Design Table** *(4 marks):*

| Subsystem | Component | Specification |
|---|---|---|
| Sensors | Soil moisture sensor (capacitive), DHT22 (temp+humidity), rain detector | Capacitive v1.2, ±3% accuracy |
| Embedded Processor | ESP32 (dual-core, Wi-Fi+BLE, 240 MHz) | Low-power MCU |
| Actuator | 12V DC solenoid valve (pneumatic/hydraulic type) with MOSFET driver | 1.5 bar working pressure |
| Communication | Wi-Fi 802.11 b/g/n (uplink to AWS IoT Core) | MQTT protocol over TLS |
| Power | 12V/5V regulated from solar panel + 18650 Li-ion backup | 10 W solar panel |
| Decision Policy | IF (soil_moisture < 30% AND rain_detected == NO) THEN open_valve(FOR 5 min) | Pre-programmed threshold |

**Block Diagram (Mermaid) — Labelled** *(2 marks):*

```mermaid
graph LR
    Q1[Soil Moisture Sensor] --> Q2[ESP32 MCU]
    Q3[DHT22 Temp Humidity] --> Q2
    Q4[Rain Detector] --> Q2
    Q2 --> Q5{Policy Check}
    Q5 --> Q6[MOSFET Driver]
    Q6 --> Q7[12V Solenoid Valve]
    Q7 --> Q8[Water Flow to Plants]
    Q8 --> Q9[Feedback to Soil Sensor]
    Q2 --> Q10[Wi-Fi MQTT]
    Q10 --> Q11[AWS IoT Cloud Dashboard]
```

**Working Logic (1 mark):**
The ESP32 reads soil moisture every 10 seconds. If moisture < 30% AND no rain is detected, the GPIO pin drives the MOSFET IRF540N, energizing the 12V solenoid valve. Water flows for 5 minutes (180 seconds), then the valve auto-closes. All events are MQTT-published to the cloud for analytics.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout (for Smart Object questions):**
> 1. **Confusing "sensors" with "smart objects" (–2 marks):** A sensor is a *component*; a smart object is a *complete system* that includes sensors, processors, actuators, and communication. Always define smart objects with all **four pillars**.
> 2. **Mixing the three levels (–1 mark):** Students often blur *activity-aware* and *policy-aware*. **Rule of thumb:** *Activity-aware* = sense only. *Policy-aware* = sense + react locally. *Process-aware* = sense + react + collaborate.
> 3. **Forgetting communication in design (–1 mark):** A "smart" object *must* include a communication module (Wi-Fi/LoRa/BLE/Zigbee). KTU penalizes designs without a network subsystem.
> 4. **No feedback loop (–1 mark):** A complete IoT design must include a **closed-loop feedback** from actuator back to sensor. Mention the feedback path explicitly in your block diagram.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Actuators and Smart Objects (Module 2):**

- ☐ An **actuator** is the *output device* of an IoT system; it converts **electrical → physical** action. Sensors do the **opposite** (physical → electrical).
- ☐ The **six families** of actuators: Electrical, Mechanical, Hydraulic, Pneumatic, Thermal, Chemical/Material. Always remember the energy-domain classification.
- ☐ **Hydraulic** actuators = high force, incompressible fluid, heavy industry. **Pneumatic** actuators = clean, compressible air, food/pharma industry.
- ☐ Key DC motor equations: $P_{in} = V \cdot I$, $E_b = V - I R_a$, $P_{mech} = E_b \cdot I$, $\tau = K_t I$, $\eta = P_{mech}/P_{in}$.
- ☐ **Conservation check:** $P_{in} = P_{heat} + P_{mech}$. Always verify this in numericals.
- ☐ A **smart object** has **four pillars**: (1) Unique ID, (2) Sensing, (3) Actuation, (4) Computation + Communication.
- ☐ **Three intelligence levels:** Activity-aware (sense only) → Policy-aware (sense + react) → Process-aware (sense + react + collaborate).
- ☐ **SMA wires** (e.g., Nitinol) are thermal actuators with **5–8% stroke** via phase change — far greater than classical thermal expansion.
- ☐ A complete IoT design must include: **sensor → ADC → MCU → driver → actuator → feedback loop → cloud**.
- ☐ Energy units: $1\ \text{kWh} = 3.6\ \text{MJ} = 1000\ \text{Wh}$. Always convert minutes to hours in cost calculations.
- ☐ Common **valuation traps**: wrong units, missing back-EMF distinction, no feedback loop in diagrams, confusing sensor-only with smart-object.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sensor Networks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECDT752 - INTRODUCTION TO INTERNET OF THINGS > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Sensor Networks # 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Sensor Network** is a distributed infrastructure comprising spatially dispersed autonomous devices called **sensor nodes (or motes)** that cooperatively monitor physical or environmental conditions, such as temperature, sound, vibration, pressure, motion, or pollutants, at different locations. In the context of the **Internet of Things (IoT)**, a sensor network acts as the *perceptual layer* (or sensing layer) — the lowest tier of the standard IoT architecture — responsible for raw data acquisition from the physical world. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A *Wireless Sensor Network (WSN)* is a self-configuring, infrastructure-less network of small, low-power, low-cost sensor nodes that communicate wirelessly over short distances to perform sensing, computation, and communication tasks cooperatively. Each **sensor node** typically integrates four key subsystems: 1. **Sensing Subsystem** — transducers/sensors 2. **Processing Subsystem** — microcontroller/processor with memory 3. **Communication Subsystem** — radio transceiver (RF) 4. **Power Subsystem** — battery or energy-harvesting unit ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > 🧠 **Real-World Analogy — The Human Nervous System** > > Think of a sensor network as the **nervous system of the IoT body**: > - Your **skin, eyes, and ears** = individual **sensor nodes** (they detect stimuli like heat, light, sound) > - Your **spinal cord and brain** = the **sink/gateway** that aggregates nerve signals and processes them > - Your **nerve fibers** = the **wireless communication links** (Wi-Fi, ZigBee, LoRa) that carry signals > - Your **body's energy (glucose)** = the **battery/power unit** of each sensor node > > Just as your nervous system never sleeps and constantly monitors the environment, a sensor network runs 24/7, gathering data from its surroundings and forwarding it to a central decision-making entity (the cloud/server). ## 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU Board Emphasis) | Term | Meaning | |---|---| | **Mote** | Another name for a sensor node | | **Sink / Base Station** | The node that collects all data from the network | | **Gateway** | Device bridging the WSN to the internet/cloud | | **PAN Coordinator** | Central node in a Personal Area Network (e.g., ZigBee) | | **Cluster Head (CH)** | An elected node that aggregates data from a cluster | | **Multi-hop Routing** | Data forwarded via intermediate nodes to reach the sink | | **Data Fusion** | Combining multiple data samples to reduce redundancy | ## 1.4 Standard Metrics (Bolded for Exam Recall) - **Operating Frequency Bands:** 2.4 GHz (ISM band), 868/915 MHz, Sub-1 GHz - **Typical Transmission Range:** **10 m – 100 m** (indoor), up to **1 km** (LoRa) - **Node Power Budget:** **< 100 mW** (battery-driven) - **Network Density:** **10 – 100 nodes per m³** in dense deployments - **Data Rate:** **< 250 kbps** for most WSN standards (ZigBee); LoRa drops to **0.3 – 50 kbps** for long range > [!TIP] > Examiners often test these bold numerical values. Memorize the **2.4 GHz ISM band** and the **ZigBee 250 kbps** data rate as standard KTU short-answer facts. # 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Sensor Node Architecture — Operational Breakdown A sensor node is the **fundamental building block** of any sensor network. Its architecture can be broken down into structured logical layers: ### 🔹 A. Sensing Subsystem - Contains one or more **sensors** (analog) and **ADC (Analog-to-Digital Converter)**. - **Sensors** transduce physical phenomena (temperature, humidity, pressure) into electrical signals. - **Why?** The digital processor cannot interpret analog signals directly; ADC is mandatory. - **Examples:** DHT11 (temp+humidity), LM35 (temperature), MQ-135 (gas), PIR (motion). ### 🔹 B. Processing Subsystem - Comprises a **microcontroller unit (MCU)** like ARM Cortex-M, MSP430, or ATmega328P. - Includes **on-chip memory** (Flash + RAM) and executes embedded software/firmware. - **Why?** Performs local computation, data filtering, encryption, and routing decisions. - **Critical Role:** Reduces transmission load via **in-network processing** (saves energy). ### 🔹 C. Communication Subsystem - A **radio transceiver** (e.g., CC2420 for ZigBee, nRF24L01 for BLE). - Operates in **half-duplex** mode typically (sends OR receives, not both simultaneously). - **Why?** Energy is most spent on communication; thus, this is the dominant power consumer. ### 🔹 D. Power Subsystem - **Battery** (Lithium coin cell, AA) and/or **energy harvesters** (solar, vibration, thermal). - **Power consumption hierarchy:** Communication > Computation > Sensing > Idle (sleep). ## 2.2 Sensor Network Topologies | Topology | Description | Pros | Cons | |---|---|---|---| | **Star** | All nodes connect directly to a central sink | Simple, low latency | Single point of failure, limited scalability | | **Mesh** | Nodes interconnect with multiple neighbors | Self-healing, robust | High energy cost, complex routing | | **Tree (Hierarchical)** | Nodes organized in parent-child clusters | Scalable, energy efficient via clustering | Cluster head failure disrupts subtree | | **Hybrid** | Combines star + mesh | Best of both worlds | Complex management | > [!IMPORTANT] > The **Cluster-Tree topology** is the **KTU-favorite** because it enables **LEACH (Low-Energy Adaptive Clustering Hierarchy)** protocol, which is a guaranteed high-yield topic. ## 2.3 Communication Protocol Stack (Simplified) Following the simplified **WSN protocol stack** (per Akyildiz et al.): | Layer | Function | Example Protocols | |---|---|---| | **Application Layer** | User applications | SNMP, COAP, MQTT | | **Transport Layer** | Reliable data delivery | UDP (mostly), TCP (rare) | | **Network Layer** | Routing | LEACH, PEGASIS, AODV | | **Data Link Layer** | MAC, error control | S-MAC, IEEE 802.15.4 | | **Physical Layer** | Radio modulation, frequency | O-QPSK, DSSS, BFSK | ## 2.4 Communication Technologies Comparison | Technology | Frequency | Range | Data Rate | Power | Best For | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Wi-Fi (802.11)** | 2.4 / 5 GHz | < 100 m | up to 1 Gbps | High | High bandwidth, indoor | | **ZigBee (802.15.4)** | 2.4 GHz | 10–100 m | **250 kbps** | Low | WSN, home automation | | **Bluetooth/BLE** | 2.4 GHz | < 50 m | 1–2 Mbps | Very Low | Wearables, beacons | | **LoRa (LPWAN)** | Sub-1 GHz | **2–15 km** | 0.3–50 kbps | Very Low | Agriculture, smart cities | | **NB-IoT** | Licensed LTE | < 10 km | < 200 kbps | Low | Cellular IoT, metering | | **Z-Wave** | 908.42 MHz | < 30 m | 100 kbps | Low | Smart home | ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Critical for Numerical Problems) > [!IMPORTANT] > The following are the **must-remember** equations for sensor network design and KTU exam numericals. **All energy and lifetime problems hinge on these.** | # | Formula | Description | Variables / Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $E_{Tx}(k,d) = E_{elec} \cdot k + \epsilon_{amp} \cdot k \cdot d^{n}$ | Energy to transmit $k$ bits over distance $d$ | $E_{elec}$ = electronic energy (50 nJ/bit), $\epsilon_{amp}$ = amplifier energy (100 pJ/bit/m²), $n$ = path-loss exponent (**2 = free space, 4 = multipath**) | | 2 | $E_{Rx}(k) = E_{elec} \cdot k$ | Energy to receive $k$ bits | Same $E_{elec}$ | | 3 | $E_{total} = E_{Tx} + E_{Rx}$ | Total energy per packet | Joules (J) | | 4 | $E_{sleep} = V \cdot I_{sleep} \cdot t$ | Energy consumed in sleep mode | $V$=voltage, $I_{sleep}$=sleep current, $t$=time | | 5 | $T_{network} = \dfrac{E_{initial}}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} E_{i,per\,round}}$ | Network lifetime (rounds) | $E_{initial}$ = initial energy, $N$ = nodes | | 6 | $N_{packets} = \dfrac{E_{available}}{E_{Tx} + E_{Rx}}$ | Max packets transmittable per node | Energy per node / Energy per packet | | 7 | $P_{r}(d) = P_{t} \cdot G_{t} \cdot G_{r} \cdot \dfrac{\lambda^{2}}{(4\pi d)^{2}}$ | **Friis Free-Space Path Loss** | $P_{r}$, $P_{t}$ in W; $\lambda$ = wavelength; $G$ = antenna gains | | 8 | $L_{path} = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.44$ (dB) | **Path loss in dB** (km, MHz) | $d$ in km, $f$ in MHz | | 9 | $\text{Data Aggregation Ratio} = \dfrac{P_{in}}{P_{out}}$ | Cluster head compression | $P_{in}$ packets in, $P_{out}$ packets out | | 10 | $S = \dfrac{\text{Coverage Area}}{N \cdot \pi r^{2}}$ | Sensing coverage redundancy | $N$=nodes, $r$=sensing radius | > [!NOTE] > 🔑 **CRITICAL CONSTANTS TO MEMORIZE** (Bolded for KTU exams): > - **$E_{elec} = 50$ nJ/bit** (transmitter/receiver electronics) > - **$\epsilon_{fs} = 10$ pJ/bit/m²** (free-space amplifier) > - **$\epsilon_{mp} = 0.0013$ pJ/bit/m⁴** (multipath amplifier) > - **Path-loss exponent $n = 2$** (free space) or **$n = 4$** (multipath urban) > - **Battery voltage** typical: **3.0 V – 3.7 V** (Li-ion) ### ⚙️ Real-World Engineering Utility - **Energy equation** (1) is used in designing **LEACH/PEGASIS protocols** to maximize network lifetime. - **Friis equation** is essential in **antenna and RF link budget design** for smart agriculture and industrial WSNs. - **Network lifetime** metric decides **battery replacement schedules** in deployment. # 📕 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Derivation #1: Energy Consumption per Node in LEACH ### 📌 Problem Statement A WSN has **100 nodes** uniformly distributed in a 100 m × 100 m field. Each node transmits a **2000-bit** packet directly to the sink located at the center. Using the first-order radio model: - $E_{elec} = 50$ nJ/bit - $\epsilon_{fs} = 10$ pJ/bit/m² - Use **$d^{2}$** path loss (free space). Calculate the total energy dissipated **per round per node**, and the **total network energy per round**. Take average distance $d = 50$ m. ### 🔢 Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1: Recall the energy transmission model (free space, n = 2).** $$ \begin{aligned} E_{Tx}(k,d) &= E_{elec} \cdot k + \epsilon_{fs} \cdot k \cdot d^{2} \end{aligned} $$ *Explanation:* The first term represents energy consumed by the transmitter electronics (radio circuitry). The second term represents energy radiated by the power amplifier to overcome path loss in free space. **Step 2: Substitute given values.** - $k = 2000$ bits - $d = 50$ m - $E_{elec} = 50 \times 10^{-9}$ J/bit - $\epsilon_{fs} = 10 \times 10^{-12}$ J/bit/m² $$ \begin{aligned} E_{Tx} &= (50 \times 10^{-9})(2000) + (10 \times 10^{-12})(2000)(50)^{2} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Compute the first term (electronics energy).** $$ \begin{aligned} E_{elec\,term} &= 50 \times 10^{-9} \times 2000 \\ &= 100{,}000 \times 10^{-9} \\ &= 1.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ J} \end{aligned} $$ *Explanation:* Multiplying 50 nJ/bit by 2000 bits gives the energy needed to run the radio circuitry for one packet. **Step 4: Compute the second term (amplifier energy).** $$ \begin{aligned} E_{amp\,term} &= 10 \times 10^{-12} \times 2000 \times 2500 \\ &= 20{,}000 \times 10^{-12} \times 2500 \\ &= 50{,}000{,}000 \times 10^{-12} \\ &= 5.0 \times 10^{-5} \text{ J} \end{aligned} $$ *Explanation:* $d^{2} = 50^{2} = 2500$ m². The amplifier must boost signal power proportionally to $d^{2}$ to maintain SNR at the receiver. **Step 5: Sum both terms to get $E_{Tx}$.** $$ \begin{aligned} E_{Tx} &= 1.0 \times 10^{-4} + 5.0 \times 10^{-5} \\ &= (10.0 + 5.0) \times 10^{-5} \\ &= 1.5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ J} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6: Compute $E_{Rx}$ (receiver energy = electronics only).** $$ \begin{aligned} E_{Rx} &= E_{elec} \cdot k = (50 \times 10^{-9})(2000) = 1.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ J} \end{aligned} $$ *Explanation:* The receiver only spends energy on its own electronics — no power amplifier is needed. **Step 7: Total energy per node per round.** $$ \begin{aligned} E_{node} &= E_{Tx} + E_{Rx} \\ &= 1.5 \times 10^{-4} + 1.0 \times 10^{-4} \\ &= 2.5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ J} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 8: Total energy of the network per round (all 100 nodes).** $$ \begin{aligned} E_{total} &= N \cdot E_{node} = 100 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-4} = 2.5 \times 10^{-2} \text{ J} = 25 \text{ mJ} \end{aligned} $$ ### ✅ Final Answer - **Energy per node per round: $2.5 \times 10^{-4}$ J (0.25 mJ)** - **Total network energy per round: $2.5 \times 10^{-2}$ J (25 mJ)** --- ## 3.2 Worked Derivation #2: Network Lifetime Estimation ### 📌 Problem Statement Given the same WSN above, each node is initialized with **$E_{initial} = 0.5$ J** of energy. Estimate the **maximum number of rounds** the network can operate before any node exhausts its energy. ### 🔢 Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1: Identify the limiting energy per round per node.** $$ \begin{aligned} E_{per\,round} &= 2.5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ J} \quad \text{(from Derivation 3.1)} \end{aligned} $$ *Explanation:* This assumes uniform energy consumption across all nodes (direct transmission model). **Step 2: Apply the network lifetime formula.** $$ \begin{aligned} T_{lifetime} = \dfrac{E_{initial}}{E_{per\,round}} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Substitute and compute.** $$ \begin{aligned} T_{lifetime} &= \dfrac{0.5}{2.5 \times 10^{-4}} \\ &= \dfrac{0.5}{0.00025} \\ &= 2000 \text{ rounds} \end{aligned} $$ *Explanation:* Each round consumes 0.25 mJ per node. With 0.5 J budget, the node survives 2000 rounds. **Step 4: Account for practical inefficiencies.** In LEACH, only ~5% of nodes act as cluster heads. Energy is not uniform. A more accurate model uses a probability factor $p = 0.05$: $$ \begin{aligned} E_{CH\,per\,round} &= E_{Tx} \cdot \left(\dfrac{N}{p} - 1\right) + E_{Rx} \cdot \dfrac{N}{p} + E_{DA} \cdot \dfrac{N}{p} \end{aligned} $$ where $E_{DA} = 5$ nJ/bit/report is the data aggregation cost. (For advanced problems.) ### ✅ Final Answer - **Direct-transmission lifetime: 2000 rounds** - (LEACH typically yields **3–5× improvement**, i.e., ~6000–10000 rounds) --- ## 3.3 Worked Derivation #3: Multi-Hop Energy vs Direct Transmission ### 📌 Problem Statement A node needs to send a **4000-bit** packet to a sink **80 m away**. Compare energy required for: - **Case A:** Direct transmission (single hop, 80 m). - **Case B:** Multi-hop via 2 intermediate nodes (each 40 m apart). Use: $E_{elec} = 50$ nJ/bit, $\epsilon_{mp} = 0.0013$ pJ/bit/m⁴ (multipath, $d > d_0$). ### 🔢 Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1: Direct transmission (n = 4, multipath).** $$ \begin{aligned} E_{direct} &= E_{elec} \cdot k + \epsilon_{mp} \cdot k \cdot d^{4} \\ &= (50 \times 10^{-9})(4000) + (0.0013 \times 10^{-12})(4000)(80)^{4} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: Compute each term.** $$ \begin{aligned} E_{elec} &= 50 \times 10^{-9} \times 4000 = 2.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ J} \\ E_{amp} &= 0.0013 \times 10^{-12} \times 4000 \times 40{,}960{,}000 \\ &= 5.2 \times 10^{-15} \times 4.096 \times 10^{7} \\ &\approx 2.13 \times 10^{-7} \text{ J} \end{aligned} $$ *Explanation:* $80^{4} = 40{,}960{,}000 \approx 4.096 \times 10^{7}$. The amplifier energy is small in this case. **Step 3: Sum.** $$ \begin{aligned} E_{direct} &\approx 2.0 \times 10^{-4} + 2.13 \times 10^{-7} \approx 2.002 \times 10^{-4} \text{ J} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4: Multi-hop transmission (2 hops × 40 m each, plus 2 receptions).** $$ \begin{aligned} E_{hop} &= E_{elec} \cdot k + \epsilon_{mp} \cdot k \cdot (40)^{4} \\ E_{mp\,term} &= 0.0013 \times 10^{-12} \times 4000 \times 2{,}560{,}000 \\ &= 5.2 \times 10^{-15} \times 2.56 \times 10^{6} \\ &\approx 1.33 \times 10^{-8} \text{ J} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} E_{Tx\,per\,hop} &= 2.0 \times 10^{-4} + 1.33 \times 10^{-8} \approx 2.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ J} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5: Two transmissions + two receptions total.** $$ \begin{aligned} E_{multi} &= 2 \cdot E_{Tx\,per\,hop} + 1 \cdot E_{Rx\,intermediate} \\ &= 2(2.0 \times 10^{-4}) + 1(2.0 \times 10^{-4}) \\ &= 6.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ J} \end{aligned} $$ ### ✅ Final Comparison | Mode | Energy | |---|---| | Direct (80 m) | $2.0 \times 10^{-4}$ J | | Multi-hop (2×40 m) | $6.0 \times 10^{-4}$ J | > [!TIP] > **Key Insight:** Direct transmission is **3× more efficient** here because the distances are short enough to stay in the free-space regime. Multi-hop only wins when the **single-hop distance is very large** (where $d^{4}$ dominates). # 📙 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Sensor Node Internal Architecture (Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TB POW["POWER UNIT
Battery or Energy Harvester"] SEN["SENSING UNIT
Analog Sensors and ADC"] PROC["PROCESSING UNIT
MCU and Memory"] COM["COMMUNICATION UNIT
RF Transceiver and Antenna"] LOC["LOCATION FINDING
GPS Module Optional"] MOB["MOBILIZER
Optional Wheels"] APP["APPLICATION
User Software"] POW --> SEN POW --> PROC POW --> COM POW --> LOC POW --> MOB SEN -- "Raw Analog Data" --> PROC PROC -- "Control Signals" --> SEN PROC -- "Data Packets" --> COM COM -- "RF Wireless Signal" --> PROC LOC -- "Position Data" --> PROC APP --> PROC ``` **Explanation:** The **Power Unit** is the lifeline — it supplies energy to every other block. The **Processing Unit (MCU)** is the central brain, coordinating sensing, communication, and optional modules. The **Sensing Unit** captures physical phenomena, the **Communication Unit** transmits data wirelessly, and the optional **Location** and **Mobilizer** blocks enable mobility and geographic awareness. --- ## 4.2 Sensor Network Topology Comparison Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph STAR["STAR TOPOLOGY"] S1["Node 1"] S2["Node 2"] S3["Node 3"] S4["Node 4"] SINK1["SINK"] S1 --- SINK1 S2 --- SINK1 S3 --- SINK1 S4 --- SINK1 end subgraph MESH["MESH TOPOLOGY"] M1["Node 1"] M2["Node 2"] M3["Node 3"] M4["Node 4"] M5["Node 5"] M1 --- M2 M2 --- M3 M3 --- M4 M4 --- M5 M5 --- M1 M1 --- M3 M2 --- M4 end subgraph TREE["CLUSTER TREE TOPOLOGY"] CH["Cluster Head"] T1["Member 1"] T2["Member 2"] T3["Member 3"] T4["Member 4"] CH --- T1 CH --- T2 CH --- T3 CH --- T4 end ``` **Explanation:** - **Star:** All nodes talk directly to a central sink. Simple but fragile. - **Mesh:** Every node connects to multiple neighbors, providing redundancy and self-healing. - **Cluster-Tree:** Nodes form clusters; a **Cluster Head (CH)** aggregates data and forwards to the sink. This is the **energy-efficient choice** used in LEACH. --- ## 4.3 WSN Protocol Stack Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB APP["APPLICATION LAYER
SNMP MQTT COAP"] TRANS["TRANSPORT LAYER
UDP TCP"] NET["NETWORK LAYER
LEACH PEGASIS AODV"] DLL["DATA LINK LAYER
S-MAC IEEE 802.15.4"] PHY["PHYSICAL LAYER
Modulation and Radio"] APP --> TRANS TRANS --> NET NET --> DLL DLL --> PHY ``` **Explanation:** The WSN protocol stack is a **5-layer simplification** of the OSI model. The **Application Layer** delivers sensor data to the user, while the **Physical Layer** handles the radio's electrical signaling. Energy management crosscuts all layers (a power management plane exists alongside this stack in advanced models). --- ## 4.4 Data Flow in a Typical IoT Sensor Network ```mermaid flowchart LR PHY["PHYSICAL WORLD
Temperature Motion Gas"] N1["Sensor Node A"] N2["Sensor Node B"] N3["Sensor Node C"] GW["GATEWAY or SINK"] CL["CLOUD SERVER"] USR["USER APPLICATION"] PHY --> N1 PHY --> N2 PHY --> N3 N1 -- "Wireless Multi Hop" --> GW N2 -- "Wireless Multi Hop" --> GW N3 -- "Wireless Multi Hop" --> GW GW -- "Internet" --> CL CL -- "Visualization" --> USR ``` **Explanation:** Physical phenomena are sensed by distributed nodes, which wirelessly forward data (often via multi-hop routing) to a **gateway/sink**. The gateway bridges the WSN to the internet, sending data to a **cloud server** for storage, analytics, and visualization in user applications (mobile apps, dashboards). # 📓 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 🅰️ PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define a Wireless Sensor Network (WSN). List any FOUR components of a sensor node.** **Model Answer:** A **Wireless Sensor Network (WSN)** is a self-organized, infrastructure-less network of spatially distributed, low-power, low-cost sensor nodes that cooperatively sense, process, and wirelessly communicate data about physical phenomena to a central sink or gateway. **Four components of a sensor node:** 1. **Sensing Unit** — sensors + ADC for physical-to-digital conversion 2. **Processing Unit** — microcontroller + memory for computation 3. **Communication Unit** — RF transceiver for wireless data transfer 4. **Power Unit** — battery/energy harvester for power supply *(Optional 5th: Location/Mobilizer — 0.5 mark extra if mentioned.)* **Mark Distribution:** Definition (1.5) + Components (1.5) --- ### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Compare ZigBee and Wi-Fi as communication technologies for IoT sensor networks. (Mention frequency, range, data rate, power.)** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | ZigBee (IEEE 802.15.4) | Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11) | |---|---|---| | Frequency | **2.4 GHz** (also 868/915 MHz) | **2.4 / 5 GHz** | | Range | **10 – 100 m** | **< 100 m** (indoor) | | Data Rate | **250 kbps** | up to **1 Gbps** | | Power | **Very Low** (years on battery) | **High** (hours on battery) | | Best Use | WSN, home automation | High-bandwidth indoor apps | **Conclusion:** ZigBee is preferred for **low-power, low-data-rate WSN** deployments; Wi-Fi suits **high-throughput, mains-powered** devices. **Mark Distribution:** Table (2 marks) + Conclusion (1 mark) --- ## 5.2 🅱️ PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** Explain the **architecture of a sensor node** with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of each block. **(7 marks)** **(b)** What is **LEACH protocol**? Explain how clustering reduces energy consumption in WSN. **(7 marks)** --- #### ✍️ Model Solution for Q.A (a): The **architecture of a sensor node** consists of four main subsystems and two optional ones, as shown in the block diagram: | Block | Role | |---|---| | **Sensing Unit** | Comprises physical sensors (e.g., LM35, DHT11) and an **ADC**. Converts physical phenomena into digital signals. | | **Processing Unit** | A **microcontroller (e.g., ARM Cortex-M, MSP430)** with on-chip memory. Runs OS/firmware, performs local processing, data aggregation, and routing. | | **Communication Unit** | **RF Transceiver** (e.g., CC2420) and antenna. Handles wireless data transmission/reception. | | **Power Unit** | **Battery** (Li-ion/coin cell) or **energy harvester** (solar/vibration). Most critical — limits node lifetime. | | **Location Finding** (optional) | **GPS module** for geo-tagging sensor data. | | **Mobilizer** (optional) | Wheels/motors for mobile sensor nodes. | **Energy Hierarchy (important):** Communication > Computation > Sensing > Sleep. **Mark Split:** Block diagram (3) + 4 blocks explained (3) + Energy hierarchy (1) --- #### ✍️ Model Solution for Q.A (b): **LEACH (Low-Energy Adaptive Clustering Hierarchy)** is a **cluster-based, hierarchical routing protocol** proposed by **Heinzelman et al. (2000)** for WSNs. **Key Idea:** Divide the network into **clusters**; each cluster elects a **Cluster Head (CH)** that aggregates data from member nodes and forwards a single compressed packet to the sink. **How Clustering Reduces Energy:** 1. **Localized Communication:** Member nodes transmit short distances to CH (energy $\propto d^{2}$). Direct long-range transmission to the sink is avoided. 2. **Data Aggregation:** CH fuses $N$ packets into 1, reducing total bits transmitted (saves radio energy). 3. **CH Rotation:** Cluster-head role is **rotated randomly** every round — distributes energy load evenly (avoids "hot-spot" problem). 4. **Sleep Scheduling:** Non-CH nodes sleep when not transmitting — saves idle/listening power. **Steady-State Phase:** TDMA schedule used; CH applies **data aggregation** ($E_{DA} = 5$ nJ/bit/report). **Result:** LEACH achieves **3–5× network lifetime improvement** over direct transmission. **Mark Split:** LEACH definition (2) + Clustering energy savings (3) + CH rotation/aggregation (2) --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** *(Alternative Choice)* `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a)** Explain the **first-order radio energy model** used in WSNs. Derive expressions for $E_{Tx}$ and $E_{Rx}$. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A WSN node transmits a **3000-bit** packet over a distance of **60 m**. Compute the energy dissipated in transmission, using the free-space model with $E_{elec} = 50$ nJ/bit and $\epsilon_{fs} = 10$ pJ/bit/m². **(7 marks)** --- #### ✍️ Model Solution for Q.B (a): The **first-order radio model** (Heinzelman, 2000) is the standard energy model for WSN simulation (LEACH, PEGASIS). Assumptions: - Radio dissipates $E_{elec}$ to run transmitter/receiver circuitry. - Transmit amplifier dissipates $\epsilon_{amp}$ to achieve acceptable SNR at receiver. - **Free-space path loss** ($\propto d^{2}$) for $d < d_0$, and **multipath loss** ($\propto d^{4}$) for $d \geq d_0$. **Transmit Energy:** $$ \begin{aligned} E_{Tx}(k,d) = E_{elec} \cdot k + \epsilon_{fs} \cdot k \cdot d^{2} \quad (d < d_0) \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} E_{Tx}(k,d) = E_{elec} \cdot k + \epsilon_{mp} \cdot k \cdot d^{4} \quad (d \geq d_0) \end{aligned} $$ **Receive Energy:** $$ \begin{aligned} E_{Rx}(k) = E_{elec} \cdot k \end{aligned} $$ Where: - $k$ = number of bits - $E_{elec}$ = 50 nJ/bit - $\epsilon_{fs} = 10$ pJ/bit/m² (free space) - $\epsilon_{mp} = 0.0013$ pJ/bit/m⁴ (multipath) - $d_0 = \sqrt{\epsilon_{fs}/\epsilon_{mp}} \approx 87$ m **Mark Split:** Assumptions (2) + $E_{Tx}$ derivation (2.5) + $E_{Rx}$ derivation (1.5) + $d_0$ formula (1) --- #### ✍️ Model Solution for Q.B (b): **Given:** $k = 3000$ bits, $d = 60$ m, $E_{elec} = 50$ nJ/bit, $\epsilon_{fs} = 10$ pJ/bit/m². **Step 1:** Since $d = 60$ m $< d_0 \approx 87$ m, use the **free-space model** ($n = 2$). $$ \begin{aligned} E_{Tx} &= E_{elec} \cdot k + \epsilon_{fs} \cdot k \cdot d^{2} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2:** Substitute values. $$ \begin{aligned} E_{Tx} &= (50 \times 10^{-9})(3000) + (10 \times 10^{-12})(3000)(60)^{2} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3:** Compute electronics term. $$ \begin{aligned} E_{elec} = 50 \times 10^{-9} \times 3000 = 150{,}000 \times 10^{-9} = 1.5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ J} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4:** Compute amplifier term. $d^{2} = 3600$ m². $$ \begin{aligned} E_{amp} &= 10 \times 10^{-12} \times 3000 \times 3600 \\ &= 30{,}000 \times 10^{-12} \times 3600 \\ &= 1.08 \times 10^{-4} \text{ J} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5:** Total transmission energy. $$ \begin{aligned} E_{Tx} &= 1.5 \times 10^{-4} + 1.08 \times 10^{-4} \\ &= 2.58 \times 10^{-4} \text{ J} = 0.258 \text{ mJ} \end{aligned} $$ ### ✅ Final Answer: $E_{Tx} = 2.58 \times 10^{-4}$ J = 0.258 mJ **Mark Split:** Formula (2) + Substitution (1) + Electronics term (1.5) + Amplifier term (1.5) + Final answer (1) --- ## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:** > > 1. **Unit Confusion (–1 to –2 marks):** Writing nJ without converting to J when using $\epsilon_{fs}$ in pJ. **Always convert to SI units (Joules) first.** > 2. **Wrong Path-Loss Exponent (–2 marks):** Using $d^{2}$ for long distances (> $d_0$) or $d^{4}$ for short distances. **Check the threshold** $d_0 = \sqrt{\epsilon_{fs}/\epsilon_{mp}} \approx 87$ m. > 3. **Forgetting $E_{Rx}$ (–1 mark):** Energy problems often ask for *total* energy — students compute $E_{Tx}$ only. **Always add $E_{Rx} = E_{elec} \cdot k$** unless explicitly excluded. > 4. **Missing ADC in Block Diagram (–1 mark):** Examiner expects **Sensors + ADC** as a unit, not just "sensors." > 5. **Confusing ZigBee and Bluetooth (–1 mark):** ZigBee = 802.15.4 (mesh-capable); BLE = 802.15.1 (point-to-point). **Don't interchange them.** > 6. **Skipping Units in Final Answer (–0.5 mark):** Always write **J or mJ** alongside the numerical value. --- ## 5.4 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - ✅ **WSN = distributed, self-organizing, low-power, low-cost sensor nodes** cooperatively monitoring physical phenomena. - ✅ **Four core components:** Sensing, Processing, Communication, Power (the **Power Unit** is the most critical — it dictates node lifetime). - ✅ **Energy hierarchy:** Communication > Computation > Sensing > Idle/Sleep. **→ Minimize radio usage!** - ✅ **Standard protocol stack:** 5 layers (Application → Transport → Network → Data Link → Physical) + cross-cutting **Power Management Plane** + **Mobility Plane** + **Task Management Plane** (Akyildiz model). - ✅ **Topologies:** Star (simple), Mesh (robust, high-energy), Cluster-Tree (LEACH — best for energy). - ✅ **Standard bands & rates:** ZigBee at **2.4 GHz / 250 kbps**; Wi-Fi at 2.4/5 GHz; LoRa at Sub-1 GHz for **2–15 km** range. - ✅ **First-order radio model:** $E_{Tx} = E_{elec} \cdot k + \epsilon_{amp} \cdot k \cdot d^{n}$, $E_{Rx} = E_{elec} \cdot k$. **Constants:** $E_{elec} = 50$ nJ/bit, $\epsilon_{fs} = 10$ pJ/bit/m², $\epsilon_{mp} = 0.0013$ pJ/bit/m⁴, $d_0 \approx 87$ m. - ✅ **LEACH protocol:** Cluster-based, **CH rotation every round**, **data aggregation** at CH, **3–5× lifetime improvement** vs direct transmission. - ✅ **Friis equation:** $P_{r} = P_{t} \cdot G_{t} G_{r} \lambda^{2} / (4\pi d)^{2}$ — used in **RF link budget design**. - ✅ **Multi-hop trade-off:** Multi-hop is **energy-efficient for long distances** ($d^{4}$ regime); direct transmission wins for short distances ($d^{2}$ regime). - ✅ **Data Aggregation Ratio (DAR):** $P_{in}/P_{out}$ — higher DAR = more energy saved. - ✅ **MAC protocols:** **S-MAC** uses sleep cycles; **IEEE 802.15.4** is the base of ZigBee. - ✅ **Routing protocols:** LEACH, PEGASIS, TEEN, APTEEN — all are **hierarchical/cluster-based** for energy efficiency. - ✅ **Smart agriculture, healthcare, industrial monitoring, smart cities** — all major **WSN application domains** (cite in long answers for 1–2 extra marks). - ✅ **Mote = sensor node; Sink = data collector; Gateway = bridge to internet; Cluster Head = local aggregator.**

topics.title: Connecting Smart Objects
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT752 - INTRODUCTION TO INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Connecting Smart Objects

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

**Connecting Smart Objects** refers to the engineering discipline of providing network and data-link layer connectivity to resource-constrained embedded devices (sensors, actuators, RFID tags, microcontrollers) so that they can participate as first-class nodes in the broader Internet of Things (IoT) ecosystem. It encompasses the selection of physical mediums (wired or wireless), link-layer protocols, adaptation mechanisms (such as **6LoWPAN**), routing strategies, and gateway mediation techniques that collectively enable "things" to publish, subscribe, and exchange data with cloud services, edge nodes, and other peers.

In the formal KTU 2024 schema, the term explicitly covers:

1. **Communication criteria** for smart objects (range, bandwidth, energy budget, security posture).
2. **IoT access technologies** spanning PAN, LAN, MAN, and WAN classes.
3. **IoT protocol stack** mapping (Link, Adaptation, Transport, Application).
4. **Gateway functionality** acting as protocol-translating intermediaries.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PECDT752, Module 2):** Students are expected to compare IoT-specific access technologies (e.g., **IEEE 802.15.4**, **ZigBee**, **Bluetooth LE**, **LoRaWAN**, **NB-IoT**, **LTE-M**) in terms of *range, data rate, power, topology, and typical use-cases*. Memorizing frequency bands and modulation schemes alone will not earn full marks; *engineering trade-off reasoning* is required.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Postal-Service Analogy**
>
> Imagine a smart object as a person in a remote village who wants to send a letter to someone in a city. Several problems arise:
> - The village has **no post office** (no native IP address).
> - The person can only **walk a few meters** before getting tired (limited radio range).
> - The letter is written on a **tiny piece of paper** (small MTU).
> - The person has **very little ink** (limited battery).
>
> "Connecting Smart Objects" is essentially the engineering effort of giving this villager a *miniature, ultra-low-power walkie-talkie* that compresses the letter (**6LoWPAN header compression**), hops messages through neighbours until a **post office** (gateway) is reached, and from there hands it over to the conventional **postal trucks** (TCP/IP over the Internet). The choice of walkie-talkie model (ZigBee, BLE, LoRa, etc.) is dictated by how far the message must travel, how much ink may be spent, and how fast it must arrive.

### 1.3 Defining Attributes of "Connected" Smart Objects

A smart object is considered *effectively connected* when it satisfies four engineering criteria:

| Attribute | Standard Metric (KTU Expected) |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Identity** | Globally unique 64-bit or 128-bit address (EUI-64 / IPv6) |
| **Reachability** | Discoverable and addressable across heterogeneous networks |
| **Energy Autonomy** | Operates for years on coin-cell or harvested energy (often **< 1 mW** average) |
| **Semantic Interoperability** | Data is interpretable via a shared data model (e.g., JSON, SenML, LwM2M) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Constant for Low-Power Radio**
> Typical transmit current for **IEEE 802.15.4** at **0 dBm** ≈ **17.4 mA**, with receive current ≈ **18.8 mA**. Compare to Wi-Fi transmit at ≈ **200 mA** — this 10× energy gap is the *primary driver* of low-power IoT radio design.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Communication Criteria for Smart Objects — The Five Pillars

Every IoT access technology is evaluated against five orthogonal criteria. The KTU examiner will almost always frame a 14-mark question as a *comparative analysis* using these pillars.

1. **Range** — maximum communication distance.
   - Personal Area Network (PAN): **0.1 – 10 m** (BLE, NFC, RFID).
   - Local Area Network (LAN): **30 – 100 m** (Wi-Fi, ZigBee).
   - Metropolitan / Wide Area Network (MAN/WAN): **1 – 15 km** urban, up to **40 km** line-of-sight (LoRa, SigFox, NB-IoT).

2. **Data Rate** — usable bits per second.
   - NB-IoT: ~**26 kbps** DL / ~**62 kbps** UL (Release 13).
   - LoRaWAN: **0.3 – 50 kbps** depending on Spreading Factor (SF7–SF12).
   - BLE 5.0: up to **2 Mbps** PHY.
   - Wi-Fi 802.11n: up to **600 Mbps** (theoretical).

3. **Power Consumption** — average current draw.
   - Expressed in Joules/bit or mAh/year.
   - LoRaWAN Class A end-device: ~**0.7 – 1.5 mAh/day** (10-byte uplink every hour).
   - ZigBee end-device: ~**0.2 mA average** in sleep; bursts at **30 mA** TX.

4. **Topology** — star, mesh, or hybrid.
   - BLE: **Star** (master → 7 slaves).
   - ZigBee: **Mesh** (up to 65 000 nodes via routers).
   - LoRaWAN: **Star-of-stars** (end-devices → gateways → network server).
   - 6LoWPAN / RPL: **DODAG** mesh (Destination Oriented Directed Acyclic Graph).

5. **Security** — encryption, authentication, integrity.
   - BLE: AES-128 CCM.
   - ZigBee 3.0: AES-128-CCM with Trust Center link keys.
   - LoRaWAN 1.1: Two session keys (NwkSKey, AppSKey) + DevNonce replay protection.
   - NB-IoT: LTE-grade mutual authentication + AS / NAS security.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Trade-off Principle (Mark Frequently in KTU):**
> *Range, data rate, and power are in a three-way tension* governed by the **Shannon–Hartley theorem**. Increasing range or data rate at a fixed noise floor forces a higher transmit power. Low-power IoT radios therefore trade **bit rate** for **link budget** through techniques such as *chirp spread spectrum* (LoRa) or *narrowband + high SF repetition* (NB-IoT).

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Formula 1 — Free-Space Path Loss (Friis Equation)**

$$PL_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(d) + 20 \log_{10}(f) + 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi}{c}\right)$$

Where $d$ is distance in metres, $f$ is frequency in Hz, $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s.

**Utility:** Used to compute *maximum range* given a transmitter's EIRP and a receiver's sensitivity. KTU may ask: "If TX power is **+14 dBm**, RX sensitivity is **-137 dBm**, operating at **868 MHz**, what is the theoretical range?" (Path loss budget = 151 dB ⇒ ~150 km — but environmental clutter and antenna gain reduce this to ~5–10 km.)

---

> [!NOTE]
> **Formula 2 — Link Budget**

$$PL_{max} = P_{TX} + G_{TX} + G_{RX} - S_{RX} - M_{fade}$$

Where $P_{TX}$ is transmit power (dBm), $G_{TX}/G_{RX}$ are antenna gains (dBi), $S_{RX}$ is receiver sensitivity (dBm), $M_{fade}$ is the fade margin (typically **20 – 30 dB** for outdoor IoT).

---

> [!NOTE]
> **Formula 3 — Shannon–Hartley Channel Capacity**

$$C = B \log_{2}\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) \;\; \text{[bits/s]}$$

Where $B$ is bandwidth (Hz), $S/N$ is signal-to-noise ratio (linear). This is the *theoretical ceiling* of IoT link capacity.

---

> [!NOTE]
> **Formula 4 — Battery Lifetime (Simplified Peukert-aware model)**

$$T_{years} = \frac{C_{mAh} \times V}{I_{avg} \times V \times 8760} = \frac{C_{mAh}}{I_{avg} \times 8760}$$

Where $C_{mAh}$ is battery capacity, $I_{avg}$ is average current draw. A **2400 mAh** coin cell with $I_{avg} = 100\,\mu A$ lasts **≈ 2.74 years** without duty-cycling.

---

> [!NOTE]
> **Formula 5 — LoRa Time-on-Air (Approximate)**

$$T_{packet} = T_{preamble} + T_{payload} = \left(N_{pre} + 4.25\right) T_{sym} + 8 + \max\!\left(\left\lceil\frac{8PL - 4SF + 28 + 16CRC - 20H}{4(SF - 2DE)}\right\rceil (CR + 4), 0\right) \cdot T_{sym}$$

Where $T_{sym} = \frac{2^{SF}}{BW}$. This is occasionally tested in design questions (battery-life analysis).

---

> [!NOTE]
> **Formula 6 — 6LoWPAN Header Compression Efficiency**

Compression gain = $\frac{\text{IPv6 header (40 bytes)} - \text{Compressed header (typically 2–6 bytes)}}{40} \times 100\%$ ≈ **85 – 95 %**.

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Derivation — Range from Link Budget (LoRaWAN 868 MHz)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Numerical Example (Frequently Asked in KTU):**
> A Kerala-based agricultural sensor uses **LoRaWAN at 868 MHz**, transmitting at **+14 dBm**, with a 2 dBi monopole antenna, the gateway has a 6 dBi collinear antenna and sensitivity of **−137 dBm**. A fade margin of **25 dB** is mandated by terrain. Calculate the **maximum range in line-of-sight** using the Friis free-space equation.

**Step 1 — Identify the link budget equation.**

The maximum allowable path loss $PL_{max}$ (in dB) is given by:

$$PL_{max} = P_{TX} + G_{TX} + G_{RX} - S_{RX} - M_{fade}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values.**

$$P_{TX} = +14 \text{ dBm}, \quad G_{TX} = +2 \text{ dBi}, \quad G_{RX} = +6 \text{ dBi}$$

$$S_{RX} = -137 \text{ dBm}, \quad M_{fade} = 25 \text{ dB}$$

$$PL_{max} = 14 + 2 + 6 - (-137) - 25 = 134 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 3 — Convert dB to linear ratio and apply Friis equation.**

Friis in linear form:

$$\frac{P_r}{P_t} = G_t G_r \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^{2}$$

Taking logarithms (base 10):

$$PL_{dB} = -10 \log_{10}(G_t G_r) + 20 \log_{10}(d) + 20 \log_{10}(f) + 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi}{c}\right)$$

For isotropic antennas ($G_t = G_r = 1$ in dBi = 0 dB), and noting that $f = 868\,\text{MHz} = 8.68 \times 10^{8}\,\text{Hz}$:

$$20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi}{c}\right) = -147.55 \text{ dB (at f = 868 MHz reference)}$$

Equivalently, in compact form for a 868 MHz link:

$$PL_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(d) + 91.45 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for d.**

Setting $PL_{max} = 134$ dB:

$$134 = 20 \log_{10}(d) + 91.45$$

$$20 \log_{10}(d) = 42.55$$

$$\log_{10}(d) = 2.1275$$

$$d = 10^{2.1275} = 134 \text{ metres (free-space)}$$

**Step 5 — Account for non-ideal ground reflection.**

A more realistic empirical model (log-distance path loss, exponent $n = 2.5$ for suburban Kerala terrain) is:

$$PL_{dB}(d) = PL_0 + 10 n \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{d}{d_0}\right)$$

Using $d_0 = 1$ m, $PL_0 = 40.45$ dB at 868 MHz:

$$134 = 40.45 + 25 \log_{10}(d)$$

$$\log_{10}(d) = \frac{93.55}{25} = 3.742$$

$$d = 5.52 \text{ km}$$

This is consistent with the empirically reported **3 – 8 km** LoRaWAN range in Kerala's plantation terrain.

### 3.2 Detailed Derivation — 6LoWPAN Fragmentation Threshold

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example — Maximum IPv6 Datagram Over IEEE 802.15.4:**
> IEEE 802.15.4 specifies a maximum MAC frame size of **127 bytes**. After deducting MAC header (**25 bytes**), security overhead (**21 bytes** max), and the 6LoWPAN dispatch byte (**1 byte**), the maximum IPv6 payload is **80 bytes** per fragment. Show that **two fragments** are required for a typical IPv6 packet with a 40-byte IPv6 header and a 60-byte UDP payload (total 100 bytes IP packet), and that no more than **one 6LoWPAN fragment header** is needed.

**Step 1 — Total IPv6 datagram size.**

$$L_{IP} = L_{header} + L_{payload} = 40 + 60 = 100 \text{ bytes}$$

**Step 2 — Maximum unfragmented 6LoWPAN payload.**

The IEEE 802.15.4 MAC frame budget is:

$$L_{budget} = 127 - 25_{MAC} - 21_{SEC} - 1_{dispatch} = 80 \text{ bytes}$$

**Step 3 — Determine fragmentation.**

Since $L_{IP} = 100$ bytes > $L_{budget} = 80$ bytes, fragmentation is required. We compute the number of fragments:

$$N_{fragments} = \left\lceil\frac{L_{IP}}{L_{budget}}\right\rceil = \left\lceil\frac{100}{80}\right\rceil = 2$$

**Step 4 — Verify reassembly buffer at receiver.**

Fragment 1 carries 80 bytes (1 dispatch + 79 IP bytes).
Fragment 2 carries 100 − 80 + 1 dispatch + 1 fragment header = 22 bytes.
Both fit inside the 127-byte MAC frame: ✓.

**Step 5 — Header compression gain.**

Without 6LoWPAN: 40-byte IPv6 header sent in full.
With IPHC (IP Header Compression) + NHC (Next-Header Compression): compressed to **2 – 4 bytes** for local communication.

$$\text{Compression ratio} = \frac{40 - 3}{40} = 92.5\%$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Why a Given Technology is Chosen

| Constraint | Recommended Technology | Justification |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Battery life > 5 years, 1 uplink/hour, 8 km range | **LoRaWAN** | Class A duty cycle < 1 %, SF7–SF12 chirp spread spectrum |
| High data (video), mains-powered, indoor | **Wi-Fi** | 600 Mbps capacity, infrastructure already in homes/offices |
| Voice, mobility across cells | **LTE-M (Cat-M1)** | LTE handover support, ~1 Mbps, VoLTE capable |
| Underground water meter (Kerala tile-roof housing) | **NB-IoT** | 164 dB MCL, penetrates concrete, uses existing LTE |
| Body-area network (heart-rate, ECG) | **BLE 5.0** | 2 Mbps bursts, < 15 mA peak, smartphone gateway |
| Industry 4.0 actuator mesh | **ZigBee 3.0 / Thread / WirelessHART** | Self-healing mesh, deterministic routing (TSCH) |
| Inductive-loop toll / payment | **RFID (HF 13.56 MHz)** | Zero-battery tag, < 1 m range acceptable |
| City-wide waste-bin level sensing | **SigFox / LoRaWAN** | Ultra-low payload (12 bytes), long range, very low OPEX |

### 3.4 Connectivity Architecture — End-to-End Path

> [!NOTE]
> **Step-by-step "smart object to cloud" data path** (essential for 7-mark sub-questions):
>
> 1. **Smart Object (sensor / MCU)** samples physical world (e.g., temperature = 28.4 °C).
> 2. **PHY / MAC Layer** (e.g., IEEE 802.15.4) frames the sample into an 802.15.4 packet.
> 3. **6LoWPAN Adaptation Layer** compresses the IPv6 header, fragments if needed.
> 4. **RPL Routing** (if mesh) routes the packet hop-by-hop toward the gateway along a DODAG.
> 5. **Gateway / Edge** (e.g., Raspberry Pi + SX1301 LoRa concentrator) decapsulates 6LoWPAN, rebuilds a full IPv6/UDP datagram, translates to TCP/IP, and forwards over Ethernet/Wi-Fi to the Internet.
> 6. **Cloud Broker** (e.g., MQTT broker, LwM2M server) authenticates the device (DTLS / TLS) and routes to subscribers.
> 7. **Application** receives the value, persists it, and triggers analytics or actuation.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> All questions below are tagged with the KTU 2024 Scheme cognitive-level distribution: **L1 – Remember**, **L2 – Understand**, **L3 – Apply**, **L4 – Analyse**. Weightages are split 60 % Part A + 40 % Part B per university ESE convention.

---

### 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]`** *(L1 – Remember, 3 marks)*
Differentiate between **IEEE 802.15.4** and **Bluetooth Low Energy** in terms of (i) frequency band, (ii) maximum data rate, and (iii) typical topology.

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | IEEE 802.15.4 | Bluetooth LE |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Frequency band | 2.4 GHz (also 868/915 MHz) | 2.4 GHz (40 channels, 2 MHz spacing) |
| Maximum data rate | **250 kbps** (O-QPSK) | **2 Mbps** (LE 2M PHY) |
| Typical topology | **Star, tree, mesh** | **Star** (1 master, up to 7 slaves; extended via scatternet) |
| Primary use | Sensor mesh, ZigBee/Thread | Beacons, wearables, audio |

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]`** *(L2 – Understand, 3 marks)*
Explain why **6LoWPAN** is needed when connecting IEEE 802.15.4-based smart objects to an IPv6 Internet.

**Model Answer:**
IEEE 802.15.4 frames carry a maximum of **127 bytes** at the MAC layer, but a full IPv6 header alone is **40 bytes** and typical link-layer overhead reduces the available payload to **≤ 81 bytes**. Furthermore, IPv6 assumes a 1280-byte MTU, making fragmentation mandatory. 6LoWPAN is the **adaptation layer** that:
1. Compresses the 40-byte IPv6 header to as little as **2 bytes** using IPHC.
2. Performs fragmentation and reassembly across 802.15.4 frames.
3. Routes IPv6 packets over multi-hop mesh networks.
Without 6LoWPAN, smart objects could not natively participate in the IPv6 Internet.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module 2 Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Pitfall Callout):**
> When answering "compare IoT technologies" questions, students frequently:
> 1. ❌ List parameters in **prose paragraphs** — KTU examiners award only **1–2 marks** for such unstructured answers. **Use a comparison TABLE** to secure the remaining 5–6 marks.
> 2. ❌ Confuse **LoRa** (the physical-layer modulation by Semtech) with **LoRaWAN** (the MAC-layer protocol by the LoRa Alliance). The two are *distinct*; mark loss is 1 mark per confusion.
> 3. ❌ Omit **frequency bands** — these are almost always asked in the ESE. Memorise the **868 MHz (Europe/India)**, **915 MHz (US)**, **2.4 GHz (global ISM)** allocations.
> 4. ❌ Forget to draw a **protocol-stack diagram** in questions about 6LoWPAN. A neat diagram is worth **3 marks minimum**.
> 5. ❌ Write "ZigBee is faster than LoRa" without justifying using the **Shannon–Hartley theorem** or specifying which PHY layer is being compared.

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Adapted]`**

**A (a)** *(7 marks, L3 – Apply)*
With a neat diagram, describe the **IoT protocol stack** used to connect a smart object (sensor) to a cloud application. Highlight the role of the **6LoWPAN adaptation layer** in enabling IPv6 over IEEE 802.15.4.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Draw the protocol stack diagram.**

Refer to the diagram in **Section 4.1** of these notes. The smart object stack comprises:

- **Application Layer**: CoAP, MQTT-SN, LwM2M.
- **Transport Layer**: UDP (mandatory, as TCP is too heavy for constrained nodes).
- **Network Layer**: IPv6 (128-bit addressing for global reachability).
- **Adaptation Layer**: 6LoWPAN (header compression + fragmentation).
- **Data Link Layer**: IEEE 802.15.4 MAC (CSMA/CA, 16-bit short or 64-bit extended address).
- **Physical Layer**: 2.4 GHz O-QPSK radio (16 channels, 5 MHz spacing).

The gateway then translates to the **standard Internet stack** (TCP/TLS/HTTP).

**Step 2 — Explain the 6LoWPAN adaptation layer (3 sub-functions).**

1. **Header Compression (LOWPAN_IPHC)**: Replaces the 40-byte IPv6 header with a 2-byte dispatch + compressed fields. Address fields are derived from the encapsulating 802.15.4 MAC address. Traffic Class, Flow Label, Next Header are elided when inferable. Compression ratio ≈ **92.5 %**.

2. **Fragmentation and Reassembly (LOWPAN_FRAG1 / FRAGN)**: Splits oversized IPv6 datagrams into multiple 802.15.4 frames. Each fragment carries a 5-byte header with the **datagram tag** (16 bits), **datagram offset** (8 bits), and the original datagram size.

3. **Mesh Routing (LOWPAN_HC1 / IPHC + RPL)**: Forwards 6LoWPAN packets over multi-hop mesh using RPL (Routing Protocol for Low-Power and Lossy Networks) as specified in **RFC 6550**.

**Step 3 — Why 6LoWPAN is essential.**

- 802.15.4 frame size = 127 bytes, but after MAC + security overhead the IP payload is **≤ 81 bytes**; an uncompressed IPv6 packet (≥ 40 bytes) plus 60-byte UDP payload (100 bytes) **does not fit**.
- IPv6 requires **1280-byte MTU**; hence fragmentation is mandatory.
- 6LoWPAN performs the **bridge** between IEEE 802.15.4 PHY/MAC and the IPv6 Internet, making smart objects *IP-native*.

**(Marks: 1 for stack diagram, 3 for 6LoWPAN sub-functions, 2 for justification, 1 for conclusion.)**

---

**A (b)** *(7 marks, L4 – Analyse)*
A Kerala-based agricultural IoT deployment must monitor soil moisture across **50 hectares** with sensors located **2 km apart** from a gateway, transmitting **20 bytes** of payload every **30 minutes** on a **2 400 mAh** coin cell. The available radio options are: (i) **ZigBee**, (ii) **LoRaWAN**, (iii) **Wi-Fi**. Justify the **best technology** with quantitative reasoning.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Range requirement.** 2 km line-of-sight through vegetation.

| Technology | Realistic Range | Verdict |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| ZigBee | 10 – 100 m | ❌ Insufficient |
| Wi-Fi 802.11n | 50 – 200 m outdoor | ❌ Insufficient |
| LoRaWAN SF10 | **5 – 8 km** | ✅ Sufficient |

**Step 2 — Power analysis.**

- **ZigBee** end-device in sleep ≈ 0.2 mA; TX burst ≈ 30 mA for 4 ms. Average ≈ 0.4 mA.
- **LoRaWAN Class A** average current ≈ 0.5 mA (10-byte hourly uplink), but with 20-byte payload every 30 min, slightly higher ≈ **0.7 mA**.
- **Wi-Fi** association + TX/RX current ≈ 200 mA; impossible on 2 400 mAh coin cell for multi-year life.

**Step 3 — Battery lifetime (using Formula 4).**

$$T_{years} = \frac{2400 \text{ mAh}}{0.7 \text{ mA} \times 8760} \approx 0.39 \text{ years (continuous, no duty cycling)}$$

However, with deep-sleep + Class A duty cycle, the effective $I_{avg}$ drops to **~30 µA**, giving:

$$T_{years} = \frac{2400}{0.03 \times 8760} = 9.13 \text{ years}$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion (Verdict).**

**LoRaWAN** is the optimal technology:
- 2 km range ✓ (via SF7–SF10).
- Multi-year battery life ✓ (Class A duty cycle).
- Star-of-stars topology suits single-gateway, multi-sensor deployment ✓.
- Wi-Fi/ZigBee fail on range and/or power.

**(Marks: 1 for table, 2 for power calculation, 2 for range analysis, 2 for verdict.)**

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted]`** *(Alternative Choice)*

**B (a)** *(7 marks, L3 – Apply)*
Compare **NB-IoT** and **LoRaWAN** across **seven** engineering parameters. Mention two distinct use-cases for each.

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | NB-IoT (3GPP Cat-NB1/NB2) | LoRaWAN (Class A) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Spectrum** | Licensed LTE in-band, guard-band, or standalone | Unlicensed ISM (868 / 915 MHz) |
| **Range (MCL)** | **164 dB** Max Coupling Loss | **157 dB** |
| **Data Rate** | ~26 kbps DL / 62 kbps UL (NB1); up to **~200 kbps** (NB2) | **0.3 – 50 kbps** depending on SF |
| **Latency** | 1.6 – 10 s | Variable, **2 – 5 s** typical |
| **Mobility / Handover** | Limited (no handover in NB1) | None (stationary) |
| **Power** | ~5 mW idle; PSM and eDRX supported | < 1.5 mAh/day (Class A) |
| **Security** | LTE-grade (mutual auth, AS/NAS) | AES-128 (AppSKey + NwkSKey) |
| **OPEX** | High (cellular subscription) | Very low (free 868 MHz band) |
| **Deployment** | Requires LTE tower upgrade | Self-install gateway |

**Use-cases for NB-IoT:**
1. **Smart electricity meters** in urban apartments (deep indoor coverage + handover-free).
2. **Connected gas meters** in basement utility rooms (164 dB MCL penetrates concrete).

**Use-cases for LoRaWAN:**
1. **Agricultural soil sensors** in rural Wayanad plantations (long range, low OPEX).
2. **Waste-bin level sensors** across Kochi Municipal Corporation (10 km range, small payload).

**(Marks: 1 per parameter row × 7 = 7, plus 0 each for use-cases — they were asked.)**

---

**B (b)** *(7 marks, L4 – Analyse)*
Explain the **architecture and role of an IoT gateway** in connecting heterogeneous smart objects to the Internet. How does it differ from a conventional IP router?

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definition.** An IoT gateway is a **protocol-translating intermediary** that bridges low-power, lossy networks (LLNs) of smart objects to conventional IP networks. It performs seven key functions:

1. **Protocol Translation**: Decapsulates 802.15.4 / LoRa / BLE frames, rebuilds them as IPv6/UDP/TCP packets, and vice-versa.
2. **Address Mapping**: Maps IEEE 802.15.4 64-bit EUI-64 addresses to globally-routable IPv6 addresses (often via SLAAC or DHCPv6).
3. **Header Compression / Decompression**: Undoes 6LoWPAN compression (and may re-compress for in-LLN traffic).
4. **Network Management**: Implements RPL root, DODAG construction, and route management.
5. **Security Boundary**: Acts as a firewall; terminates DTLS sessions from constrained devices and re-establishes TLS to the cloud.
6. **Data Buffering / Aggregation**: Buffers uplinks during WAN outage; aggregates sensor data to reduce cloud bandwidth.
7. **Edge Analytics**: Optionally runs local ML inference (e.g., anomaly detection) to reduce cloud round-trips.

**Step 2 — Diagram of Gateway Position.**

Refer to the **end-to-end architecture** in **Section 4.3** of these notes.

**Step 3 — Differences from a Conventional IP Router.**

| Aspect | Conventional IP Router | IoT Gateway |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Protocols | IP ↔ IP | **Non-IP ↔ IP** (e.g., 802.15.4 ↔ Ethernet) |
| Power | Mains-powered | Often PoE or solar |
| Intelligence | Forwarding only | Translation + edge compute + security |
| Throughput | Gbps | 10 – 100 kbps |
| Routing | OSPF/BGP | RPL (LLN-specific) |
| Stack | Layers 1 – 3 | Layers 1 – **7** |

**Conclusion.** The IoT gateway is a **heterogeneous bridge** with far greater functional richness than a router. It is *the* mandatory component for connecting smart objects running non-IP protocols to the IP Internet.

**(Marks: 2 for 7 functions, 2 for diagram, 2 for comparison table, 1 for conclusion.)**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🔑 Top-Priority Memorisation List for KTU 2024 Module 2 — "Connecting Smart Objects":**

- ☐ **IEEE 802.15.4** operates in **2.4 GHz (16 channels, 5 MHz spacing)**, **868 MHz (1 channel)**, **915 MHz (10 channels)**, with **O-QPSK modulation** and a maximum **PHY rate of 250 kbps**. Maximum MAC frame = **127 bytes**.
- ☐ **6LoWPAN** compresses the **40-byte IPv6 header** to as little as **2 bytes** (≈ 92.5 % compression) and performs fragmentation.
- ☐ **ZigBee 3.0** uses **AES-128-CCM** and supports **up to 65 000 nodes** in a mesh.
- ☐ **Bluetooth LE 5.0** offers **2 Mbps** PHY, **8 × advertising** improvement, **4× range** over BLE 4.2.
- ☐ **LoRaWAN** uses **Chirp Spread Spectrum (CSS)** with **SF7 – SF12**; higher SF = longer range, lower data rate, longer Time-on-Air. **Class A** (lowest power) → Class B (ping slots) → Class C (continuous RX).
- ☐ **NB-IoT** has a **Max Coupling Loss of 164 dB**, supports **PSM (Power Saving Mode)** and **eDRX (extended Discontinuous Reception)** for multi-year battery life.
- ☐ **LTE-M (Cat-M1)** supports **handover and VoLTE**, peak rate **~1 Mbps**, **MCL = 156 dB**.
- ☐ **Shannon–Hartley theorem** dictates the rate–power–bandwidth trade-off: $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$.
- ☐ **Friis free-space path loss** at 868 MHz: $PL_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(d) + 91.45$ (isotropic antennas, 1 m reference).
- ☐ **LoRaWAN Star-of-stars** topology: end-devices → gateways → **Network Server** → **Application Server**.
- ☐ **6LoWPAN** enables three key functions: **header compression**, **fragmentation & reassembly**, and **mesh forwarding**.
- ☐ **RPL (RFC 6550)** is the routing protocol for LLNs, building a **DODAG** rooted at the gateway.
- ☐ **Gateways** perform **protocol translation, address mapping, security termination, edge analytics, and data buffering** — far more than a conventional router.
- ☐ **Constrained-node classes (RFC 7228)**: Class 0 (< 10 KB RAM, no IP), Class 1 (~10 KB RAM, IP via 6LoWPAN), Class 2 (~50 KB RAM, full IP stack).
- ☐ **Frequency bands to remember (India-relevant)**: **868 MHz** (LoRa, SigFox), **2.4 GHz** (Wi-Fi, BLE, ZigBee, Thread), **Sub-1 GHz** (Z-Wave 865.2 MHz in India, 908.42 MHz in US), **5 GHz** (Wi-Fi 5/6).
- ☐ **Application-layer protocols**: **CoAP** (Constrained Application Protocol, RFC 7252, UDP-based, 4-byte header), **MQTT-SN** (MQTT for Sensor Networks), **LwM2M** (Lightweight M2M, used with NB-IoT/LTE-M).
- ☐ **Security triad**: **AES-128-CCM** (link layer), **DTLS** (transport), **TLS 1.3** (gateway-to-cloud).
- ☐ **Key Indian standards body**: **TEC (Telecommunication Engineering Centre)** and **DoT** regulate IoT spectrum in India; **BIS** governs device certification.
- ☐ **Kerala-specific deployment context**: NB-IoT pilots by KSEB, LoRaWAN experiments by IIT Palakkad and CUSAT, smart-village pilots in Kannur.
- ☐ **Common 14-mark template**: (i) *Protocol stack diagram* (3 marks) + (ii) *Comparative table* (5 marks) + (iii) *Numerical example using Friis/Shannon* (4 marks) + (iv) *Conclusion* (2 marks).
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Communications Criteria
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT752 - INTRODUCTION TO INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Communications Criteria

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Communications Criteria (in IoT context):** The set of quantitative and qualitative performance, environmental, and operational parameters that must be jointly evaluated to select an appropriate communication technology, protocol stack, and network topology for a given Internet of Things (IoT) deployment. These criteria act as the *engineering design constraints* bridging the application layer requirements (e.g., smart agriculture, industrial automation, healthcare) to the physical/MAC layer technology choices (e.g., BLE, Zigbee, LoRa, NB-IoT, 5G).

The universally accepted primary criteria in IoT communication design are:

1. **Range** (Transmission distance)
2. **Data Rate / Bandwidth**
3. **Power Consumption** (Energy efficiency)
4. **Security & Privacy**
5. **Reliability / Quality of Service (QoS)**
6. **Latency / Determinism**
7. **Scalability**
8. **Mobility**
9. **Cost** (Device + Infrastructure)
10. **Interoperability & Standardization**
11. **Topology** (Star, Mesh, Tree, Hybrid)
12. **Spectrum & Regulatory Compliance**

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Courier Service Analogy"

Imagine you need to deliver a **package** (a *data packet*) across a city for an IoT smart-parking sensor:

| Courier Option (IoT Tech) | Range | Speed (Data Rate) | Fuel (Power) | Lockbox (Security) | Cost |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Walking Courier (**BLE / Zigbee**) | 10–100 m | Low | Zero fuel | Basic lock | Cheap |
| Bicycle Courier (**Wi-Fi / Bluetooth Mesh**) | 100 m | High | Pedal-power | Tamper-evident | Medium |
| Delivery Truck (**LoRa / Sigfox**) | 5–15 km | Very low | Diesel-efficient | GPS-tracked | Moderate |
| Express Air Freight (**5G / LTE-M**) | 100s of km | Ultra-high | Jet fuel | Insured vault | Expensive |

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** A smart farmer monitoring soil moisture does **not** need an "air freight" — the package is tiny, the trip is short, and the battery of the sensor must last 10 years. Choosing a high-speed, high-power technology would be engineering malpractice. **Communication Criteria = the "design brief" that filters the right technology from thousands of available options.**

### 1.3 Why These Criteria Are Non-Negotiable in KTU 2024 Scheme

- IoT is **heterogeneous** — a single deployment may contain devices needing mm-level range (NFC payments) and km-level range (rural crop monitoring).
- The **trade-off triangle** of *Range – Data Rate – Power* is the most heavily weighted concept in Module 2.
- KTU 2024 Scheme outcome **CO2 (Analyze communication technologies for IoT applications)** is directly mapped to this topic.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Detailed Breakdown of Each Criterion

#### 2.1.1 Range
- **Definition:** Maximum distance over which the receiver can successfully decode a transmitted signal for a given minimum acceptable Bit Error Rate (BER), typically **BER ≤ 10⁻³** for IoT.
- **Categories (as per KTU syllabus):**
  - **Short Range (SR):** ≤ 100 m — BLE, Zigbee, NFC, RFID
