  - **Medium Range (MR):** 100 m – 1 km — Wi-Fi, Bluetooth Mesh, Thread
  - **Long Range (LR) / LPWAN:** 1 – 50 km — LoRa, Sigfox, NB-IoT, LTE-M
- **Why it matters:** A smart city traffic sensor on a highway lamp-post cannot use NFC; it needs ≥ 1 km line-of-sight.
- **Theoretical driver:** Governed by the **Friis Transmission Equation** (see Section 3) and **path loss exponent (n)**.

#### 2.1.2 Data Rate / Bandwidth
- **Definition:** Number of bits successfully delivered per second (bps, kbps, Mbps).
- **Typical IoT requirements:**
  - Environmental monitoring (temperature, humidity): **< 1 kbps**
  - Wearable health: **10 – 100 kbps**
  - Video surveillance IoT camera: **1 – 10 Mbps**
- **Theoretical ceiling:** **Shannon-Hartley Channel Capacity** — sets the absolute upper bound.

#### 2.1.3 Power Consumption
- **Definition:** Energy (in Joules) consumed per transmitted bit, often expressed as **Energy per bit (E_b)** in nano-joules.
- **Key metric:** Battery lifetime target — many IoT nodes are specified for **5–10 years on a single coin cell**.
- **Driver:** Modulation scheme, duty cycling, MAC protocol (e.g., TDMA vs. CSMA/CA).
- **Critical link:** Power Consumption **↔** Data Rate **↔** Range — the classic *trade-off triangle*.

#### 2.1.4 Security
- **Pillars:** Confidentiality, Integrity, Authentication, Non-repudiation, Freshness.
- **Mechanisms:** AES-128/256, ECC, TLS/DTLS, device attestation.
- **Cost of security:** Cryptographic operations **increase latency and power**; this is why lightweight crypto (e.g., PRESENT, ASCON) is studied for IoT.

#### 2.1.5 Reliability / QoS
- **Definition:** Probability of successful packet delivery (PDR) under stated conditions.
- **Metric:** **Packet Delivery Ratio (PDR)** = (Packets Received / Packets Sent) × 100%.
- **Drivers:** Channel coding (FEC), ARQ/HARQ, multi-path fading mitigation.

#### 2.1.6 Latency
- **Definition:** End-to-end delay from packet generation to successful reception.
- **Criticality:**
  - Industrial control: **< 1 ms** (Ultra-Reliable Low-Latency Communication — URLLC in 5G)
  - Smart agriculture: **seconds acceptable**

#### 2.1.7 Scalability
- **Definition:** Maximum number of end-devices a gateway/core network can manage.
- **Example:** LoRaWAN supports **millions of devices per network server** through hierarchical addressing.

#### 2.1.8 Mobility
- **Definition:** Support for nodes moving at non-zero velocity while maintaining link.
- **Doppler shift** must be tolerated; relevant for **vehicle IoT (V2X)** and **wearables**.

#### 2.1.9 Cost
- **Components:** Bill of Materials (BOM), licensing (3GPP vs. proprietary), maintenance, spectrum fees.
- **Reality:** A LoRa module ≈ **\$3–5**; a 5G modem ≈ **\$30–60**. Cost often **outranks performance** in mass-market IoT.

#### 2.1.10 Interoperability & Standardization
- Bodies: **IEEE 802.15.4** (Zigbee, Thread), **IETF** (CoAP, 6LoWPAN, RPL), **3GPP** (NB-IoT, LTE-M, 5G), **ITU-T** (Y.2060 IoT reference model).

#### 2.1.11 Topology
- **Star** (simple, low-latency, limited range) — used in BLE beacons.
- **Mesh** (multi-hop, high reliability, more power) — used in Zigbee, Thread.
- **Tree / Hierarchical** (scalable) — used in RPL-based IoT.

#### 2.1.12 Spectrum & Regulatory Compliance
- **ISM bands:** 433 MHz, 868 MHz (Europe), 915 MHz (US), 2.4 GHz (global).
- **Duty cycle regulations** (e.g., European 868 MHz: 1 % duty cycle to avoid mandatory listen-before-talk).

### 2.2 The Trade-off Triangle (Most KTU-Repeated Concept)

> [!NOTE]
> **Range – Data Rate – Power Consumption** form an **inverse-coupled triangle** in nearly every IoT communication system. Improving any one generally degrades at least one of the others (a manifestation of the **resource trilemma**).

- **High Data Rate + Long Range ⇒ High Power** (e.g., 5G)
- **Long Range + Low Power ⇒ Very Low Data Rate** (e.g., LoRaWAN — 0.3–50 kbps)
- **High Data Rate + Low Power ⇒ Short Range** (e.g., Wi-Fi at 2.4 GHz)

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | LaTeX Form | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Friis Transmission Equation | $P_r = P_t \cdot G_t \cdot G_r \cdot \left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2$ | $P_r, P_t$ in W; $G_t, G_r$ dimensionless; $\lambda$ in m; $d$ in m |
| 2 | Free-Space Path Loss (dB) | $FSPL(dB) = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.44$ (d, f in km, MHz) | dB |
| 3 | Log-Distance Path Loss | $PL(d) = PL(d_0) + 10n\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{d}{d_0}\right) + X_\sigma$ | dB; $n$ = path loss exponent; $X_\sigma$ = shadow fading (Gaussian, dB) |
| 4 | Link Budget | $P_{rx}(dBm) = P_{tx} + G_t + G_r - L_{total}$ | dBm |
| 5 | SNR | $SNR = \dfrac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}}$ | dB when $10\log_{10}$ applied |
| 6 | Thermal Noise Floor | $N = kTB$ | $k$ = 1.38 × 10⁻²³ J/K; $T$ in K; $B$ in Hz |
| 7 | Shannon-Hartley Capacity | $C = B \cdot \log_{2}\!\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right)$ | bits/s; $B$ in Hz |
| 8 | Spectral Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{C}{B} = \log_{2}\!\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right)$ | bits/s/Hz |
| 9 | Energy per Bit | $E_b = \dfrac{P_{tx} \cdot T_{bit}}{N_0}$ | Joules |
| 10 | Duty Cycle | $DC = \dfrac{T_{on}}{T_{on} + T_{off}} \times 100\%$ | % |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Exam Tip:** Always state the **unit of $P_r$** explicitly. If you plug $P_t$ in dBm, the result must also be in dBm. A common mark-deduction mistake is mixing linear (Watt) and logarithmic (dBm) quantities.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Friis Transmission Equation (KTU Hot Topic)

The Friis equation is the **fundamental link-budget tool** for every IoT radio engineer. We will derive it from first principles.

**Starting Point:** A transmitting isotropic antenna radiates power uniformly in all directions over a sphere of radius $d$. Power flux density at the receiver:

$$S = \frac{P_t}{4\pi d^2} \quad \text{(W/m}^2\text{)}$$

**Step 1 — Apply transmit antenna gain:** Replace the isotropic radiator with a directional antenna of gain $G_t$. The effective flux density becomes:

$$S = \frac{P_t \, G_t}{4\pi d^2}$$

**Step 2 — Compute power captured by the receiving antenna:** The receiving antenna has an *effective aperture* $A_e$, related to its gain $G_r$ and wavelength $\lambda$ by:

$$A_e = \frac{G_r \, \lambda^2}{4\pi}$$

**Step 3 — Multiply flux density by effective aperture:**

$$P_r = S \cdot A_e = \frac{P_t \, G_t}{4\pi d^2} \cdot \frac{G_r \, \lambda^2}{4\pi}$$

**Step 4 — Simplify:**

$$\boxed{\,P_r = P_t \, G_t \, G_r \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2\,}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to logarithmic (dB) form for engineering convenience:**

$$P_r (dBm) = P_t (dBm) + G_t (dBi) + G_r (dBi) - 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)$$

The term $20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)$ is the **Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL)**.

**Step 6 — Express FSPL in km / MHz (the form you must memorize for KTU):**

Using $\lambda = c/f$ and converting units, with $d$ in km and $f$ in MHz:

$$FSPL (dB) = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}[d_{(km)}] + 20\log_{10}[f_{(MHz)}]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize the constant 32.44.** It comes from $20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{4\pi \cdot 10^3}{3 \times 10^8 / 10^6}\right)$. KTU often gives you a numeric problem with this exact constant.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (14-Mark Style)

**Problem:** An IoT soil-moisture sensor transmits at **+14 dBm** on **868 MHz** (European ISM band). The transmit antenna gain is **2 dBi**, the receiver gateway antenna gain is **5 dBi**, and the sensor is placed **5 km** away in line-of-sight rural terrain. Receiver sensitivity is **−110 dBm**. Will the link close? Compute the link margin.

**Step 1 — Compute FSPL:**

$$FSPL = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(5) + 20\log_{10}(868)$$

$$20\log_{10}(5) = 13.98 \text{ dB}$$

$$20\log_{10}(868) = 20 \times 2.9385 = 58.77 \text{ dB}$$

$$FSPL = 32.44 + 13.98 + 58.77 = 105.19 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Link Budget Equation:**

$$P_{rx} = P_{tx} + G_t + G_r - FSPL$$

$$P_{rx} = 14 + 2 + 5 - 105.19 = -84.19 \text{ dBm}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Link Margin:**

$$\text{Margin} = P_{rx} - P_{sensitivity} = -84.19 - (-110) = 25.81 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 4 — Verdict:**

$$\boxed{\,P_{rx} = -84.19 \text{ dBm} > -110 \text{ dBm (sensitivity)} \Rightarrow \text{Link CLOSES with 25.81 dB margin.}\,}$$

A positive margin ≥ 10 dB is the **industry rule of thumb** for a robust IoT link (accounting for fading, rain, foliage, interference).

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Criteria vs. Technology (KTU Quick Reference)

| Criterion | BLE 5.0 | Zigbee | Wi-Fi 6 | LoRaWAN | NB-IoT | 5G mMTC |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Range** | ≤ 100 m | ≤ 100 m | ≤ 200 m | ≤ 15 km | ≤ 35 km | ≤ 100 km (cell) |
| **Data Rate** | 2 Mbps | 250 kbps | 1.2 Gbps | 0.3 – 50 kbps | 26 – 159 kbps | 100 Mbps peak |
| **Power** | Very Low | Low | High | Very Low | Low | High |
| **Latency** | 7.5 ms | 15 ms | < 10 ms | 1 – 5 s | 1 – 10 s | < 1 ms (URLLC) |
| **Topology** | Star / Mesh | Mesh | Star | Star-of-stars | Star (cellular) | Star (cellular) |
| **Spectrum** | 2.4 GHz | 2.4 GHz | 2.4 / 5 GHz | 433/868/915 MHz | Licensed LTE | Licensed 5G NR |
| **Cost/Node** | < \$2 | < \$3 | \$5–10 | \$3–5 | \$8–12 | \$30–60 |
| **Scalability** | Thousands | Thousands | Dozens | Millions | Millions | Millions/km² |
| **Best For** | Wearables | Home automation | Video IoT | Smart Agri | Smart Metering | Industry 4.0 |

### 3.4 Shannon Capacity — Why It Is the *Theoretical Ceiling*

For a Gaussian channel, **no coding scheme can transmit reliably at a rate above $C$ bits/s**, where:

$$C = B \log_{2}\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right)$$

- Doubling the bandwidth $B$ doubles capacity (linearly).
- Doubling the SNR **does not** double capacity (logarithmically).
- For IoT, $B$ is often **tiny** (e.g., 125 kHz in LoRa), forcing a high SNR to compensate.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world IoT systems operate well below Shannon's limit.** LoRaWAN uses Chirp Spread Spectrum (CSS) to trade spectral efficiency for extreme range and robustness, operating at $\eta \approx 0.3$ bits/s/Hz versus a Shannon limit that may reach 5+ bits/s/Hz.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Communications Criteria Decision Framework (Mermaid Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[IoT Application Requirement] --> B[Define Service Profile]
    B --> C{What is the dominant constraint?}
    C -- "Long battery life" --> D[Power Constrained Design]
    C -- "Real-time control" --> E[Latency Constrained Design]
    C -- "High resolution video" --> F[Data Rate Constrained Design]
    C -- "City wide coverage" --> G[Range Constrained Design]
    D --> H[Select LPWAN: LoRa or Sigfox or NB-IoT]
    E --> I[Select 5G URLLC or Wi-Fi 6]
    F --> J[Select Wi-Fi 6 or 5G eMBB]
    G --> K[Select LoRaWAN or LTE-M]
    H --> L[Validate Link Budget using Friis Equation]
    I --> L
    J --> L
    K --> L
    L --> M{Link Margin greater than 10 dB}
    M -- "Yes" --> N[Deploy Network]
    M -- "No" --> O[Add Repeater or increase Ptx or change antenna]
    O --> L
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an IoT Communication Link

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TX[Transmitter IoT Node]
        A1[Application Layer] --> A2[Network Layer: 6LoWPAN or CoAP]
        A2 --> A3[Datalink MAC: TDMA or CSMA or ALOHA]
        A3 --> A4[Physical Layer: Modulation OOK or FSK or LoRa CSS or OFDM]
        A4 --> A5[RF Front End: PA plus Filter plus Antenna Gt]
    end
    subgraph CHANNEL[Wireless Channel]
        A5 --> B1[Path Loss: Lp]
        B1 --> B2[Shadow Fading: Xsigma]
        B2 --> B3[Multipath Fading: Rayleigh or Rician]
        B3 --> B4[Noise plus Interference: N plus I]
    end
    subgraph RX[Receiver Gateway]
        B4 --> C1[RF Front End: LNA plus Antenna Gr]
        C1 --> C2[PHY: Demodulation plus Decoding]
        C2 --> C3[MAC: ARQ or FEC]
        C3 --> C4[Network: Routing to Cloud]
    end
```

### 4.3 The Trade-off Triangle (Mermaid Conceptual Map)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Range] ---|inverse| DR[Data Rate]
    DR ---|inverse| P[Power Consumption]
    P ---|inverse| R
    R --> R1[LoRa achieves 15 km by lowering DR and lowering P]
    DR --> DR1[Wi-Fi 6 achieves 1 Gbps by shortening R and raising P]
    P --> P1[BLE achieves 1 year battery by lowering DR and shortening R]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the triangle:** The three nodes are bound by inverse relationships. To move a design toward a corner (e.g., longer range), the other two corners retreat. This is the **single most exam-relevant visual** in Module 2 of KTU IoT.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *List and briefly explain any three communication criteria that influence the selection of an IoT communication technology.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Range (1 mark):** The maximum distance over which reliable communication is possible. IoT applications may need short (BLE: 10 m), medium (Wi-Fi: 100 m) or long range (LoRa: 15 km) depending on deployment scale.

2. **Power Consumption (1 mark):** The energy required to transmit, receive, and process a packet. Critical for battery-operated nodes; technologies like LoRa and BLE are designed for ultra-low power (target lifetime 5–10 years on a single cell).

3. **Data Rate (1 mark):** The throughput in bits per second. Environmental sensors need < 1 kbps, while video IoT cameras need several Mbps. The choice depends on the type of data being transported.

*(Examiner's note: Any three of the criteria listed in Section 2.1 with at least one sentence of context earns full marks.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** *Differentiate between short-range, medium-range, and long-range IoT communication technologies with one example each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Category | Range | Example Protocol | Typical Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Short Range** | < 100 m | Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE) / NFC | Wearables, contactless payment |
| **Medium Range** | 100 m – 1 km | Wi-Fi / Zigbee / Thread | Home automation, indoor IoT |
| **Long Range (LPWAN)** | 1 – 50 km | LoRaWAN / Sigfox / NB-IoT | Smart agriculture, smart city, metering |

*(1 mark for the table/classification, 1 mark for example protocol, 1 mark for appropriate use case.)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Communications Criteria:**
> 1. **Never** list criteria without a one-line *engineering justification* of why that criterion matters. A bare list earns ≤ 50 % of allocated marks.
> 2. **Always** state units explicitly (dBm vs. dBW vs. Watt). A 14-mark numerical without units loses 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Link budget problems** demand *all five steps*: FSPL computation → total path loss → received power → comparison with sensitivity → link margin comment. Skipping the final step is the #1 cause of mark loss.
> 4. **Trade-off triangle** must be explained, not merely drawn. Examiners want the words *"inverse coupling"* or *"resource trilemma"*.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **seven primary communication criteria** used to evaluate IoT communication technologies. Explain how each criterion influences the choice of technology for a **smart agriculture deployment**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An IoT gateway antenna is mounted 20 m above ground. A soil sensor transmits at **+20 dBm** on **433 MHz** with antenna gain **0 dBi**. The gateway antenna has gain **6 dBi**, and the sensor is located **3 km** away. The receiver sensitivity is **−120 dBm**. **Assume free-space propagation.** Determine the received power and the link margin. Comment on the suitability of the link. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Seven Communication Criteria for Smart Agriculture (7 marks = 1 mark each criterion + application mapping):**

| # | Criterion | Definition | Why it matters for Smart Agriculture |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Range** | Maximum reliable communication distance | Fields span 1–10 km; need LPWAN-class range |
| 2 | **Data Rate** | Throughput (bps) | Soil moisture / pH updates are < 1 kbps; no need for high bandwidth |
| 3 | **Power Consumption** | Energy per bit | Sensors run on coin cells or solar; must last 5+ years |
| 4 | **Cost** | BOM + infrastructure | Hundreds of sensors per hectare; must be < \$5/node |
| 5 | **Security** | Confidentiality, integrity | Field telemetry is not highly sensitive, but integrity against tampering is needed |
| 6 | **Scalability** | Devices per gateway | A farm may have 10 000 sensors; LoRaWAN supports this |
| 7 | **Topology** | Network structure | Star-of-stars is sufficient; mesh adds cost and complexity |

**Recommended technology: LoRaWAN** because it simultaneously satisfies long range, ultra-low power, low cost, and high scalability — at the cost of low data rate (which is acceptable for soil sensing).

**(b) Numerical Link Budget Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Identify quantities:**
$P_{tx} = +20$ dBm, $G_t = 0$ dBi, $G_r = 6$ dBi, $f = 433$ MHz, $d = 3$ km, $P_{sens} = -120$ dBm.

**Step 2 — Compute FSPL using $FSPL = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f)$:**

$$20\log_{10}(3) = 9.54 \text{ dB}$$

$$20\log_{10}(433) = 20 \times 2.6365 = 52.73 \text{ dB}$$

$$FSPL = 32.44 + 9.54 + 52.73 = 94.71 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 3 — Received power:**

$$P_{rx} = P_{tx} + G_t + G_r - FSPL = 20 + 0 + 6 - 94.71 = -68.71 \text{ dBm}$$

**Step 4 — Link margin:**

$$\text{Margin} = P_{rx} - P_{sens} = -68.71 - (-120) = 51.29 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 5 — Verdict:**

The received power of **−68.71 dBm** is comfortably above the receiver sensitivity of **−120 dBm**, yielding an exceptionally healthy **link margin of 51.29 dB** (≫ 10 dB rule of thumb). The link is **highly suitable for free-space deployment**. In real agricultural environments, an additional **10–20 dB fade margin** must be reserved for vegetation, multipath, and seasonal weather effects, leaving a usable margin of ≥ 30 dB — still acceptable.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** With the help of a **neat block diagram**, describe the **IoT communication link architecture** from the application layer of the transmitter to the application layer of the receiver. Identify the layer at which **each communication criterion is enforced**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Apply the **Shannon-Hartley theorem** to compute the maximum theoretical capacity of an IoT channel with bandwidth **125 kHz** and SNR of **20 dB**. If the technology used operates at **25 % spectral efficiency** of the Shannon limit, compute the **achievable data rate**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Block Diagram & Criterion Mapping (7 marks):**

**Block diagram (5 layers × 2 sides = 10 blocks):**
- **Application Layer** (data origin / destination)
- **Transport / Network Layer** (CoAP / UDP / 6LoWPAN / RPL)
- **Data Link / MAC Layer** (TDMA, CSMA/CA, ARQ)
- **Physical Layer** (Modulation: OOK, FSK, LoRa CSS, OFDM; Coding: FEC)
- **RF Front End** (PA, LNA, Antenna, Filter)

**Criterion-to-layer mapping (1 mark):**

| Criterion | Layer Enforced |
|---|---|
| Range, Power | PHY + RF Front End |
| Data Rate, Spectral Efficiency | PHY (modulation order) |
| Latency | MAC + Network |
| Reliability | MAC (ARQ) + PHY (FEC) |
| Security | Network + Application (DTLS, AES) |
| Scalability | Network (addressing, routing) |
| Interoperability | Application (data model: JSON, CBOR) |

**(b) Shannon Capacity Calculation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Convert SNR from dB to linear scale:**

$$SNR_{lin} = 10^{20/10} = 10^2 = 100$$

**Step 2 — Apply Shannon-Hartley theorem:**

$$C_{max} = B \cdot \log_2(1 + SNR) = 125{,}000 \cdot \log_2(1 + 100) = 125{,}000 \cdot \log_2(101)$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\log_2(101)$:**

$$\log_2(101) = \frac{\log_{10}(101)}{\log_{10}(2)} = \frac{2.0043}{0.3010} \approx 6.658 \text{ bits/s/Hz}$$

**Step 4 — Shannon capacity:**

$$C_{max} = 125{,}000 \times 6.658 = 832{,}250 \text{ bps} \approx 832.25 \text{ kbps}$$

**Step 5 — Achievable data rate at 25 % efficiency:**

$$C_{achievable} = 0.25 \times 832.25 = 208.06 \text{ kbps}$$

**Step 6 — Verdict:**

$$\boxed{\,C_{max} = 832.25 \text{ kbps}; \quad C_{achievable} = 208.06 \text{ kbps}\,}$$

This is consistent with **LoRaWAN's highest data rate setting** (SF7, 125 kHz BW → 11 kbps) being far below the Shannon ceiling because LoRa trades spectral efficiency for *range and robustness* — a deliberate engineering decision aligned with the trade-off triangle.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ⭐ **The 12 communication criteria:** Range, Data Rate, Power, Security, Reliability, Latency, Scalability, Mobility, Cost, Interoperability, Topology, Spectrum/Regulation.
- ⭐ **Trade-off triangle:** Range ↔ Data Rate ↔ Power are inversely coupled. Memorize one example per vertex (LoRa, Wi-Fi, BLE).
- ⭐ **Friis equation:** $P_r = P_t G_t G_r (\lambda / 4\pi d)^2$. Memorize the **logarithmic form** with **32.44 constant** for km/MHz units.
- ⭐ **Shannon capacity:** $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$. Linear in $B$, logarithmic in SNR.
- ⭐ **Link budget procedure:** $P_{rx} = P_{tx} + G_t + G_r - FSPL - L_{other}$; compare with $P_{sensitivity}$; compute margin.
- ⭐ **ISM bands to remember:** 433 MHz (EU/Asia), 868 MHz (Europe), 915 MHz (USA), 2.4 GHz (global).
- ⭐ **Path loss exponent $n$:** Free space = 2; urban cellular ≈ 3–4; obstructed indoor ≈ 4–6.
- ⭐ **QoS metric:** Packet Delivery Ratio (PDR).
- ⭐ **Energy metric:** Joules per bit, driven by duty cycle.
- ⭐ **Exam mantra:** Always state units; always end numericals with a one-line *engineering comment* on link suitability.
- ⭐ **Common pitfall:** Confusing **dBm** (decibels relative to 1 mW) with **dBi** (antenna gain relative to isotropic radiator). The Friis equation is dimensionally consistent **only** if you remember $0 \text{ dBm} = 1 \text{ mW}$ and $0 \text{ dBi} = \text{linear gain} = 1$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: IoT Access Technologies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT752 - INTRODUCTION TO INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** IoT Access Technologies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📡 IoT Access Technologies — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are IoT Access Technologies?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> *IoT Access Technologies* refer to the **Physical (PHY) and Medium Access Control (MAC) layer protocols** that govern how constrained IoT devices (sensors, actuators, motes) transmit and receive data over short or long ranges using shared wireless or wired media. They form the **"first mile"** connectivity bridge between resource-limited edge devices and the IP/Internet backhaul in the IoT protocol stack.

In simple words, these are the **"languages" and "manners"** that tiny IoT devices use to "talk" to each other and to gateways, using the radio spectrum as their "air medium."

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Postal System" of IoT

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: Think of IoT as a giant city of tiny citizens (sensors) trying to send letters (data packets).**

| Real Postal System Element | IoT Access Technology Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Post Office rules (who speaks first?) | **MAC Layer Protocol** (e.g., CSMA/CA, TDMA) |
| Type of letter/postcard (size, format) | **PHY Layer Modulation** (e.g., O-QPSK, BPSK) |
| Delivery truck / bicycle / airplane | **Wireless Medium** (Sub-GHz, 2.4 GHz, licensed LTE bands) |
| City limits (how far the truck can go) | **Range** (10 m, 100 m, 10 km, etc.) |
| Battery of the delivery vehicle | **Power Consumption** (mW, µW) |
| Cost of sending one letter | **Per-Device Cost & Deployment Density** |

Just like cities choose between local couriers (BLE), national trucks (LoRa), or international air freight (NB-IoT/LTE-M) depending on **distance, urgency, and cost**, IoT deployments choose their access technology based on **range, data rate, power budget, and scalability**.

---

## 1.3 The IoT Protocol Stack — Where Access Tech Sits

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Access technologies occupy the **bottom two layers (PHY + MAC)** of the OSI model, which is the foundation of the entire IoT communication pipeline.

$$
\boxed{\text{Application Layer (CoAP/MQTT/HTTP)} \rightarrow \text{Network (IPv6/6LoWPAN/RPL)} \rightarrow \text{Adaptation (6LoWPAN)} \rightarrow \text{MAC (802.15.4)} \rightarrow \text{PHY (Radio)}}
$$

---

## 1.4 Classification of IoT Access Technologies

IoT access technologies are broadly classified based on **coverage range** and **network topology**:

### A. Based on Coverage Range

| Category | Range | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **PAN (Personal Area Network)** | 1 – 10 m | BLE, NFC, Zigbee, Thread |
| **LAN (Local Area Network)** | 10 – 100 m | Wi-Fi (802.11ah - HaLow) |
| **MAN/WAN (Wide Area Network)** | 1 – 50 km | **LoRaWAN**, **Sigfox**, **NB-IoT**, **LTE-M** |

### B. Based on Spectrum Licensing

> [!NOTE]
> **Licensed Spectrum** = You pay telecom regulators (e.g., 4G/5G/NB-IoT).
> **Unlicensed ISM Bands** = Free but shared (e.g., 868 MHz, 915 MHz, 2.4 GHz used by Wi-Fi/BLE/Zigbee).

### C. Based on Topology

- **Star Topology** — All nodes talk directly to a central gateway (e.g., LoRaWAN, BLE, Sigfox).
- **Mesh Topology** — Nodes relay each other's packets (e.g., Zigbee, Thread).
- **Hybrid** — Star-of-stars (e.g., Wi-Fi HaLow with relay).

---

## 1.5 Design Goals of IoT Access Technologies (KPIs)

Every IoT access technology is engineered to optimize a subset of these **Key Performance Indicators (KPIs)**:

$$
\text{KPI Set} = \{\text{Range (R)}, \text{Data Rate (DR)}, \text{Power (P)}, \text{Node Density (N)}, \text{Latency (L)}, \text{Cost (C)}\}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Trade-off Golden Rule:** There is **NO single access technology** that simultaneously maximizes all KPIs. *Longer range* typically requires *lower data rates* and *higher transmit power* — this is the fundamental **energy-bandwidth-range trade-off** in IoT PHY design.

---

## 1.6 Standard Metrics to Remember (Bold Highlights)

- **ISM Band**: Industrial, Scientific, Medical radio bands (e.g., **2.4 GHz**, **868 MHz** EU, **915 MHz** US).
- **Bit Error Rate (BER)**: Target for IoT = **10⁻³ to 10⁻⁶** depending on modulation.
- **Link Budget**: Total tolerable path loss in dB (LoRaWAN = **157 dB**, LTE-M = **146 dB**).
- **Spreading Factor (SF)**: LoRaWAN-specific parameter (**SF7 to SF12**), trade-off between range and bitrate.
- **Duty Cycle Limit**: **1 %** in EU868 sub-band for duty-cycled access (e.g., Sigfox, LoRaWAN).
- **Channel Bandwidth (BW)**: 125 kHz, 250 kHz, 500 kHz for LoRa; 180 kHz for NB-IoT.

---

## 1.7 Why Access Technology Choice Matters in KTU Context

In the KTU 2024 syllabus, Module 2 specifically tests your ability to **select the right access technology for a given IoT use case**. A smart agriculture deployment in Wayanad (hilly terrain, 5 km farms) needs **LoRaWAN**, whereas a wearable heart-rate monitor needs **BLE**. Picking wrongly = project failure.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 🔬 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Major IoT Access Technologies — Operational Deep-Dive

### 2.1.1 IEEE 802.15.4 — The Foundation Protocol

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IEEE 802.15.4** is the **base standard** for most low-rate wireless personal area networks (LR-WPANs) used in IoT. It defines the **PHY and MAC layers only**, leaving upper layers to Zigbee, Thread, 6LoWPAN, etc.

**PHY Layer Highlights:**
- Operates in three ISM bands: **868 MHz (Europe)**, **915 MHz (US)**, **2.4 GHz (Global)**.
- Modulation schemes: **BPSK** (868/915), **O-QPSK** (2.4 GHz).
- Maximum raw data rate: **250 kbps** at 2.4 GHz.
- Channel access: **16 channels in 2.4 GHz** (each 2 MHz wide, 5 MHz spacing).

**MAC Layer Highlights:**
- Uses **CSMA/CA (Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Avoidance)**.
- Supports **beacon-enabled** (slotted, superframe structure with CAP + CFP) and **non-beacon** modes.
- **GTS (Guaranteed Time Slots)** for low-latency applications.
- Maximum frame size: **127 bytes** (very small → motivates 6LoWPAN fragmentation).

**Superframe Structure:**

$$
\underbrace{\text{Beacon}}_{\text{Synchronization}} + \underbrace{\text{CAP}}_{\text{Contention Access Period}} + \underbrace{\text{CFP}}_{\text{Contention Free Period}}
$$

### 2.1.2 LoRaWAN (Long Range Wide Area Network)

LoRaWAN is a **two-layer stack**: **LoRa (PHY, by Semtech)** + **LoRaWAN (MAC, by LoRa Alliance)**.

**LoRa PHY:**
- **Chirp Spread Spectrum (CSS)** modulation.
- Operates in sub-GHz ISM: **433 MHz, 868 MHz, 915 MHz**.
- Channel BW: **125 / 250 / 500 kHz**.
- Spreading Factor (SF): **SF7 to SF12** (higher SF → longer range, lower bitrate).

**LoRaWAN MAC:**
- **Pure ALOHA** (no carrier sensing) at MAC layer — simple but collision-prone.
- Three device classes:
  - **Class A** (bi-directional, lowest power, downlink only after uplink).
  - **Class B** (adds scheduled downlink via beacons).
  - **Class C** (continuously listening, mains-powered only).
- Adaptive Data Rate (ADR) mechanism managed by network server.

**Key Bit-Rate Equation (KTU High-Yield):**

$$
R_b = SF \times \frac{BW}{2^SF} \times \text{(coding rate factor)}
$$

Simplified for CR = 4/5:

$$
R_b \approx SF \times \frac{BW}{2^SF} \times \frac{4}{5}
$$

| SF | BW = 125 kHz | BW = 250 kHz |
|---|---|---|
| **SF7** | **5.47 kbps** | 10.94 kbps |
| **SF9** | **1.37 kbps** | 2.74 kbps |
| **SF12** | **0.293 kbps** | 0.586 kbps |

### 2.1.3 Sigfox

- **Ultra-Narrowband (UNB)** PHY with **100 Hz bandwidth** channels.
- Operates in **868 MHz / 902 MHz** ISM.
- **Data rate: 100 bps (uplink), 600 bps (downlink)** — extremely low.
- Each uplink message limited to **12 bytes** (max 140 messages/day).
- Uses **DBPSK modulation** in uplink; **GFSK** in downlink.
- **Star topology**; relies on **random frequency + time** access (no carrier sense).

### 2.1.4 NB-IoT (Narrowband-IoT)

- **3GPP Release 13 (2016)** standardized cellular LPWAN.
- Operates **in-band** within LTE carrier, or in **guard-band**, or **stand-alone** in refarmed GSM spectrum.
- Channel BW: **180 kHz** (one LTE PRB).
- Data rate: **~26 kbps (DL)**, **~62 kbps (UL)** for multi-tone.
- **Modulation**: QPSK (uplink), 16-QAM optional (downlink).
- Supports **massive MIMO** compatibility and **extensive coverage** up to **164 dB MCL** (Maximum Coupling Loss).
- **Half-duplex FDD** operation.

### 2.1.5 LTE-M (LTE-Machine Type Communication)

- 3GPP Release 13 alongside NB-IoT.
- Bandwidth: **1.4 MHz** (6 PRBs).
- Data rate: up to **1 Mbps (DL/UL)**.
- Lower latency than NB-IoT, supports **VoLTE (voice over LTE)**.
- Coverage: **156 dB MCL**.

### 2.1.6 Zigbee (IEEE 802.15.4-based)

- Built on top of IEEE 802.15.4 PHY/MAC.
- Mesh networking, up to **65,000 nodes** per network.
- Data rate: **250 kbps** at 2.4 GHz.
- Used heavily in **smart home** (Philips Hue, IKEA TRÅDFRI).

### 2.1.7 Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE) — IEEE 802.15.1

- **Frequency Hopping Spread Spectrum (FHSS)** across **40 channels in 2.4 GHz**.
- **GFSK modulation**, 1 Mbps symbol rate (2 Mbps in BLE 5.0).
- **Advertising + Connection** modes.
- Range: **10 – 100 m**; ultra-low power (months to years on coin cell).
- BLE 5.x: **LE Coded PHY** (S=2, S=8) for extended range, **LE 2M PHY** for higher speed.

### 2.1.8 Wi-Fi HaLow (IEEE 802.11ah)

- Sub-1 GHz Wi-Fi, ranges up to **1 km**.
- Channels: **1/2/4/8/16 MHz** bandwidths.
- Data rate: **150 kbps to 86.7 Mbps** (adaptive MCS).
- Designed for **sensor networks and IoT backhaul**.

### 2.1.9 RFID & NFC (Brief)

- **RFID**: Passive (no battery) backscatter; **LF (125 kHz), HF (13.56 MHz), UHF (860–960 MHz)**.
- **NFC**: HF RFID subset; **13.56 MHz**; range **< 10 cm**; peer-to-peer mode.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table verbatim for KTU ESE — these equations appear in 14-mark questions.**

| Parameter | Formula / Definition | Typical Value / Unit | Used In |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL)** | $\text{FSPL} = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.44$ | dB (d in km, f in MHz) | All RF |
| **Link Budget** | $LB = P_{tx} + G_{tx} - L_{tx} + G_{rx} - L_{rx} - S_{rx}$ | dB | LoRaWAN, NB-IoT |
| **LoRa Bit Rate** | $R_b = SF \cdot \dfrac{BW}{2^{SF}} \cdot \dfrac{4}{5}$ | bps | LoRa |
| **Time on Air (LoRa)** | $T_{air} = T_{preamble} + T_{payload}$ | ms | LoRa |
| **Payload Symbol Count** | $n_{payload} = 8 + \max\left(\left\lceil \dfrac{8PL - 4SF + 28 + 16CRC - 20H}{4(SF-2DE)} \right\rceil (CR+4), 0\right)$ | symbols | LoRa |
| **SNR Threshold (LoRa SF)** | $\text{SNR}_{min} = -7.5 - 2.5(SF - 7)$ | dB | LoRa |
| **Symbol Period** | $T_s = \dfrac{2^{SF}}{BW}$ | seconds | LoRa |
| **Capacity (Shannon)** | $C = BW \cdot \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$ | bps | All |
| **Receive Sensitivity** | $S = -174 + 10\log_{10}(BW) + \text{NF} + \text{SNR}_{min}$ | dBm | All |
| **Max Coupling Loss (MCL)** | $\text{MCL} = P_{tx} - S_{rx}$ | dB | NB-IoT, LTE-M |
| **NPRACH Preamble Repetitions** | $N_{rep} \in \{1, 2, 4, 8, 16, 32, 64, 128\}$ | integer | NB-IoT coverage classes |
| **Duty Cycle Limit** | $\text{DC} = \dfrac{T_{on}}{T_{on}+T_{off}} \leq 1\%$ | dimensionless | EU868 |

---

## 2.3 The "Why" Behind Each Design Choice

### Why Sub-GHz for LPWAN?
- **Lower propagation loss** at lower frequencies (free-space path loss ∝ f²).
- **Better wall/foliage penetration** (important for smart agriculture, basements).
- **Less crowded** than 2.4 GHz (which has Wi-Fi, BLE, Zigbee, microwaves).

### Why CSS (Chirp Spread Spectrum) in LoRa?
- **Robust to Doppler** and multipath fading.
- **Processing gain** trades bandwidth for SNR.
- **Orthogonal SFs** allow simultaneous transmission on same channel (high capacity).

### Why Ultra-Narrowband in Sigfox?
- **Energy per bit concentrated** in tiny BW → high sensitivity receiver, long range.
- But very **low throughput** (~100 bps).

### Why Half-Duplex FDD in NB-IoT?
- **Simpler, cheaper** transceivers.
- Sufficient for **asymmetric** IoT traffic (mostly uplink, rare downlink ACKs).

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smart Agriculture (Kerala use case)**: LoRaWAN soil moisture sensors in Wayanad rubber plantations — 5–10 km range, 5+ year battery life.
- **Smart Metering (KSEB Kerala)**: NB-IoT electricity meters — underground basements, deep indoor coverage.
- **Wearable Health**: BLE heart-rate strap — 1 week battery, paired to smartphone.
- **Industrial IoT**: Zigbee mesh in factory floor — 250 nodes, deterministic latency.
- **Asset Tracking**: RFID + GPS hybrid — inventory in warehouses.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL)

The Friis transmission equation in linear form is:

$$
P_r = P_t \, G_t \, G_r \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2
$$

Converting to dB (10·log₁₀ of both sides):

$$
P_r\,[\text{dBm}] = P_t\,[\text{dBm}] + G_t\,[\text{dBi}] + G_r\,[\text{dBi}] + 20\log_{10}\left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)
$$

The path loss term alone is:

$$
\text{FSPL} = -20\log_{10}\left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right) = 20\log_{10}\left(\frac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)
$$

Substitute $\lambda = c/f$, where $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s and $f$ in Hz:

$$
\text{FSPL} = 20\log_{10}\left(\frac{4\pi d \, f}{c}\right)
$$

Insert $d$ in km and $f$ in MHz (using $c = 3 \times 10^5$ km/s = $3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s):

$$
\text{FSPL}\,[\text{dB}] = 20\log_{10}(d\,[\text{km}]) + 20\log_{10}(f\,[\text{MHz}]) + 32.44
$$

> **Step-by-step logic:** Friis equation → isolate path loss term → convert linear to dB → substitute wavelength by $c/f$ → apply unit conversions to obtain the engineer-friendly form with the constant **32.44**.

**Numerical example (KTU-style):** $f = 868$ MHz, $d = 5$ km.

$$
\text{FSPL} = 20\log_{10}(5) + 20\log_{10}(868) + 32.44
$$

$$
= 13.98 + 58.77 + 32.44 = 105.19 \text{ dB}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — LoRa Bit Rate

In LoRa, each symbol encodes $SF$ bits, and the symbol period is $T_s = 2^{SF}/BW$.

Therefore, the **raw bit rate** is:

$$
R_b = \frac{SF}{T_s} = \frac{SF \cdot BW}{2^{SF}}
$$

The coding rate (CR) introduces redundancy. With CR = $4/5$ (default), only $4/5$ of the symbol bits carry data:

$$
R_b = SF \cdot \frac{BW}{2^{SF}} \cdot \frac{4}{5}
$$

> **Step logic:** $R_b = \text{bits per symbol} / \text{symbol duration}$ → multiply by effective data fraction from CR.

**Numerical check (SF7, BW = 125 kHz):**

$$
R_b = 7 \times \frac{125000}{2^7} \times 0.8 = 7 \times 976.56 \times 0.8 = 5468.75 \text{ bps}
$$

Matches the datasheet's 5.47 kbps. ✓

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — LoRa Receiver Sensitivity

The thermal noise floor in bandwidth $BW$:

$$
N_0\,[\text{dBm}] = -174 + 10\log_{10}(BW\,[\text{Hz}])
$$

Add noise figure $NF$ and minimum SNR required for demodulation:

$$
S_{rx}\,[\text{dBm}] = -174 + 10\log_{10}(BW) + NF + \text{SNR}_{min}
$$

For LoRa, $\text{SNR}_{min}$ is empirically:

$$
\text{SNR}_{min} = -7.5 - 2.5(SF - 7) \text{ dB}
$$

**Example (SF12, BW = 125 kHz, NF = 6 dB):**

$$
\text{SNR}_{min} = -7.5 - 2.5(12-7) = -20 \text{ dB}
$$

$$
S_{rx} = -174 + 10\log_{10}(125000) + 6 + (-20) = -174 + 50.97 + 6 - 20 = -137.03 \text{ dBm}
$$

> This ultra-low sensitivity is what gives LoRa its legendary range.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Link Budget and Maximum Range

Link budget equation:

$$
LB\,[\text{dB}] = P_{tx} + G_{tx} + G_{rx} - L_{total} - S_{rx}
$$

At maximum range, the path loss equals the link budget:

$$
L_{max} = P_{tx} + G_{tx} + G_{rx} - S_{rx}
$$

Using FSPL (assuming line-of-sight):

$$
20\log_{10}\left(\frac{d_{max}}{1 \text{ km}}\right) = L_{max} - 20\log_{10}(f\,[\text{MHz}]) - 32.44
$$

**Example:** LoRaWAN EU868, $P_{tx} = 14$ dBm, $G_{tx} = G_{rx} = 2$ dBi, $S_{rx} = -137$ dBm, $f = 868$ MHz.

$$
L_{max} = 14 + 2 + 2 - (-137) = 155 \text{ dB}
$$

$$
20\log_{10}(d_{max}) = 155 - 58.77 - 32.44 = 63.79
$$

$$
d_{max} = 10^{63.79/20} = 10^{3.19} = 1545 \text{ km (free-space)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> In practice, range is limited to **5–15 km** by terrain, foliage, building clutter, and antenna heights. Free-space calculation gives a **theoretical upper bound**.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Shannon Channel Capacity (Used for Wi-Fi HaLow & NB-IoT)

$$
C\,[\text{bps}] = BW \cdot \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})
$$

**Example:** Wi-Fi HaLow channel BW = 8 MHz, SNR = 30 dB = 1000 (linear).

$$
C = 8 \times 10^6 \times \log_2(1001) = 8 \times 10^6 \times 9.97 = 79.7 \text{ Mbps}
$$

---

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix (For KTU 14-Mark Answers)

| Technology | Standard | Frequency | Data Rate | Range | Battery Life | Topology | Cost/Device |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **BLE 5** | IEEE 802.15.1 | 2.4 GHz | 2 Mbps | 100 m | Years (coin cell) | Star / Mesh | Low (~₹80) |
| **Zigbee 3.0** | IEEE 802.15.4 | 2.4 GHz | 250 kbps | 100 m | Years | Mesh | Low (~₹120) |
| **Z-Wave** | Proprietary | 908/868 MHz | 100 kbps | 30 m | Years | Mesh | Medium |
| **Thread** | IEEE 802.15.4 + 6LoWPAN | 2.4 GHz | 250 kbps | 100 m | Years | Mesh (border router) | Low |
| **Wi-Fi HaLow** | IEEE 802.11ah | 900 MHz | 150 kbps – 86 Mbps | 1 km | Days–Months | Star | Medium |
| **LoRaWAN** | LoRa Alliance | 433/868/915 MHz | 0.3 – 50 kbps | 5 – 15 km | 10+ years | Star-of-stars | Low (~₹300) |
| **Sigfox** | Sigfox | 868/902 MHz | 100 bps | 10 – 50 km | 10+ years | Star | Low + subscription |
| **NB-IoT** | 3GPP Rel-13 | Licensed (700–900 MHz) | 26/62 kbps | 15+ km | 10+ years | Star (cellular) | Medium + subscription |
| **LTE-M** | 3GPP Rel-13 | Licensed LTE | 1 Mbps | 10 km | Months | Star (cellular) | Medium + subscription |
| **RFID Passive** | ISO/IEC 14443/15693 | 13.56 MHz / UHF | Few kbps | < 10 m | Battery-free | Tag-reader | Very low (~₹5) |
| **NFC** | ISO/IEC 18092 | 13.56 MHz | 106/212/424 kbps | < 10 cm | Battery-free | Peer-to-peer | Very low |

---

## 3.7 Analytical Use-Case Decision Tree

```
IoT Application
│
├── Range < 10 m, no battery? ──► RFID / NFC
│
├── Range 10–100 m, low power, smartphone interaction? ──► BLE
│
├── Range 10–100 m, smart home mesh? ──► Zigbee / Thread / Z-Wave
│
├── Range 10–100 m, IP-native, high data? ──► Wi-Fi HaLow
│
├── Range 1–15 km, low data (kbps), battery decade-long? ──► LoRaWAN / Sigfox
│
└── Cellular coverage, deep indoor, mobility? ──► NB-IoT / LTE-M
```

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 🗺️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — IoT Access Technology Stack

```mermaid
graph TD
    appLayer[Application Layer CoAP MQTT HTTP]
    netLayer[Network Layer IPv6 RPL 6LoWPAN]
    adaptLayer[Adaptation Layer 6LoWPAN]
    macLayer[MAC Layer CSMA TDMA ALOHA]
    phyLayer[PHY Layer Modulation Spreading]

    panTech[PAN BLE Zigbee NFC RFID]
    lanTech[LAN WiFi HaLow]
    lpwanTech[LPWAN LoRaWAN Sigfox]
    cellTech[Cellular NB-IoT LTE-M]

    appLayer --> netLayer
    netLayer --> adaptLayer
    adaptLayer --> macLayer
    macLayer --> phyLayer

    phyLayer --> panTech
    phyLayer --> lanTech
    phyLayer --> lpwanTech
    phyLayer --> cellTech

    macLayer --> panTech
    macLayer --> lanTech
    macLayer --> lpwanTech
    macLayer --> cellTech
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — LoRaWAN Network Architecture (Star-of-Stars)

```mermaid
graph TD
    sensorA[Soil Moisture Sensor SF7]
    sensorB[Temperature Sensor SF9]
    sensorC[Water Level Sensor SF12]
    gw1[LoRaWAN Gateway 1]
    gw2[LoRaWAN Gateway 2]
    nwkServer[Network Server]
    appServer[Application Server]
    cloud[Cloud Dashboard]

    sensorA -->|868 MHz uplink| gw1
    sensorB -->|868 MHz uplink| gw1
    sensorC -->|868 MHz uplink| gw2
    sensorB -->|868 MHz uplink| gw2
    gw1 -->|IP backhaul| nwkServer
    gw2 -->|IP backhaul| nwkServer
    nwkServer -->|JSON via MQTT HTTP| appServer
    appServer --> cloud
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — NB-IoT Protocol Stack (3GPP)

```mermaid
graph TD
    appN[User Application CoAP LWM2M]
    scep[Lightweight M2M Bootstrap]
    nas[NAS Non-Access Stratum]
    rrc[Radio Resource Control RRC]
    pdcp[Packet Data Convergence Protocol]
    rlc[Radio Link Control]
    macN[MAC Layer NB-IoT]
    phyN[PHY NB-IoT 180 kHz]

    appN --> scep
    scep --> nas
    nas --> rrc
    rrc --> pdcp
    pdcp --> rlc
    rlc --> macN
    macN --> phyN

    phyN -->|In-band Guard-band Standalone| lteCarrier[LTE Carrier Spectrum]
```

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Access Technology Selection Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    start[Start Selection]
    q1{Need Cellular Mobility?}
    q2{Range more than 1 km?}
    q3{Phone or Gateway Nearby?}
    q4{Need Mesh Networking?}
    q5{Need Ultra-Low Power Coin Cell?}

    start --> q1
    q1 -->|Yes| cellChoice[NB-IoT or LTE-M]
    q1 -->|No| q2
    q2 -->|Yes| lpwanChoice[LoRaWAN or Sigfox]
    q2 -->|No| q4
    q4 -->|Yes| meshChoice[Zigbee Thread Z-Wave]
    q4 -->|No| q3
    q3 -->|Yes| bleChoice[BLE]
    q3 -->|No| q5
    q5 -->|Yes| bleChoice
    q5 -->|No| wifiChoice[Wi-Fi or Wi-Fi HaLow]
```

---

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram — LoRaWAN MAC Class A Timing (Frame Exchange)

```mermaid
graph LR
    dev[End Device Uplink Window 1]
    rx1[Receive Window 1 RX1 Opens 1 sec after uplink]
    rx2[Receive Window 2 RX2 Opens 2 sec after uplink on different channel]
    dev -->|TX| rx1
    rx1 -->|If no downlink received| rx2
    rx2 -->|If no downlink received| idle[End Device Sleep until next uplink]
```

> [!NOTE]
> This Class A timing is the **most energy-efficient** LoRaWAN mode. The end device only opens two short receive windows after each uplink, then sleeps. This is why LoRaWAN end nodes can last **10+ years on a 2400 mAh battery**.

---

## 4.6 Mermaid Diagram — NB-IoT Coverage Enhancement Levels

```mermaid
graph TD
    cel0[CE Level 0 Normal 164 dB MCL 1 rep]
    cel1[CE Level 1 Robust 154 dB MCL 1 to 2 rep]
    cel2[CE Level 2 Extreme 144 dB MCL 1 to 16 rep]
    cel3[CE Level 3 Deep Indoor 134 dB MCL 1 to 32 rep]
    cel4[CE Level 4 Basement 124 dB MCL 1 to 64 rep]
    cel5[CE Level 5 Underground 114 dB MCL 128 to 512 rep]

    cel0 --> cel1 --> cel2 --> cel3 --> cel4 --> cel5
```

> **Reading:** Higher CE levels repeat the same transmission many times to "burn through" obstructions — trading latency and energy for coverage.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Don't confuse LoRa (PHY) with LoRaWAN (MAC)** — KTU loves this trick question.
> 2. **Always mention BOTH range AND data rate** when asked to "describe" a technology. Single-metric answers lose 4–6 marks.
> 3. **Do not write "Bluetooth" generically** — specify *Bluetooth Classic* or *BLE*. They are **not** the same protocol.
> 4. **Zigbee ≠ IEEE 802.15.4** — Zigbee is a *full stack*, 802.15.4 is *only PHY + MAC*.
> 5. **In numerical link budget problems**, units (dBm vs mW, km vs m) carry marks. Show conversion steps.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between LoRa and LoRaWAN. State any two applications of LoRaWAN.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | LoRa | LoRaWAN |
|---|---|---|
| **OSI Layer** | Physical (PHY) layer only | MAC + Network layer |
| **Owner** | Semtech (proprietary chips) | LoRa Alliance (open spec) |
| **Function** | Modulation (Chirp Spread Spectrum) | MAC protocol, device classes, security |
| **Data Rate** | 0.3 – 50 kbps (SF/BW dependent) | Same as LoRa, but adds MAC overhead |

**Two applications of LoRaWAN:**
1. **Smart Agriculture** — Soil moisture and weather monitoring across 5–10 km farms.
2. **Smart Water/Gas Metering** — Daily consumption uploads from basement utility rooms to KSEB/cloud servers.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**List any three differences between BLE and Zigbee. Mention the typical use case of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | BLE (Bluetooth Low Energy) | Zigbee |
|---|---|---|
| **Standard** | IEEE 802.15.1 (modified) | IEEE 802.15.4-based |
| **Topology** | Star (point-to-point) | Mesh (up to 65k nodes) |
| **Data Rate** | 1 – 2 Mbps | 250 kbps |
| **Power** | Ultra-low (months – 1 yr) | Low (1 – 2 yr typical) |
| **Typical Use** | Wearables, smartphone peripherals | Smart home (lights, sensors) |

**Use case of BLE:** Heart-rate monitor paired with smartphone.
**Use case of Zigbee:** Philips Hue smart lighting mesh in homes.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### 🔹 **Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model Paper]**

**Q. (a)** Explain the architecture of **IEEE 802.15.4** with a neat block diagram of the superframe structure. List the four types of frames supported by 802.15.4. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the **LoRaWAN network architecture** and the three device classes. A LoRaWAN end device transmits at **SF = 10, BW = 125 kHz, CR = 4/5**. Compute the bit rate. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a)**

The IEEE 802.15.4 standard defines the **lowest two layers (PHY + MAC)** of the OSI model for Low-Rate Wireless Personal Area Networks (LR-WPANs).

**Architecture (block diagram of superframe):**

```
| Beacon (BO=SO) | CAP (Contention Access Period) | CFP (Contention Free Period) | Inactive |
|   Synchronize  |  CSMA/CA based access           |   GTS slots                 |  Sleep   |
```

**Superframe key parameters:**
- **Beacon Order (BO)** — defines beacon interval.
- **Superframe Order (SO)** — defines active duration.
- **BO = SO** → no inactive period.
- **BO > SO** → inactive period exists (energy saving).

**Four Frame Types in IEEE 802.15.4:**

1. **Beacon Frame** — Synchronization, sent by coordinator.
2. **Data Frame** — Payload transfer.
3. **ACK Frame** — Acknowledgment (no payload, only frame ID).
4. **MAC Command Frame** — Used for association, dissociation, GTS request, etc.

**PHY Layer Specifications:**
- Three ISM bands: 868 MHz, 915 MHz, 2.4 GHz.
- 27 channels total (1 @ 868, 10 @ 915, 16 @ 2.4 GHz).
- Modulation: BPSK (868/915) and O-QPSK (2.4 GHz).
- Maximum data rate: 250 kbps (at 2.4 GHz).

**MAC Functions:**
- CSMA/CA with random backoff.
- PAN association/disassociation.
- GTS allocation for time-critical traffic.
- Security using AES-128.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b)**

**LoRaWAN Network Architecture:**

A LoRaWAN deployment is a **star-of-stars topology** consisting of:

1. **End Devices (ED)** — Battery-powered sensors/actuators with LoRa radio.
2. **Gateways (GW)** — Forward packets between EDs and Network Server via IP backhaul (typically Ethernet, 4G, Wi-Fi).
3. **Network Server (NS)** — Removes duplicate packets, schedules downlinks, manages ADR.
4. **Application Server (AS)** — Application logic, device management.

**Three LoRaWAN Device Classes:**

| Class | Downlink Window | Power | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **A** | Only after each uplink (RX1, RX2) | **Lowest** | Battery sensors, periodic uplinks |
| **B** | Scheduled slots via gateway beacons | Medium | Battery actuators needing periodic downlinks |
| **C** | Continuously open (mains-powered) | **Highest** | Smart street lighting, mains-powered actuators |

**Bit Rate Computation:**

Given: SF = 10, BW = 125 kHz, CR = 4/5.

Using the LoRa bit-rate formula:

$$
R_b = SF \cdot \frac{BW}{2^{SF}} \cdot CR
$$

$$
R_b = 10 \times \frac{125{,}000}{2^{10}} \times \frac{4}{5}
$$

$$
R_b = 10 \times \frac{125{,}000}{1024} \times 0.8
$$

$$
R_b = 10 \times 122.07 \times 0.8 = 976.56 \text{ bps} \approx 0.977 \text{ kbps}
$$

**Result: ~977 bps** — sufficient for periodic sensor reporting every few minutes.

---

### 🔹 **Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks**

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model Paper]**

**Q. (a)** Compare **NB-IoT and LoRaWAN** in terms of spectrum, range, data rate, battery life, and deployment cost. Mention two real-world use cases of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **BLE 5.0 transmitter** operating at 2 Mbps is used in a wearable. The receiver has NF = 8 dB and required BER = 10⁻³, corresponding to **SNR_min = 12 dB** for GFSK. Compute the **receiver sensitivity** if channel BW = 2 MHz. Then, if the link budget is 100 dB, calculate the **maximum free-space range** at 2.4 GHz. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a)**

| Parameter | NB-IoT | LoRaWAN |
|---|---|---|
| **Spectrum** | **Licensed** (in-band LTE / guard-band) | **Unlicensed ISM** (868/915 MHz) |
| **Standard** | 3GPP Release 13 | LoRa Alliance |
| **Modulation** | QPSK (UL), 16-QAM optional (DL) | Chirp Spread Spectrum (CSS) |
| **Channel BW** | 180 kHz | 125 / 250 / 500 kHz |
| **Data Rate** | 26 kbps (DL) / 62 kbps (UL) | 0.3 – 50 kbps (SF dependent) |
| **Range** | 15+ km (with 164 dB MCL) | 5 – 15 km (urban/rural) |
| **Battery Life** | 10+ years (PSM/eDRX) | 10+ years (Class A) |
| **Deployment Cost** | High (cellular subscription) | Low (own gateway, no SIM) |
| **Mobility Support** | Yes (handovers) | No (fixed EDs) |
| **Latency** | 1.6 – 10 s | Variable (depends on SF) |

**NB-IoT Use Cases:**
1. **Smart Electricity Meters (KSEB)** in basements with deep indoor coverage.
2. **Connected Health Devices** (e.g., continuous glucose monitors) requiring reliable cellular reach.

**LoRaWAN Use Cases:**
1. **Smart Agriculture** in Wayanad rubber plantations.
2. **Smart Waste Management** — fill-level sensors on municipal bins.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b)**

**Step 1 — Receiver Sensitivity Calculation:**

Given: BW = 2 MHz = 2 × 10⁶ Hz, NF = 8 dB, SNR_min = 12 dB.

Using the sensitivity equation:

$$
S_{rx}\,[\text{dBm}] = -174 + 10\log_{10}(BW) + NF + \text{SNR}_{min}
$$

Substitute:

$$
S_{rx} = -174 + 10\log_{10}(2 \times 10^6) + 8 + 12
$$

$$
S_{rx} = -174 + 63.01 + 8 + 12
$$

$$
S_{rx} = -90.99 \text{ dBm} \approx -91 \text{ dBm}
$$

**Result: Receiver Sensitivity = −91 dBm** (very typical for BLE receivers).

**Step 2 — Maximum Free-Space Range:**

Given: Link budget $L_{max} = 100$ dB, $f = 2400$ MHz.

Using the FSPL equation:

$$
L_{max} = 20\log_{10}(d\,[\text{km}]) + 20\log_{10}(f\,[\text{MHz}]) + 32.44
$$

$$
100 = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(2400) + 32.44
$$

$$
100 = 20\log_{10}(d) + 67.6 + 32.44
$$

$$
20\log_{10}(d) = 100 - 100.04 = -0.04
$$

$$
\log_{10}(d) = -0.002
$$

$$
d = 10^{-0.002} = 0.995 \text{ km} \approx 1 \text{ km}
$$

**Result: Maximum free-space range ≈ 1 km**.

> [!NOTE]
> In real indoor environments with walls, multipath, and body shadowing (wearable on wrist), practical range reduces to **20–50 m**, consistent with BLE marketing specifications.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory anchor points for KTU ESE — memorize the starred items.**

- ⭐ **IEEE 802.15.4** = PHY + MAC only; max 250 kbps; CSMA/CA; superframe with CAP + CFP; max packet 127 bytes.
- ⭐ **LoRaWAN** = Star-of-stars; CSS modulation; SF7–SF12; **Class A** = lowest power, **Class C** = highest.
- ⭐ **LoRa bit rate** $R_b = SF \cdot BW / 2^{SF} \cdot CR$ — at **SF7, BW=125 kHz** → **5.47 kbps**; at **SF12, BW=125 kHz** → **0.29 kbps**.
- ⭐ **Sigfox** = UNB 100 Hz BW; 100 bps UL; 12 bytes/msg; 140 msgs/day; star topology.
- ⭐ **NB-IoT** = 3GPP Rel-13; 180 kHz BW; 164 dB MCL; in-band / guard-band / standalone deployment.
- ⭐ **LTE-M** = 1.4 MHz BW; 1 Mbps; supports VoLTE and mobility; 156 dB MCL.
- ⭐ **BLE 5** = 2 Mbps LE 2M PHY; LE Coded PHY with S=2 or S=8 for long range; FHSS 40 channels at 2.4 GHz.
- ⭐ **Zigbee** = 250 kbps; mesh up to 65k nodes; smart home.
- ⭐ **Wi-Fi HaLow (802.11ah)** = sub-1 GHz; up to 1 km; up to 86 Mbps.
- ⭐ **RFID passive** = battery-free backscatter; NFC = < 10 cm, 13.56 MHz.
- ⭐ **FSPL** = $20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.44$ (d in km, f in MHz).
- ⭐ **Sensitivity** = $-174 + 10\log_{10}(BW) + NF + \text{SNR}_{min}$.
- ⭐ **EU868 duty cycle** = **1 %** for license-free sub-GHz.
- ⭐ **Selection rule of thumb**: short range + smartphone → BLE; medium mesh → Zigbee/Thread; long range + battery → LoRaWAN; cellular + deep coverage → NB-IoT.

---

### 🔚 End of Module 2 — IoT Access Technologies Notes
**Best of luck for your KTU ESE preparation! 🌟**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: IoT Network Layer: IP as the IoT Network Layer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT752 - INTRODUCTION TO INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** IoT Network Layer: IP as the IoT Network Layer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Network Layer** is **Layer 3** of the OSI / TCP-IP reference model and is responsible for **end-to-end packet delivery** between a source host and a destination host across one or more packet-switched networks. In the context of the **Internet of Things (IoT)**, the **Internet Protocol (IP)** — standardized by the IETF in **RFC 791 (IPv4, 1981)** and **RFC 8200 (IPv6, 2017)** — functions as the **unifying network-layer protocol** that allows heterogeneous IoT devices (sensors, actuators, embedded boards, edge gateways) to interoperate over the global Internet.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> *"IP is the IoT Network Layer — it provides the logical addressing, routing, fragmentation, and best-effort delivery service that allows billions of constrained devices to be uniquely identified and to exchange datagrams over heterogeneous physical and link-layer IoT substrates."*

Formally, an **IP datagram** is a **connectionless, best-effort, hop-by-hop forwarded** unit of data. Each datagram carries a **header** containing the source and destination logical addresses, and the routers / 6LoWPAN border routers examine these addresses to perform **lookup-based forwarding**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Global Postal System"

Imagine the **Internet as the world's postal service**:

- Every **house** has a unique **postal address** → this is exactly what an **IP address** is to an IoT device (e.g., a temperature sensor in Kochi has a globally unique IPv6 address).
- A **letter** has a *sender address* and a *receiver address* printed on its envelope → this is the **IP header** with `Source IP` and `Destination IP` fields.
- A **letter** may pass through several **sorting offices** before reaching the destination → these sorting offices are the **routers / gateways** that hop-by-hop forward IP datagrams.
- A **large letter** may be split into **multiple smaller envelopes** (e.g., if it exceeds the sorting-office sack size) → this is **IP fragmentation** at routers whose outgoing **MTU (Maximum Transmission Unit)** is smaller than the original packet.

Just as the postal system works irrespective of whether you send the letter from a small village or a metro city, **IP allows an IoT device of any capability class to communicate as long as it speaks the IP "language"** at Layer 3.

### 1.3 Why IP for IoT? — The Four Pillars

| Pillar | Engineering Meaning | IoT Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| **Unique Global Addressing** | Every node gets a globally routable ID | Billions of IoT endpoints can coexist |
| **Interoperability** | Vendor-neutral, open standard | Any device can talk to any other device |
| **Scalable Routing** | Hierarchical, aggregated routing tables | Internet-scale IoT deployments work |
| **Transport-Agnostic** | Sits above *any* link layer (Wi-Fi, LoRa, ZigBee, BLE, 6LoWPAN, Ethernet) | IoT heterogeneity is naturally absorbed |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight Box**
> The KTU syllabus explicitly states that **IP forms the unifying network layer of the IoT stack**, with the *Application* and *Adaptation* layers (e.g., 6LoWPAN) wrapped *around* it to support constrained devices. **Remember**: *IoT ≠ replacement of IP — IoT = IP with adaptation for constrained networks.*

### 1.4 Key Physical & Protocol Constants (Memorize for KTU)

| Constant | Value | Symbol |
|---|---|---|
| **IPv4 Address Length** | **32 bits** (4 bytes) | 4 294 967 296 unique addresses |
| **IPv6 Address Length** | **128 bits** (16 bytes) | $3.4 \times 10^{38}$ unique addresses |
| **IPv4 Header Min / Max Length** | **20 bytes / 60 bytes** | — |
| **IPv6 Fixed Header Length** | **40 bytes** | — |
| **IPv4 Total Length Field Max** | **65 535 bytes** | $2^{16} - 1$ |
| **IPv4 TTL Default (Linux)** | **64** | Decremented per hop |
| **IPv4 Fragment Offset Granularity** | **8 bytes** | $2^3$ |
| **Protocol Number — ICMP** | **1** | Network diagnostics |
| **Protocol Number — TCP** | **6** | Reliable transport |
| **Protocol Number — UDP** | **17** | Lightweight transport (CoAP) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Role of the IP Network Layer in the IoT Architecture

The IoT protocol stack is *not* a clean re-invention — it is **IP-centric**. Layer 3 of every major IoT reference model (e.g., the ITU-T Y.2060 model, the IETF IoT stack, and the KTU-mandated architecture) places **IP** at the heart of communication. The complete KTU-mandated view is:

$$\text{App Layer (CoAP / MQTT / HTTP)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Transport (UDP / TCP)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Network (IPv4 / IPv6)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Adaptation (6LoWPAN)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Link + PHY (IEEE 802.15.4 / LoRa / Wi-Fi)}$$

**Three core jobs of IP in IoT:**

1. **Logical Addressing** — assigning each IoT node a globally unique address so that packets know *who* is *talking* to *whom*.
2. **Routing & Forwarding** — using distributed routing protocols (e.g., **RPL — Routing Protocol for Low-Power and Lossy Networks, RFC 6550**) to build paths across mesh topologies.
3. **Fragmentation & Reassembly** — breaking large datagrams into smaller units that fit the underlying link-layer MTU (critical for IEEE 802.15.4, whose MTU is only **127 bytes**).

### 2.2 Why is IP Uniquely Suited for IoT?

- **Open & Royalty-Free** — no licensing fees (unlike legacy industrial protocols such as Modbus or Profinet).
- **Routing Infrastructure Already Exists** — the global Internet's BGP, OSPF, IS-IS hierarchy is reused.
- **Stateless & Best-Effort** — keeps the protocol simple enough to run on **8-bit MCUs** (with adaptation via 6LoWPAN).
- **End-to-End Principle** — intelligence stays at the edges, which aligns with the IoT edge-computing paradigm.
- **Vendor-Neutral Interworking** — ZigBee IP, Thread, LoRaWAN (with IP backhaul), NB-IoT, LTE-M — *all* converge to IP at the network layer.

### 2.3 IPv4 vs IPv6 for IoT — The Decisive Trade-off

| Property | IPv4 (RFC 791) | IPv6 (RFC 8200) |
|---|---|---|
| Address Width | **32 bits** | **128 bits** |
| Total Addresses | $2^{32} \approx 4.29 \times 10^9$ | $2^{128} \approx 3.4 \times 10^{38}$ |
| Header Size | 20 – 60 bytes (variable) | **40 bytes (fixed)** |
| Fragmentation | Routers & sender | **Sender only** (Path MTU Discovery) |
| Checksum in Header | **Yes** (recomputed every hop) | **Removed** (faster, offloaded to L4) |
| Address Autoconfiguration | **DHCP** required | **SLAAC** (stateless, RFC 4861) |
| Security (IPsec) | Optional, bolt-on | **Mandatory in original spec** |
| IoT Suitability | Poor at scale (NAT workarounds) | **Excellent** (designed for IoT) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Point to Remember**
> The KTU module explicitly highlights **IPv6** as the *de-facto* IoT network-layer protocol of the future because of (a) the address exhaustion problem of IPv4, (b) the elimination of NAT (which breaks true end-to-end IoT), and (c) the existence of **6LoWPAN**, which is *only defined for IPv6*.

### 2.4 The Problem: IP Was *Not* Designed for 8-Bit IoT Devices

The classical IP datagram — designed in 1981 for ARPANET — carries a **minimum header of 20 bytes** and assumes link-layer MTUs of **≥ 576 bytes**. However, a typical IoT constraint profile is:

- **MCU Word Size:** 8 / 16 / 32 bits
- **RAM:** 4 – 32 KB
- **MTU (IEEE 802.15.4):** **127 bytes** (leaving only 81 bytes for upper layers after security)
- **Battery:** Coin cell, months-to-years lifetime

> A **full IPv6 header (40 bytes) + UDP (8 bytes) + CoAP (4 bytes)** = 52 bytes consumed — which is **64 % of the usable payload** of an IEEE 802.15.4 frame. Hence the need for **Adaptation Layer (6LoWPAN)**.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Rule | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $N_{\text{IPv4}} = 2^{32}$ | Total IPv4 addresses ≈ $4.29 \times 10^{9}$ |
| 2 | $N_{\text{IPv6}} = 2^{128}$ | Total IPv6 addresses ≈ $3.4 \times 10^{38}$ |
| 3 | $\text{Header Min}_{\text{v4}} = 20$ bytes | Without options |
| 4 | $\text{Header Max}_{\text{v4}} = 60$ bytes | With 40 bytes of options |
| 5 | $\text{Header}_{\text{v6}} = 40$ bytes | Fixed for all datagrams |
| 6 | $\text{MaxPayload}_{\text{v4}} = 2^{16} - 1 = 65\,535$ bytes | Total Length field limit |
| 7 | $\text{FragOffset}_{\text{units}} = \dfrac{\text{data offset in bytes}}{8}$ | Granularity rule |
| 8 | $\text{EffectivePayload} = \text{MTU}_{\text{link}} - \text{Header}_{\text{IP}} - \text{Header}_{\text{L4}}$ | Realistic upper-layer data |
| 9 | $\text{IEEE 802.15.4 MTU} = 127$ bytes | Drives 6LoWPAN adaptation |
| 10 | $\text{Compressed 6LoWPAN Header} \approx 4$ – 7 bytes | IPHC compression savings |
| 11 | $\text{IPv6 Address} = \text{64 bits prefix} + \text{64 bits IID}$ | Standard subnetting |
| 12 | $\text{IPv6 CIDR} = /64$ per IoT subnet | One subnet per PAN |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smart Agriculture:** LoRaWAN gateways back-haul sensor packets using IPv6 over 6LoWPAN, allowing direct cloud access without proprietary concentrators.
- **Smart Cities:** Streetlight controllers running **Contiki-NG / RIOT-OS** use IPv6 + RPL to form a self-healing mesh.
- **Industrial IoT (IIoT):** OPC-UA over TCP/IP allows seamless SCADA integration with cloud analytics.
- **Healthcare Wearables:** BLE-based devices tunnel IPv6 packets via **6LoBLU** adaptation to allow direct Internet connectivity.

## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations, Field Walk-throughs & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 IPv4 Header — Bit-Level Walk-through

The **IPv4 header** is the most-asked topic in any KTU networking module. The header is **20 bytes (160 bits)** minimum. We will derive the meaning of every field and the byte budget.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Header}_{\text{IPv4}} &= \underbrace{1}_{\text{Ver}} + \underbrace{1}_{\text{IHL}} + \underbrace{1}_{\text{ToS}} + \underbrace{2}_{\text{TotalLen}} + \underbrace{2}_{\text{Ident}} + \underbrace{2}_{\text{Flags+FO}} \\
&\quad + \underbrace{1}_{\text{TTL}} + \underbrace{1}_{\text{Proto}} + \underbrace{2}_{\text{HdrCksum}} + \underbrace{4}_{\text{SrcIP}} + \underbrace{4}_{\text{DstIP}} \\
&= 20 \text{ bytes (no options)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Field-by-field derivation:**

1. **Version (4 bits)** — Always set to `0100` (= 4) for IPv4. Used by routers to parse the header.
2. **IHL — Internet Header Length (4 bits)** — Length of the header in **32-bit words**.
   $$\text{IHL}_{\min} = 5 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 5 \times 4 = 20 \text{ bytes}$$
   $$\text{IHL}_{\max} = 15 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 15 \times 4 = 60 \text{ bytes (max 40 bytes of options)}$$
3. **Type of Service / DSCP + ECN (8 bits)** — Originally for QoS priority; modern usage maps to **DSCP (6 bits)** + **ECN (2 bits)**.
4. **Total Length (16 bits)** — Length of the *entire* datagram in bytes, including header.
   $$\text{Total Length}_{\max} = 2^{16} - 1 = 65\,535 \text{ bytes}$$
5. **Identification (16 bits)** — A unique ID assigned by the sender; **all fragments of one original datagram share the same ID**. This is how the receiver knows which fragments belong together.
6. **Flags (3 bits) — Bit 0: Reserved, Bit 1: DF (Don't Fragment), Bit 2: MF (More Fragments).**
   - **DF = 1** → packet must *not* be fragmented; if path MTU is too small, the router sends back an **ICMP "Fragmentation Needed"** (Type 3, Code 4).
   - **MF = 1** → *more fragments follow*; **MF = 0** → this is the *last* fragment.
7. **Fragment Offset (13 bits)** — Position of this fragment's data relative to the start of the *original* datagram, measured in **8-byte units**.
   $$\text{Offset (bytes)} = \text{Fragment Offset field} \times 8$$
8. **TTL — Time To Live (8 bits)** — Decremented by **1 at every router**. When TTL = 0, the packet is dropped and an **ICMP Time Exceeded (Type 11)** is sent back. Prevents infinite routing loops.
9. **Protocol (8 bits)** — Demultiplexing key: **1 = ICMP, 6 = TCP, 17 = UDP, 58 = ICMPv6, 132 = SCTP**.
10. **Header Checksum (16 bits)** — **One's complement sum** of the 16-bit words of the header. Recomputed at *every* hop (because TTL changes).
11. **Source IP (32 bits)** — Logical address of the sender.
12. **Destination IP (32 bits)** — Logical address of the receiver.
13. **Options (0 – 40 bytes, variable)** — Rarely used; examples: Record Route, Timestamp, Loose Source Routing.

### 3.2 Fragmentation Worked Example (Exam-Style)

> **Question:** A host sends a **4000-byte** datagram over a link with **MTU = 1500 bytes** (typical Ethernet). The IP header is **20 bytes**. How many fragments are produced? Compute the **Fragment Offset** and **MF flag** for each.

**Step 1 — Maximum data per fragment:**
$$\text{Data}_{\max} = \text{MTU} - \text{Header} = 1500 - 20 = 1480 \text{ bytes}$$

**Step 2 — Verify 1480 is divisible by 8** (mandatory alignment rule):
$$1480 \div 8 = 185 \quad \checkmark \text{ (aligned)}$$

**Step 3 — Compute number of fragments:**
$$\text{Fragments} = \left\lceil \dfrac{4000 - 20}{1480} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \dfrac{3980}{1480} \right\rceil = 3$$

**Step 4 — Distribute the 3980 bytes of data into 3 fragments:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Fragment 1} &: \text{Data} = 1480 \text{ bytes}, \quad \text{Offset} = 0, \quad \text{MF} = 1 \\
\text{Fragment 2} &: \text{Data} = 1480 \text{ bytes}, \quad \text{Offset} = \dfrac{1480}{8} = 185, \quad \text{MF} = 1 \\
\text{Fragment 3} &: \text{Data} = 3980 - 2960 = 1020 \text{ bytes}, \quad \text{Offset} = \dfrac{2960}{8} = 370, \quad \text{MF} = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification check:** Last fragment's offset in bytes = $370 \times 8 = 2960$. Adding its data of 1020 bytes gives $2960 + 1020 = 3980$ bytes — matches the original data length. ✔

### 3.3 IPv6 Header — Bit-Level Walk-through

The IPv6 header is **always exactly 40 bytes** and is **drastically simplified** versus IPv4:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Header}_{\text{IPv6}} &= \underbrace{4}_{\text{Ver}} + \underbrace{8}_{\text{TrafficClass}} + \underbrace{20}_{\text{FlowLabel}} + \underbrace{16}_{\text{PayloadLen}} + \underbrace{8}_{\text{NextHdr}} \\
&\quad + \underbrace{8}_{\text{HopLimit}} + \underbrace{128}_{\text{SrcAddr}} + \underbrace{128}_{\text{DstAddr}} \\
&= 40 \text{ bytes (fixed)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Key engineering changes (the "Why" behind every removal):**

1. **No IHL field** — header is fixed, so the parser doesn't need to measure it.
2. **No Header Checksum** — upper/lower-layer checksums (e.g., UDP, TCP, Ethernet CRC) already cover the data; removing the IP checksum saves router CPU and allows faster ASIC-based forwarding.
3. **No Fragmentation fields** — routers no longer fragment; **Path MTU Discovery (PMTUD, RFC 8201)** is used. Sender fragments *before* transmission.
4. **Next Header replaces Protocol** — chains extension headers (`Hop-by-Hop`, `Routing`, `Fragment`, `Destination Options`, `AH`, `ESP`) — supports a clean *extension* model.
5. **Flow Label (20 bits)** — pseudo-circuit identifier for QoS; allows routers to treat all packets of a flow identically (used in voice / video over IoT).

### 3.4 IPv6 Address Notation

A full IPv6 address is **128 bits** written as **eight groups of four hex digits** separated by colons.

**Example (full form):**
$$\text{2001:0db8:85a3:0000:0000:8a2e:0370:7334}$$

**Canonical compression rules:**
- Leading zeros in a group may be dropped: `0db8` → `db8`, `0000` → `0`.
- One run of consecutive zero groups may be replaced by `::`:
  `2001:db8:85a3::8a2e:370:7334`

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The `::` may appear *at most once* per address** — this is a KTU exam favourite.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — IP-Adaptation Layers Used in IoT

| Adaptation Layer | Network Protocol | Underlying Link | Header Compression |
|---|---|---|---|
| **6LoWPAN** (RFC 4944, 6282) | IPv6 | IEEE 802.15.4 | IPHC, NHC |
| **6Lo** (RFC 7668) | IPv6 | IEEE 802.11 (Wi-Fi) | IPHC |
| **6LoBLU** | IPv6 | Bluetooth LE | GATT fragmentation |
| **6Lo-G3** | IPv6 | ITU-T G.3 PLC (Power Line) | IPHC |
| **SCHC** (RFC 8724) | IPv6 / IPv4 | LoRaWAN, Sigfox | Static-context compression |
| **6TiSCH** | IPv6 | IEEE 802.15.4 TSCH | 6LoWPAN + scheduled MAC |

### 3.6 RPL — The Routing Protocol for IoT (Routed IP)

RPL (**RFC 6550**) builds a **DODAG (Destination-Oriented Directed Acyclic Graph)** rooted at a border router. The **Objective Function (OF)** is typically **OF0 (RFC 6552)** or **MRHOF (RFC 6719)** minimizing *ETX* (Expected Transmission Count) or *hop count*. RPL is *the* routing protocol used by Thread, ZigBee IP, and most academic IoT testbeds.
