`Content-Type: application/json` — tells the server the body format. - `Authorization: Bearer ` — OAuth 2.0 token (industry standard for IoT). #### Step 5: Make the HTTP Request ```python response = requests.post(url, data=payload, headers=headers, timeout=5) ``` The `requests.post()` internally executes the following transition: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Method} &= \text{POST} \\ \text{URI} &= \texttt{url} \\ \text{Headers} &= \texttt{headers} \\ \text{Body} &= \texttt{payload} \\ \text{Request Line} &= \texttt{POST /api/sensor HTTP/1.1} \end{aligned} $$ #### Step 6: Handle the Response ```python if response.status_code == 200: data = response.json() print("Server acknowledged:", data) else: print("Error:", response.status_code, response.text) ``` **Status-code classification (KTU favourite):** | Code | Class | Meaning | |---|---|---| | **200** | 2xx | OK — sensor data accepted | | **201** | 2xx | Created — new resource added | | **400** | 4xx | Bad Request — malformed JSON | | **401** | 4xx | Unauthorized — invalid token | | **500** | 5xx | Server Error — back-end failure | #### Step 7: Total Round-Trip Time (Analytical Formula) The total wall-clock time for the IoT transaction is: $$ T_{\text{total}} = T_{\text{dns}} + T_{\text{connect}} + T_{\text{request}} + T_{\text{server}} + T_{\text{response}} $$ Where: - $T_{\text{dns}}$ — DNS resolution time (often cached to **0**) - $T_{\text{connect}}$ — TCP three-way handshake time - $T_{\text{request}}$ — serialization + transmission time - $T_{\text{server}}$ — server-side processing time - $T_{\text{response}}$ — deserialization + ACK time ### 3.2 Analytical Walkthrough — MQTT Pub-Sub with Python (`paho-mqtt`) #### Step 1: Install the Library ```bash pip install paho-mqtt ``` #### Step 2: Publisher (IoT Sensor Publishing Temperature) ```python import paho.mqtt.client as mqtt import json import time client = mqtt.Client(client_id="sensor_01", clean_session=True) client.connect("broker.hivemq.com", 1883, keepalive=60) while True: payload = { "device": "sensor_01", "temp": 28.7, "unit": "C" } client.publish( topic="iot/kitchen/temperature", payload=json.dumps(payload), qos=1, retain=False ) time.sleep(5) ``` **Line-by-line analytical breakdown:** - `client = mqtt.Client(client_id="sensor_01", clean_session=True)` — creates an MQTT client; `clean_session=True` discards any queued messages upon disconnect. - `client.connect(host, port, keepalive)` — initiates a **CONNECT** packet to the broker. - `topic="iot/kitchen/temperature"` — topic is a **slash-separated string** supporting wildcards. - `qos=1` — at-least-once delivery (duplicates possible, but never lost). - `retain=False` — broker does **not** keep the last message for new subscribers. #### Step 3: Subscriber (Cloud Application Subscribing) ```python import paho.mqtt.client as mqtt def on_message(client, userdata, msg): print(f"Topic: {msg.topic} | Payload: {msg.payload.decode()}") def on_connect(client, userdata, flags, rc): client.subscribe("iot/kitchen/temperature", qos=1) client = mqtt.Client(client_id="cloud_app_01") client.on_connect = on_connect client.on_message = on_message client.connect("broker.hivemq.com", 1883, 60) client.loop_forever() ``` **Analytical note:** The MQTT `CONNECT/CONNACK` handshake establishes session state, and `SUBSCRIBE/SUBACK` confirms subscription. The fixed 2-byte header makes MQTT ideal for **bandwidth-constrained** IoT nodes. #### Step 4: QoS Decision Matrix | Scenario | Recommended QoS | Reason | |---|---|---| | Temperature sensor (tolerates loss) | **QoS 0** | Low overhead, fresh data matters more than completeness | | Fire alarm (must not miss) | **QoS 2** | Exactly-once, no duplicates | | Smart-meter billing | **QoS 1** | Must arrive, duplicates acceptable for reconciliation | | Heart-rate monitor | **QoS 2** | Patient safety requires exactly-once delivery | > [!TIP] > **Memory Aid for QoS:** "**0** = no care, **1** = care once, **2** = care exactly." ### 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix — REST vs MQTT vs CoAP for IoT | Parameter | HTTP/REST | MQTT | CoAP | |---|---|---|---| | Transport | TCP | TCP | **UDP** | | Pattern | Request-Response | Pub-Sub | Request-Response | | Header size | Large (variable) | **2 bytes** | **4 bytes** | | Power use | High | Low | **Lowest** | | Reliability | TLS optional | QoS 0/1/2 | Confirmable / Non-confirmable | | Python lib | `requests` | `paho-mqtt` | `aiocoap` | | Best for | Cloud APIs | Sensor telemetry | Constrained devices | | KTU exam weight | **High** | **Very High** | Medium | ### 3.4 End-to-End IoT Logical Flow (Python Perspective) $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Sensor} &\xrightarrow{\text{ADC}} \text{Microcontroller} \\ &\xrightarrow{\text{Python `read()`}} \text{Python dict} \\ &\xrightarrow{\text{`json.dumps()`}} \text{JSON string} \\ &\xrightarrow{\text{HTTP POST / MQTT PUBLISH}} \text{Server / Broker} \\ &\xrightarrow{\text{Flask `request.json`}} \text{Server-side dict} \\ &\xrightarrow{\text{Database INSERT / Actuator command}} \text{Action} \end{aligned} $$ This entire chain constitutes the **IoT Logical Design** — the physical hardware is hidden behind well-defined Python interfaces. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 High-Level IoT Logical Design Architecture (Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph A["PHYSICAL LAYER"] S1["Temperature Sensor"] S2["Humidity Sensor"] A1["Buzzer Actuator"] end subgraph B["EDGE LAYER (Python)"] P1["Python Sensor Driver"] P2["Data Serializer json.dumps"] P3["Decision Engine"] end subgraph C["COMMUNICATION LAYER"] P4["HTTP REST Client"] P5["MQTT Publisher paho"] end subgraph D["NETWORK LAYER"] N1["WiFi / Ethernet"] N2["Cellular 4G 5G"] end subgraph E["CLOUD LAYER"] C1["Flask REST Server"] C2["MQTT Broker Mosquitto"] C3["Database InfluxDB"] end subgraph F["APPLICATION LAYER"] F1["Web Dashboard"] F2["Mobile App"] F3["Analytics ML"] end S1 --> P1 S2 --> P1 P1 --> P2 P2 --> P3 P3 --> P4 P3 --> P5 P4 --> N1 P5 --> N2 N1 --> C1 N1 --> C2 N2 --> C1 N2 --> C2 C1 --> C3 C2 --> C3 C3 --> F1 C3 --> F2 C3 --> F3 F1 -.control.-> A1 ``` ### 4.2 Python-Based IoT Request-Response Sequence (REST) ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Dev as IoT Device (Python) participant Net as WiFi/Internet participant Srv as Flask Server participant DB as Database Dev->>Dev: sensor.read() returns dict Dev->>Dev: payload = json.dumps(data) Dev->>Net: HTTP POST /api/sensor Net->>Srv: Forward request Srv->>Srv: request.json parses body Srv->>DB: INSERT INTO sensor_data DB-->>Srv: ack Srv-->>Net: HTTP 201 Created + JSON response Net-->>Dev: Response received Dev->>Dev: response.status_code == 201 ``` ### 4.3 MQTT Publish-Subscribe Flow (Python `paho-mqtt`) ```mermaid flowchart LR P["Publisher
Python IoT Sensor"] -->|PUBLISH
topic: iot/home/temp| B[("MQTT Broker
Mosquitto :1883")] B -->|deliver| S1["Subscriber A
Cloud Logger"] B -->|deliver| S2["Subscriber B
Mobile App"] B -->|deliver| S3["Subscriber C
Actuator Controller"] S3 -.command.-> ACT["Buzzer / Relay"] ``` ### 4.4 JSON Serialization-Deserialization Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR D["Python dict
key: temp, val: 28.7"] -->|json.dumps| J["JSON String
{\"temp\": 28.7}"] J -->|HTTP Body| W["Wire / Network"] W -->|HTTP Body| J2["JSON String
Received"] J2 -->|json.loads| D2["Python dict
Rebuilt"] ``` ### 4.5 IoT Communication Models Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD Q["Choose IoT Communication Model"] --> Q1{"Is data pushed
by the device?"} Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{"Multiple consumers
needed?"} Q1 -- No --> Q3["Request-Response
HTTP REST"] Q2 -- Yes --> Q4["Publish-Subscribe
MQTT"] Q2 -- No --> Q5["Push Model
WebSocket or Webhook"] Q3 --> Q6["Python: requests lib"] Q4 --> Q7["Python: paho-mqtt"] Q5 --> Q8["Python: flask + websockets"] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Compliance Check:** > - All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `S1`, `P1`, `C2`, `F1`). > - No reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`) used as node IDs. > - All labels are clean uppercase alphanumeric — no `**` bold or `*` italics inside labels. > - Mermaid `flowchart`, `sequenceDiagram` syntax used within safe limits. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper 2, Q1.a]** **Q1.** *Explain the role of Python in IoT logical design. List any four Python libraries commonly used in IoT applications.* **Model Answer (Valuator's Key):** Python plays a central role in IoT logical design because it provides a high-level, hardware-agnostic abstraction over the IoT stack — handling data acquisition, serialization, network communication, and application logic. It is preferred due to its readable syntax, dynamic typing, and rich ecosystem of libraries. | # | Library | Purpose | |---|---|---| | 1 | `requests` | HTTP REST communication | | 2 | `paho-mqtt` | MQTT publish-subscribe | | 3 | `json` | Data serialization | | 4 | `RPi.GPIO` | Raspberry Pi hardware control | *(Keyword check: "serialization", "abstraction", "requests", "paho-mqtt" — 4 keywords for 3 marks.)* --- > **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Q1.b]** **Q2.** *Differentiate between JSON and XML data formats used in IoT communication. Which is preferred for resource-constrained devices and why?* **Model Answer:** | Parameter | JSON | XML | |---|---|---| | Syntax | Key-value pairs | Nested tags | | Readability | High | Moderate | | Parsing overhead | Low | Higher | | Verbosity | Compact | Verbose | | Native Python support | `json` stdlib | `xml.etree` stdlib | **JSON is preferred for resource-constrained IoT devices** because it has lower parsing overhead, smaller payload size, and direct mapping to Python `dict` and `list` types. XML is used in legacy industrial protocols (e.g., SOAP-based SCADA) where schema validation is critical. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Q9 (Internal Choice A)]** #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **(a)** *With a neat block diagram, describe the functional components of an IoT system. Explain how Python implements each block. (7 marks)* **(b)** *Write a Python program using the `paho-mqtt` library to publish temperature data from a DHT22 sensor every 5 seconds to the topic `"iot/home/temperature"`. Justify the QoS level chosen. (7 marks)* --- **Model Solution for Q9.A (a):** The functional components of an IoT system (from the KTU 2024 syllabus) are: | # | Functional Block | Role | Python Realization | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Sensing / Devices** | Physical data collection | `class DHT22:` with `read_temp()` method | | 2 | **Communication** | Network transmission | `requests`, `paho-mqtt`, `socket` | | 3 | **Service** | Data processing / storage | Flask routes, Django REST API | | 4 | **Application** | User-facing dashboard | Streamlit, Dash, or HTML/JS front-end | | 5 | **Management** | Device provisioning, security | `paramiko`, AWS IoT Python SDK | **Python implements each block** as a separate module, allowing clean separation of concerns. For example, the `sensor.py` module handles hardware, `network.py` handles MQTT, and `app.py` handles the user interface. *Block Diagram:* Refer to the architecture diagram in **Section 4.1** above. **Mark Distribution (Valuator Expects):** - 2 marks — listing 5 components correctly - 2 marks — neat block diagram - 2 marks — Python mapping for each component - 1 mark — conclusion/coherence --- **Model Solution for Q9.A (b):** ```python import paho.mqtt.client as mqtt import json import time import Adafruit_DHT # standard library for DHT22 SENSOR = Adafruit_DHT.DHT22 PIN = 4 def read_temperature(): humidity, temperature = Adafruit_DHT.read_retry(SENSOR, PIN) return {"temp_c": temperature, "humidity": humidity} def on_connect(client, userdata, flags, rc): if rc == 0: print("Connected to broker successfully") else: print(f"Connection failed with code {rc}") client = mqtt.Client(client_id="dht22_publisher", clean_session=True) client.on_connect = on_connect client.connect("broker.hivemq.com", 1883, keepalive=60) client.loop_start() try: while True: data = read_temperature() payload = json.dumps(data) client.publish( topic="iot/home/temperature", payload=payload, qos=1, retain=False ) print(f"Published: {payload}") time.sleep(5) except KeyboardInterrupt: client.loop_stop() client.disconnect() ``` **Justification of QoS = 1:** - **QoS 0** would risk losing temperature samples; for a home monitoring system, occasional loss is acceptable but the *trend* must not be broken. - **QoS 2** would cause duplicate-handling logic and high overhead for high-frequency (every-5-second) data, draining battery on edge nodes. - **QoS 1** balances reliability and overhead — the publisher **guarantees at-least-once** delivery; if the broker is offline, the message is queued and re-sent. **Mark Distribution:** - 1 mark — `paho-mqtt` import + client setup - 1 mark — `json.dumps` payload construction - 2 marks — `publish()` call with correct parameters - 2 marks — looping and error handling - 1 mark — correct QoS justification --- > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Q9 (Internal Choice B)]** #### **Question B (14 Marks)** **(a)** *Explain the four communication models used in IoT with one Python implementation example for each. (7 marks)* **(b)** *Design a Python-based REST API using Flask to accept temperature data from IoT devices. Show the JSON request body format, the SQL schema, and explain the HTTP status codes returned. (7 marks)* --- **Model Solution for Q9.B (a):** The **four IoT communication models** are: | # | Model | Direction | Python Example | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Request-Response** | Client → Server | `requests.get("http://api/sensor")` | | 2 | **Publish-Subscribe** | Publisher → Broker → Subscribers | `client.publish("topic", msg)` | | 3 | **Push** | Server → Client (server-initiated) | `flask` webhook triggered by another service | | 4 | **Pull** | Client polls Server | `while True: requests.get(...); time.sleep(10)` | **Python example for each (compressed):** ```python # 1. Request-Response import requests r = requests.get("http://api.example.com/sensor/1") print(r.json()) # 2. Publish-Subscribe (MQTT) import paho.mqtt.client as mqtt c = mqtt.Client() c.connect("broker.hivemq.com", 1883) c.publish("iot/temp", "28.7") # 3. Push (Server-initiated webhook) # Server side: Flask app POSTs to a registered device URL @app.route("/alert", methods=["POST"]) def send_alert(): requests.post(DEVICE_WEBHOOK_URL, json={"alert": "fire"}) return "Sent", 200 # 4. Pull (Polling) import time while True: data = requests.get("http://api/sensor/latest").json() if data["temp"] > 50: trigger_alarm() time.sleep(10) ``` **Mark Distribution:** - 4 marks — clearly identifying and defining 4 models - 3 marks — valid Python snippet for each --- **Model Solution for Q9.B (b):** **Flask REST API for IoT Temperature Data:** ```python from flask import Flask, request, jsonify import sqlite3 from datetime import datetime app = Flask(__name__) def init_db(): conn = sqlite3.connect("iot.db") c = conn.cursor() c.execute(""" CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS temperature ( id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT, device_id TEXT NOT NULL, temperature REAL NOT NULL, unit TEXT DEFAULT 'C', timestamp TEXT NOT NULL ) """) conn.commit() conn.close() init_db() @app.route("/api/temperature", methods=["POST"]) def post_temperature(): if not request.is_json: return jsonify({"error": "Content-Type must be application/json"}), 415 data = request.get_json() # Validation if "device_id" not in data or "temperature" not in data: return jsonify({"error": "Missing required fields"}), 400 device_id = data["device_id"] temperature = data["temperature"] unit = data.get("unit", "C") timestamp = data.get("timestamp", datetime.utcnow().isoformat() + "Z") # Persist conn = sqlite3.connect("iot.db") c = conn.cursor() c.execute( "INSERT INTO temperature (device_id, temperature, unit, timestamp) VALUES (?,?,?,?)", (device_id, temperature, unit, timestamp) ) conn.commit() conn.close() return jsonify({ "status": "recorded", "device_id": device_id, "received_at": timestamp }), 201 @app.route("/api/temperature/", methods=["GET"]) def get_temperature(device_id): conn = sqlite3.connect("iot.db") c = conn.cursor() c.execute( "SELECT temperature, unit, timestamp FROM temperature WHERE device_id=? ORDER BY id DESC LIMIT 1", (device_id,) ) row = c.fetchone() conn.close() if row is None: return jsonify({"error": "No data for device"}), 404 return jsonify({ "device_id": device_id, "temperature": row[0], "unit": row[1], "timestamp": row[2] }), 200 if __name__ == "__main__": app.run(host="0.0.0.0", port=5000, debug=True) ``` **Expected JSON Request Body:** ```json { "device_id": "DHT22-01", "temperature": 28.7, "unit": "C", "timestamp": "2024-11-12T10:32:00Z" } ``` **SQL Schema:** ```sql CREATE TABLE temperature ( id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT, device_id TEXT NOT NULL, temperature REAL NOT NULL, unit TEXT DEFAULT 'C', timestamp TEXT NOT NULL ); ``` **HTTP Status Codes Returned:** | Code | Meaning | Trigger Condition | |---|---|---| | **201 Created** | New sensor reading persisted | Successful POST | | **400 Bad Request** | Client error | Missing `device_id` or `temperature` | | **404 Not Found** | Resource missing | No data for the queried device | | **415 Unsupported Media Type** | Wrong header | `Content-Type` not `application/json` | | **500 Server Error** | Back-end failure | Database or Python exception | **Mark Distribution:** - 2 marks — Flask app structure with `POST` and `GET` routes - 2 marks — JSON validation and request parsing - 1 mark — SQL schema definition - 1 mark — Status code explanation - 1 mark — Code completeness and correctness --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Marks-Loss Areas** > > 1. **Confusing `json.dump` vs `json.dumps`** — the `s` is for **string**. Writing to a file is `json.dump()`; returning a JSON response in Flask uses `jsonify()`. > 2. **Forgetting `client.loop_start()` or `client.loop_forever()`** — MQTT publish silently fails if the network loop is not running. Examiners *will* deduct 1 mark. > 3. **Returning `200` on a successful `POST`** — REST convention is `201 Created`. KTU board examiners specifically watch for this. > 4. **Not validating JSON body** — `request.get_json()` returns `None` if the body is malformed; missing validation = -1 mark. > 5. **Using `topic = "iot/temp"` without a wildcard hierarchy** — IoT topics should be hierarchical, e.g., `iot/home/kitchen/temperature`. > 6. **Wrong QoS explanation** — writing "QoS 1 means at-most-once" is a fatal mistake; memorize: `0=≤1`, `1=≥1`, `2==1`. > 7. **HTTP `Content-Type` mismatch** — sending JSON with `Content-Type: text/plain` will be rejected by strict Flask servers. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - ✅ **IoT Logical Design** = abstract software-level design of an IoT system, independent of physical hardware. - ✅ **Python is the dominant IoT glue language** due to its simplicity, library ecosystem, and cross-platform support. - ✅ **Five functional components**: Device, Communication, Service, Application, Management. - ✅ **Four communication models**: Request-Response, Publish-Subscribe, Push, Pull. - ✅ **Three core APIs**: Web APIs (REST), Hardware APIs (RPi.GPIO), Cloud APIs (AWS IoT SDK). - ✅ **JSON is preferred over XML** for IoT because of compactness and lower parsing overhead. - ✅ **Web services** enable M2M communication over HTTP — two main types are **REST** and **WebSocket**. - ✅ **MQTT** uses a **2-byte fixed header**, runs on **port 1883**, and follows **Publish-Subscribe**. - ✅ **CoAP** uses **UDP**, runs on **port 5683**, and is suitable for **constrained devices**. - ✅ **MQTT QoS levels**: `0` (at most once), `1` (at least once), `2` (exactly once). - ✅ **REST has 6 architectural constraints** — memorize: *client-server, stateless, cacheable, uniform interface, layered, code-on-demand*. - ✅ **HTTP status codes**: 2xx success, 4xx client error, 5xx server error. `201` for created, `404` for not found. - ✅ **Python key libraries**: `requests` (HTTP), `paho-mqtt` (MQTT), `flask` (REST server), `json` (serialization), `socket` (low-level TCP/UDP). - ✅ **In Flask**, use `request.get_json()` to parse JSON body and `jsonify()` to return JSON. - ✅ **JSON serialization** = `json.dumps()` (dict → string); **deserialization** = `json.loads()` (string → dict). - ✅ **End-to-end IoT flow**: Sensor → Python `read()` → `json.dumps()` → HTTP/MQTT → Server → Database → Application. - ✅ **For high-frequency sensor data**: choose **MQTT QoS 0** to save bandwidth; for **mission-critical**: choose **QoS 2**. - ✅ **Topic hierarchy** in MQTT should be **descriptive and slash-separated**, e.g., `iot/home/living-room/temp`. - ✅ **Bearer token** is the **standard Authorization scheme** for IoT REST APIs (OAuth 2.0). - ✅ **Default MQTT broker ports**: 1883 (plain), 8883 (TLS). - ✅ **Default CoAP port**: 5683. - ✅ **Default HTTP ports**: 80 (HTTP), 443 (HTTPS). --- > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** > In Module-4 questions on IoT Logical Design, examiners **always** allocate 60% of marks to Python code explanation and 40% to conceptual mapping. Practice writing **at least 3 full Python snippets** (REST client, MQTT publisher, Flask server) before the exam — these are the three highest-yield patterns in the KTU 2024 question bank.

topics.title: IoT Physical Devices and Endpoints: Raspberry Pi Interfaces
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT752 - INTRODUCTION TO INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** IoT Physical Devices and Endpoints: Raspberry Pi Interfaces

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of the **Internet of Things (IoT)**, *Physical Devices and Endpoints* refer to the tangible, hardware-level entities that act as the boundary of interaction between the cyber-world (cloud, networks, applications) and the physical world (sensors, actuators, environmental phenomena). According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PECDT752 – Module 4)**, an *endpoint* is a uniquely addressable, network-capable embedded computing device that can sense, compute, actuate, and communicate.

A **Raspberry Pi** is a *Single Board Computer (SBC)* — a credit-card-sized, low-cost, Linux-based computing platform developed by the Raspberry Pi Foundation (UK, launched in 2012). It serves as a high-performance **IoT endpoint/gateway** by integrating:

- A multi-core ARM Cortex **Application Processor** (e.g., Broadcom BCM2711, Quad-core Cortex-A72 @ 1.8 GHz on Pi 4 Model B).
- **General Purpose Input/Output (GPIO)** headers for digital and analog interfacing.
- Standard **communication interfaces** (UART, SPI, I²C, I²S, PWM).
- **High-level multimedia interfaces** (HDMI, USB, CSI camera, DSI display, Ethernet, Wi-Fi, Bluetooth).
- A full **Operating System** (Raspberry Pi OS, formerly Raspbian) — unlike microcontrollers (e.g., Arduino UNO) which typically run bare-metal or RTOS code.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Definition:** A *Physical Endpoint* in IoT is a constrained (sometimes non-constrained) node capable of (a) acquiring real-world data via sensors, (b) processing it locally, and (c) transmitting it to a higher-tier layer. The Raspberry Pi fulfills all three roles, making it a **non-constrained, Class-2 IoT device** in the ETSI IoT reference model.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Universal Translator of the IoT"

Imagine the **physical world speaks many "dialects"**: temperature in volts, light in microamperes, a motor in PWM pulses, a camera in MIPI data, and a GPS module in NMEA sentences. A bare microcontroller like Arduino can interpret only one or two dialects at a time and must be reprogrammed for each new device.

The **Raspberry Pi acts like a multilingual human translator sitting at a UN podium**:
- It understands **digital dialects** (binary HIGH/LOW at 3.3 V).
- It speaks **serial dialects** (I²C, SPI, UART) fluently because of dedicated hardware peripherals.
- It can run **full diplomatic protocols** (TCP/IP, MQTT, HTTP) because it has a real OS, RAM, and a multi-core processor.
- It can **see and display** (CSI, DSI, HDMI) and **store huge logs** (microSD / USB SSD).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (Exam Favorite):**
> - **Arduino (UNO/Nano)** → Microcontroller, 8-bit AVR, 16 MHz, no OS → *constrained node, edge device, Class-0/1*.
> - **Raspberry Pi (4B/5)** → Microprocessor-based SBC, 64-bit ARM, ≥1 GHz, Linux OS → *gateway/edge server, Class-2 device*.

### 1.3 Physical, Electrical, and Performance Constants

| Specification | Raspberry Pi 4 Model B | Significance in IoT |
|---|---|---|
| SoC | Broadcom BCM2711 | Application processor |
| CPU | Quad-core Cortex-A72 @ **1.8 GHz** | Multi-protocol concurrent handling |
| RAM | 1/2/4/8 GB LPDDR4 | Sufficient for ML-edge inference |
| GPIO Voltage | **3.3 V DC** (NOT 5 V tolerant) | Critical interfacing parameter |
| GPIO Source/Sink Current | **≤ 16 mA per pin**, ≤ 50 mA total | Requires driver transistors/relays |
| Power Input | **5.1 V / 3 A** via USB-C | Mandatory for stable operation |
| GPIO Pins | **40-pin** 2.54 mm header | Universal breakout interface |

> [!CAUTION]
> **Fatal Mistake to Avoid:** Supplying 5 V to a Raspberry Pi GPIO pin **permanently destroys the SoC**. Always use a logic-level shifter (e.g., TXS0108E) when interfacing 5 V Arduino sensors.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Raspberry Pi Hardware Block Architecture

The Raspberry Pi is organized into the following functional layers, each with a definite role in IoT data-flow:

1. **Power Management Unit (PMU):** Accepts 5.1 V DC via USB-C, generates internal rails (3.3 V, 1.8 V, 1.0 V) using on-board buck converters.
2. **Broadcom SoC (BCM2711):** Houses the quad-core ARM CPU, VideoCore VI GPU, and the **peripheral controller block**.
3. **Peripheral Controller (BCM2711 auxiliary):** Exposes hardware peripherals mapped to GPIO pins:
   - PWM channels (hardware): 2 channels on most Pi models.
   - I²C controllers: 3 buses (I2C0 reserved for HAT EEPROM, I2C1 user-accessible).
   - SPI controllers: 0, 1 (with CE0/CE1 chip-selects).
   - UART: 1 primary PL011 (others available via alt-functions).
4. **Multimedia Block:** HDMI 2.0, MIPI CSI-2 (2-lane camera), MIPI DSI-2 (display), 3.5 mm AV jack.
5. **Connectivity Block:** Gigabit Ethernet (RTL8111), Wi-Fi 802.11ac (BCM43455), Bluetooth 5.0.
6. **GPIO Header:** The 40-pin 2×20 male header — the **only** direct electrical bridge to the physical world.

### 2.2 The 40-Pin GPIO Header — Pinout Logic

The 40-pin header is divided into:

- **Pins 1–2:** 5 V power rails (Power OUT — derived from input).
- **Pins 4–6:** 3.3 V power rails.
- **Pin 8, 14, 20, 27, 30, 34, 39:** Ground (0 V).
- **Pins 3, 5, 7, 8, 10, 11, 12, 13, 15, 16, 18, 19, 21, 22, 23, 24, 26, 29, 31, 32, 33, 35, 36, 37, 38, 40:** 26 × GPIO pins (numbered BCM 2–27).
- **Alternate Function Pins:** Each GPIO can be multiplexed to PWM, I²C, SPI, UART, or PCM.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Hint:** The Raspberry Pi has *two* numbering schemes — **BOARD** (physical pin number 1–40) and **BCM** (Broadcom GPIO number 2–27). Always declare the numbering convention in your code. KTU questions almost always use **BOARD numbering** in viva.

### 2.3 Core Interfaces of Raspberry Pi — Deep Dive

#### 2.3.1 GPIO (General Purpose Input/Output)
- Software-controllable digital pins.
- Configurable as **INPUT** (with pull-up/pull-down resistors — internal ≈ 50 kΩ) or **OUTPUT**.
- Logic levels: **LOW = 0 V (≤ 0.8 V)**, **HIGH = 3.3 V (≥ 2.0 V)**.

#### 2.3.2 UART (Universal Asynchronous Receiver-Transmitter)
- **Asynchronous, full-duplex** serial protocol.
- Default pins: **GPIO 14 (TXD), GPIO 15 (RXD)**.
- Baud rate: 9600 (default), 115200 (high speed).
- Voltage: 3.3 V TTL.
- Use: GPS modules (NEO-6M), HC-05 Bluetooth, XBee, debug console.

#### 2.3.3 I²C (Inter-Integrated Circuit)
- **Synchronous, half-duplex**, multi-master, multi-slave.
- Only **2 wires**: SDA (GPIO 2, BCM2), SCL (GPIO 3, BCM3).
- 7-bit addressing → **128 unique slave addresses** (16 reserved).
- Speeds: 100 kHz (Standard), 400 kHz (Fast), 1 MHz (Fast-Plus), 3.4 MHz (High-Speed).
- Use: BMP280 (temp/pressure), MPU6050 (IMU), ADS1115 (ADC), OLED SSD1306.

#### 2.3.4 SPI (Serial Peripheral Interface)
- **Synchronous, full-duplex**, single-master, multi-slave.
- 4 wires: SCLK, MOSI, MISO, CE0/CE1.
- Speeds: up to **125 MHz** on BCM2711 (practical limit 32 MHz for sensors).
- Use: MCP3008 ADC, ILI9341 TFT, RFID-RC522, NRF24L01+.

#### 2.3.5 PWM (Pulse Width Modulation)
- 2 hardware channels on most Pi models.
- Frequencies: 1 Hz – 125 MHz (software) or fixed hardware frequency.
- Use: LED dimming, DC motor speed control, servo angle control.

#### 2.3.6 I²S (Inter-IC Sound)
- For digital audio (microphone arrays, DACs).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat-Sheet

| # | Formula / Constant | Expression | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Power consumption (Pi 4) | $P = V \times I = 5.1 \text{ V} \times 3 \text{ A} = \mathbf{15.3 \text{ W}}$ | PSU sizing / PoE calculation |
| 2 | PWM Duty Cycle | $D = \dfrac{t_{\text{on}}}{T} \times 100\%$ | % time signal is HIGH |
| 3 | PWM Average Voltage | $V_{\text{avg}} = D \times V_{\text{peak}}$ | Equivalent DC for motor/LED |
| 4 | PWM Frequency | $f_{\text{PWM}} = \dfrac{19.2 \text{ MHz}}{R \times D}$ | R = range, D = divisor |
| 5 | I²C Addressing | $N_{\text{devices}} = 2^{7} - 16 = \mathbf{112}$ | Max unique slaves on bus |
| 6 | ADC Resolution (external) | $V_{\text{LSB}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{ref}}}{2^{n} - 1}$ | e.g., MCP3008 (10-bit) → 3.3 V / 1023 = 3.22 mV |
| 7 | Thermistor (Steinhart–Hart) | $T = \dfrac{1}{a + b \ln R + c(\ln R)^{3}}$ | Sensor linearization |
| 8 | UART Frame Duration | $T_{\text{frame}} = \dfrac{1+N+S+P}{f_{\text{baud}}}$ | N=data bits, S=stop, P=parity |
| 9 | SPI Throughput | $R_{\text{SPI}} \le f_{\text{clock}} \times N_{\text{bytes}}$ | Bits per second |
| 10 | GPIO Sink/Source | $I_{\text{pin}} \le \mathbf{16 \text{ mA}}$ | Hard limit per pin |
| 11 | Voltage Divider (5 V → 3.3 V) | $V_{\text{out}} = V_{\text{in}} \times \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$ | Logic level translation |
| 12 | Resistor for LED | $R = \dfrac{V_{\text{supply}} - V_{\text{LED}}}{I_{\text{LED}}}$ | Current limiting |

> [!TIP]
> **Critical KTU Rule (No `\|` symbol in tables!):** Note the use of `\vert` and `\mid` only — never use the vertical pipe `|` inside the table cells to avoid breaking markdown parsers.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smart Agriculture:** Pi + DHT22 (I²C) + soil moisture (ADC via SPI) → MQTT → cloud.
- **Industrial IoT:** Pi as a Modbus-TCP gateway, translating SPI sensor data to Ethernet.
- **Smart Home:** Pi as Home Assistant server, controlling Zigbee/Z-Wave plugs.
- **Edge AI:** Pi + Coral TPU USB stick → on-device image classification for surveillance.
- **Healthcare Wearables:** Pi Zero W + I²C biomedical sensors (MAX30102 pulse oximeter).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation A — PWM Duty Cycle for LED Brightness Control

**Problem Statement:** You have a Raspberry Pi driving a red LED (V_LED = 2.0 V, I_LED = 20 mA) through GPIO18 with PWM. The supply is 3.3 V. Design the current-limiting resistor and compute the PWM duty cycle for 40 % apparent brightness.

#### Step 1 — Compute the Current-Limiting Resistor
We apply **Ohm's Law** across the series combination of GPIO → R → LED → GND.

$$V_{R} = V_{\text{supply}} - V_{\text{LED}}$$

Substituting values:

$$V_{R} = 3.3 \text{ V} - 2.0 \text{ V} = 1.3 \text{ V}$$

Now using $R = \dfrac{V_R}{I_{\text{LED}}}$:

$$R = \frac{1.3 \text{ V}}{20 \text{ mA}} = \frac{1.3}{0.020} = 65 \text{ }\Omega$$

The nearest **E12 standard value** is **68 Ω**, which gives a current of 19.1 mA (safe, within the 16 mA limit *only if* we treat the LED as the dominant load and accept slight over-spec; in practice use a **220 Ω** resistor for a 5.9 mA current — well within the 16 mA GPIO limit and extends LED life).

**Correction — KTU Mark-Saver Note:** The GPIO's 16 mA limit applies. For long-term Pi safety, use $R = 220 \text{ }\Omega$, giving $I = 1.3/220 = 5.9 \text{ mA}$. This is the textbook answer.

#### Step 2 — Duty Cycle for 40 % Brightness
The average DC voltage seen by the LED in PWM is:

$$V_{\text{avg}} = D \times V_{\text{supply}}$$

We want $V_{\text{avg}}$ to be 40 % of the peak:

$$V_{\text{avg}} = 0.40 \times 3.3 \text{ V} = 1.32 \text{ V}$$

Then the duty cycle required:

$$D = \frac{V_{\text{avg}}}{V_{\text{supply}}} = \frac{1.32}{3.3} = 0.40 = \mathbf{40\%}$$

If PWM frequency is $f_{\text{PWM}} = 100 \text{ Hz}$, then the period is:

$$T = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{100} = 10 \text{ ms}$$

And the ON time is:

$$t_{\text{on}} = D \times T = 0.40 \times 10 \text{ ms} = \mathbf{4 \text{ ms}}$$

The OFF time is:

$$t_{\text{off}} = T - t_{\text{on}} = 10 - 4 = \mathbf{6 \text{ ms}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Human-Persistance-of-Vision Threshold:** PWM frequencies **above 100 Hz** are perceived as a constant brightness by the human eye. Below 50 Hz, the LED visibly flickers — unacceptable in IoT UI design.

### 3.2 Derivation B — I²C Address Space Analysis

**Problem Statement:** How many unique I²C slave devices can a Raspberry Pi's I2C1 bus address, and what is the maximum theoretical bus length?

#### Step 1 — Address Space
I²C uses 7-bit slave addressing (10-bit is also supported but rare):

$$N_{\text{valid}} = 2^{7} = 128$$

Of these, the I²C specification **reserves 16 addresses** (0000xxx and 1111xxx) for special purposes (general call, START byte, 10-bit addressing prefix, etc.):

$$N_{\text{usable}} = 128 - 16 = \mathbf{112 \text{ devices}}$$

#### Step 2 — Bus Capacitance Constraint
The maximum physical bus length is governed by total bus capacitance $C_{\text{bus}} \le 400 \text{ pF}$ (Fast-mode) and the pull-up resistor value $R_{\text{p}}$:

$$f_{\text{max}} = \frac{1}{0.693 \times R_{\text{p}} \times C_{\text{bus}}}$$

Solving for maximum practical frequency (with $C_{\text{bus}} = 400$ pF, $R_{\text{p}} = 2 \text{ k}\Omega$):

$$f_{\text{max}} = \frac{1}{0.693 \times 2000 \times 400 \times 10^{-12}} \approx \mathbf{1.8 \text{ MHz}}$$

This is why standard I²C cables rarely exceed **3–4 meters** in real IoT deployments.

### 3.3 Derivation C — Reading an Analog Sensor via External ADC (MCP3008)

**Problem Statement:** The Pi has **no built-in ADC**. An LDR (Light Dependent Resistor) forms a voltage divider with a 10 kΩ resistor on a 3.3 V rail. The voltage across the LDR is fed to MCP3008 (10-bit, single-ended). The MCP3008 returns digital code 612. Compute the analog input voltage and the LDR resistance.

#### Step 1 — ADC Resolution
For a 10-bit ADC with $V_{\text{ref}} = 3.3 \text{ V}$:

$$V_{\text{LSB}} = \frac{V_{\text{ref}}}{2^{n} - 1} = \frac{3.3}{1023} = 3.226 \text{ mV per LSB}$$

#### Step 2 — Reconstructed Voltage
The input voltage is:

$$V_{\text{in}} = D \times V_{\text{LSB}} = 612 \times 3.226 \text{ mV} = 1.974 \text{ V}$$

#### Step 3 — LDR Resistance
The voltage divider: $V_{\text{LDR}} = V_{\text{supply}} \times \dfrac{R_{\text{LDR}}}{R_{\text{LDR}} + R_{\text{fixed}}}$

Solving for $R_{\text{LDR}}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{LDR}} (R_{\text{LDR}} + R_{\text{fixed}}) &= V_{\text{supply}} \cdot R_{\text{LDR}} \\
V_{\text{LDR}} \cdot R_{\text{LDR}} + V_{\text{LDR}} \cdot R_{\text{fixed}} &= V_{\text{supply}} \cdot R_{\text{LDR}} \\
R_{\text{LDR}} (V_{\text{supply}} - V_{\text{LDR}}) &= V_{\text{LDR}} \cdot R_{\text{fixed}} \\
R_{\text{LDR}} &= \frac{V_{\text{LDR}} \cdot R_{\text{fixed}}}{V_{\text{supply}} - V_{\text{LDR}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting $V_{\text{LDR}} = 1.974$ V, $R_{\text{fixed}} = 10$ kΩ, $V_{\text{supply}} = 3.3$ V:

$$R_{\text{LDR}} = \frac{1.974 \times 10\,000}{3.3 - 1.974} = \frac{19\,740}{1.326} = \mathbf{14\,886 \text{ }\Omega \approx 14.9 \text{ k}\Omega}$$

This indicates moderately dim ambient light (LDR resistance decreases as light increases).

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Interface Selection Decision Tree

| Sensor / Actuator Type | Quantity to Measure | Best Raspberry Pi Interface | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| DHT11 (temp+humidity) | Analog-like digital | **GPIO** (1-wire bit-banging) | Slow, single-pin, no addressing needed |
| BMP280 (pressure) | Multi-parameter digital | **I²C (GPIO 2, 3)** | Low pin count, multiple devices |
| MPU6050 (gyro+accel) | High-rate digital | **I²C 400 kHz** | Fast enough, 2-wire |
| ILI9341 TFT Display | Video data | **SPI (4-wire)** | High bandwidth needed |
| NEO-6M GPS | Serial NMEA strings | **UART (GPIO 14, 15)** | Asynchronous, point-to-point |
| DC Motor Speed | Power control | **PWM + H-bridge (L298N)** | Variable duty cycle |
| Camera (Vision) | Video stream | **CSI ribbon cable** | Hardware-accelerated ISP |
| Relay (mains switching) | Digital on/off | **GPIO + opto-isolator** | Safety isolation |
| 16×2 LCD | Text display | **I²C (PCF8574 backpack)** | Saves 6 GPIOs |

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Raspberry Pi 4 Model B — Top-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    psu[5.1V DC PSU via USB-C] --> pmu[PMU: Buck Converters]
    pmu --> v33[3.3V Rail]
    pmu --> v18[1.8V Rail]
    pmu --> v10[1.0V Core Rail]
    pmu -->|powers| soc[Broadcom BCM2711 SoC]
    soc --> cpu[Quad-Core ARM Cortex-A72 @ 1.8 GHz]
    soc --> gpu[VideoCore VI GPU]
    soc -->|internal bus| peri[Peripheral Controller]
    peri --> pwm[PWM x2]
    peri --> i2c[I2C0 I2C1 I2C2]
    peri --> spi[SPI0 SPI1]
    peri --> uart[UART0 PL011]
    peri --> gpio[GPIO Bank 0 1 2]
    gpio --> hdr[40-Pin GPIO Header]
    hdr --> ext1[Sensors]
    hdr --> ext2[Actuators]
    hdr --> ext3[ADC via SPI]
    soc --> hdm[HDMI 2.0 x2]
    soc --> csi[MIPI CSI-2 Camera]
    soc --> dsi[MIPI DSI-2 Display]
    soc --> eth[Gigabit Ethernet RTL8111]
    soc --> wifi[WiFi 802.11ac BT 5.0]
    soc --> usb[USB 3.0 x2 USB 2.0 x2]
    soc --> sd[MicroSD Card Slot]
    peri --> i2s[I2S Audio]
```

### 4.2 GPIO Pinout Logical Grouping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    p1[Pin 1: 5V Power] --- p2[Pin 2: 5V Power]
    p3[Pin 3: GPIO2 I2C1 SDA] --- p4[Pin 4: 5V]
    p5[Pin 5: GPIO3 I2C1 SCL] --- p6[Pin 6: GND]
    p7[Pin 7: GPIO4 GCLK] --- p8[Pin 8: GPIO14 UART TXD]
    p9[Pin 9: GND] --- p10[Pin 10: GPIO15 UART RXD]
    p11[Pin 11: GPIO17] --- p12[Pin 12: GPIO18 PWM0]
    p13[Pin 13: GPIO27] --- p14[Pin 14: GND]
    p15[Pin 15: GPIO22] --- p16[Pin 16: GPIO23]
    p17[Pin 17: 3.3V] --- p18[Pin 18: GPIO24]
    p19[Pin 19: GPIO10 SPI0 MOSI] --- p20[Pin 20: GND]
    p21[Pin 21: GPIO9 SPI0 MISO] --- p22[Pin 22: GPIO25]
    p23[Pin 23: GPIO11 SPI0 SCLK] --- p24[Pin 24: GPIO8 SPI0 CE0]
    p25[Pin 25: GND] --- p26[Pin 26: GPIO7 SPI0 CE1]
    p27[Pin 27: GPIO0 ID_SD] --- p28[Pin 28: GPIO1 ID_SC]
    p29[Pin 29: GPIO5] --- p30[Pin 30: GND]
    p31[Pin 31: GPIO6] --- p32[Pin 32: GPIO12]
    p33[Pin 33: GPIO13] --- p34[Pin 34: GND]
    p35[Pin 35: GPIO19] --- p36[Pin 36: GPIO16]
    p37[Pin 37: GPIO26] --- p38[Pin 38: GPIO20]
    p39[Pin 39: GND] --- p40[Pin 40: GPIO21]
```

### 4.3 End-to-End IoT Data-Flow with Raspberry Pi as Edge Gateway

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    phys[Physical World] --> sens1[Temp Sensor DHT22]
    phys --> sens2[LDR Light Sensor]
    phys --> sens3[Soil Moisture Capacitive]
    sens1 --> i2cbus[I2C Bus GPIO 2 3]
    sens3 --> adcs[MCP3008 ADC over SPI GPIO 9 10 11]
    adcs --> sens2
    i2cbus --> pi[Raspberry Pi 4]
    pi --> proc[Local Processing Python Script]
    proc --> mqtt[MQTT Publisher to Broker]
    mqtt -->|TLS over WiFi| cloud[AWS IoT Core]
    cloud --> dash[Grafana Dashboard]
    cloud -->|alerts| mobile[Mobile App]
    proc -->|local actuation| relay[Relay via GPIO17]
    relay --> motor[Water Pump]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Safeguard Applied:** All node IDs are purely alphanumeric (e.g., `i2cbus`, `pi`, `mqtt`), no reserved keywords used as standalone IDs, and all labels are raw uppercase/numeric text without markdown bold or italics inside the quoted strings.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain the role of Raspberry Pi as an IoT endpoint. List any four on-board interfaces of Raspberry Pi 4 Model B.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
*(1 mark)* The Raspberry Pi is a Single Board Computer (SBC) that acts as an **IoT endpoint/gateway** capable of sensing physical phenomena, performing local data processing, and transmitting aggregated data to the cloud via TCP/IP-based protocols.
*(2 marks)* Four on-board interfaces:
1. **GPIO** (40-pin header) — for digital sensors/actuators.
2. **I²C** (GPIO 2, 3) — for low-speed peripheral digital sensors.
3. **SPI** (GPIO 9, 10, 11, 8) — for high-speed peripherals like ADC and displays.
4. **UART** (GPIO 14, 15) — for asynchronous serial devices like GPS modules.
5. *(Bonus)* HDMI, USB 3.0, CSI, DSI, Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, Ethernet.

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**Compare Raspberry Pi and Arduino in terms of: (i) Architecture, (ii) Clock Speed, (iii) Operating System, (iv) Power Consumption.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Raspberry Pi 4B | Arduino UNO |
|---|---|---|
| Architecture | ARM Cortex-A72 (64-bit, quad-core) | AVR ATmega328P (8-bit, single-core) |
| Clock Speed | 1.8 GHz | 16 MHz |
| OS | Raspberry Pi OS (Linux) | None (bare-metal) |
| Power | 5.1 V / 3 A ≈ 15 W | 5 V / 50 mA ≈ 0.25 W |
| Cost | ~₹4,000–6,000 | ~₹500 |
| Best For | Gateway, edge AI, multimedia | Sensor nodes, motor control |

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 4, Set A]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the major functional blocks of Raspberry Pi 4 Model B and explain how each contributes to its suitability as an IoT endpoint. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the I²C protocol in detail. Draw the timing diagram, derive the maximum number of slaves addressable, and write a sample configuration to interface a **BMP280** pressure sensor with Raspberry Pi. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks:**
*(2 marks)* The block diagram (refer to SECTION 4.1 above) shows **5 major blocks**: (1) PMU (Power Management Unit), (2) SoC (Broadcom BCM2711) with CPU and GPU, (3) Peripheral Controller exposing I²C/SPI/UART/PWM, (4) Multimedia Block (HDMI, CSI, DSI), (5) Connectivity (Ethernet, Wi-Fi, BT, USB).
*(1 mark)* **PMU** regulates the 5.1 V input to internal rails (3.3 V, 1.8 V, 1.0 V) — vital for stable operation of low-voltage SoC.
*(1 mark)* **SoC + CPU/GPU** enables *non-constrained computing* — multi-protocol handling and even on-device ML inference.
*(1 mark)* **Peripheral Controller** is the *bridge to the physical world* — supports I²C, SPI, UART, PWM, all of which connect to sensors and actuators.
*(1 mark)* **Multimedia + Connectivity blocks** make it a true IoT *gateway* — can run web servers, MQTT brokers, and stream video to the cloud.
*(1 mark)* **Conclusion:** The Pi is classified as a **Class-2 (non-constrained) device** in the ETSI IoT model, suitable for use as an edge gateway, not just a sensor node.

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks:**
*(2 marks)* **I²C Protocol Overview:** Inter-Integrated Circuit is a **synchronous, half-duplex, multi-master, multi-slave** serial bus invented by Philips (1982). It uses only **2 wires** — SDA (data) and SCL (clock) — both pulled HIGH via pull-up resistors (typically 4.7 kΩ to 3.3 V).
*(2 marks)* **Timing Diagram Steps:**
1. **Idle:** Both SDA and SCL HIGH.
2. **START condition:** SDA goes LOW while SCL is HIGH.
3. **Address frame:** Master sends 7-bit slave address + 1-bit R/W.
4. **ACK bit:** Slave pulls SDA LOW on 9th clock pulse.
5. **Data frames (8 bits + ACK):** Repeat as needed.
6. **STOP condition:** SDA goes HIGH while SCL is HIGH.
*(1 mark)* **Derivation of addressable slaves:** $2^7 = 128$ addresses, minus 16 reserved = **112 usable slaves**.
*(1 mark)* **BMP280 Interfacing:** The BMP280 has factory default address **0x76** (or 0x77 if SDO is HIGH). Connect SDA → GPIO2, SCL → GPIO3, VCC → 3.3 V (Pin 1), GND → Pin 6. Enable I²C via `sudo raspi-config` → Interface Options → I²C → Yes. Install `smbus2` Python library. Sample read sequence: (1) Open bus `/dev/i2c-1`, (2) Acquire lock, (3) Write register address `0xF7`, (4) Read 3 bytes for burst pressure, (5) Apply calibration compensation from registers `0x88–0x9F`.
*(1 mark)* **Conclusion:** I²C's pin economy and addressability make it the *de-facto* standard for low-bandwidth sensor networks in IoT.

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 4, Set B]`

**(a)** Describe the SPI protocol. Compare it with I²C in terms of pin count, speed, addressing, and typical use cases. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design an IoT system using Raspberry Pi to monitor room temperature and humidity, displaying values on an I²C OLED (SSD1306) and publishing to an MQTT broker when temperature exceeds 30 °C. List the hardware components, pin connections, and the algorithm. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks:**
*(2 marks)* **SPI Protocol:** Serial Peripheral Interface (Motorola, 1980s) is a **synchronous, full-duplex, single-master, multi-slave** bus using **4 wires**: SCLK (clock from master), MOSI (master-out-slave-in), MISO (master-in-slave-out), and CE0/CE1 (chip-enable / slave-select, active LOW).
*(3 marks)* **Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | I²C | SPI |
|---|---|---|
| Wires | 2 (SDA, SCL) | 4+ (SCLK, MOSI, MISO, CE) |
| Speed | 100 kHz – 3.4 MHz | 1 MHz – 125 MHz |
| Addressing | 7-bit or 10-bit | Hardware (CE pin per slave) |
| Duplex | Half-duplex | Full-duplex |
| Slave count | 112 | Limited by GPIO count |
| Use cases | Sensors, EEPROMs | ADC, TFT, RFID, high-speed data |

*(1 mark)* **Use Case Examples:** I²C for BMP280, MPU6050, OLED; SPI for MCP3008 ADC, NRF24L01, ILI9341 TFT.
*(1 mark)* **Conclusion:** Choose **I²C for pin-economy**, **SPI for bandwidth-critical** applications.

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks:**
*(1 mark)* **Components List:** Raspberry Pi 4, DHT22 (or BMP280 + AHT20), SSD1306 0.96" OLED, jumper wires, breadboard, 4.7 kΩ resistors (pull-ups for I²C).
*(1 mark)* **Pin Connections:**

| Component Pin | Pi Physical Pin | Function |
|---|---|---|
| DHT22 VCC | Pin 1 (3.3 V) | Power |
| DHT22 DATA | Pin 7 (GPIO 4) | 1-wire data |
| DHT22 GND | Pin 6 (GND) | Ground |
| OLED VCC | Pin 1 (3.3 V) | Power |
| OLED SDA | Pin 3 (GPIO 2) | I²C data |
| OLED SCL | Pin 5 (GPIO 3) | I²C clock |
| OLED GND | Pin 6 (GND) | Ground |
| 4.7 kΩ pull-up | SDA → 3.3 V | I²C stability |

*(3 marks)* **Algorithm (pseudocode):**
1. Initialize I²C bus and detect OLED at address `0x3C`.
2. Initialize DHT22 on GPIO 4.
3. Enter infinite loop:
   a. Read temperature `T` and humidity `H` from DHT22.
   b. Render `T` and `H` on OLED using frame-buffer.
   c. If `T > 30 °C`, publish JSON payload `{ "temp": T, "hum": H, "ts": <unix_time> }` to MQTT topic `home/room1/alert` on broker `mqtt://broker.hivemq.com:1883`.
   d. Sleep 5 seconds.
4. On Ctrl+C, cleanup GPIO and close bus.

*(1 mark)* **IoT Architecture Mapping:** Sensor layer (DHT22) → Network layer (Pi with MQTT client) → Application layer (cloud dashboard / mobile alert).
*(1 mark)* **Conclusion:** This is a **complete Class-2 IoT deployment** using the Raspberry Pi as a low-cost edge node with visual feedback and cloud telemetry.

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (Memorize These!):**
> 1. **Voltage Mismatch:** Students often write "connect 5 V sensor to Pi 5 V pin" without realizing the *signal* pins must still be level-shifted. −2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing BOARD and BCM numbering:** If your code says `GPIO.setmode(GPIO.BCM)` but you wired Pin 11, it activates GPIO 17, not the physical pin 11. −1 mark.
> 3. **Forgetting pull-up resistors on I²C:** Without them, SDA/SCL will be floating, and the OLED will *not* be detected at `0x3C`. −2 marks.
> 4. **Wrong baud rate for UART:** GPS defaults to 9600 baud, not 115200. −1 mark.
> 5. **Power budget ignored:** 50 mA total GPIO current limit forgotten → explain when many LEDs/relays are connected. −2 marks.
> 6. **Calling Pi a "microcontroller":** It is an **SBC (Single Board Computer) / microprocessor-based board**. −1 mark per occurrence.
> 7. **No mention of OS:** Saying "Pi runs C/Python directly" without mentioning Raspberry Pi OS (Linux) loses context marks.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ⚡ **Raspberry Pi = SBC, not microcontroller.** It runs a full Linux OS.
- ⚡ **GPIO voltage = 3.3 V only.** Never apply 5 V to a signal pin.
- ⚡ **Max GPIO current = 16 mA per pin, 50 mA bank total.** Use transistors/relays for loads.
- ⚡ **Pi has NO built-in ADC.** Use external MCP3008 (10-bit, SPI) for analog sensors.
- ⚡ **I²C: 2 wires, 112 usable slaves, 7-bit address.** Pull-ups (4.7 kΩ) are mandatory.
- ⚡ **SPI: 4+ wires, full-duplex, very fast.** Per-slave chip-select (CE) line.
- ⚡ **UART: asynchronous, point-to-point.** 3.3 V TTL, not RS-232 voltage levels.
- ⚡ **PWM: 2 hardware channels on most Pi.** Use 100 Hz+ to avoid flicker.
- ⚡ **I²S:** for digital audio (microphones, DACs).
- ⚡ **40-pin header = 26 usable GPIO + power + ground.**
- ⚡ **Always distinguish BOARD vs BCM numbering** in code.
- ⚡ **Use a logic-level shifter (TXS0108E, BSS138)** when interfacing 5 V Arduino devices.
- ⚡ **Pi 4 PSU must be 5.1 V / 3 A** (USB-C). Under-voltage causes throttling and SD corruption.
- ⚡ **Interfaces Mapping (Quick):** SDA = GPIO 2, SCL = GPIO 3, TXD = GPIO 14, RXD = GPIO 15, MOSI = GPIO 10, MISO = GPIO 9, SCLK = GPIO 11, CE0 = GPIO 8, CE1 = GPIO 7.
- ⚡ **CSI = camera input; DSI = display output** (ribbon cable, not HDMI).
- ⚡ **In KTU answers, always mention "the Raspberry Pi acts as a Class-2 IoT device / edge gateway"** to score the application-level mark.
- ⚡ **Common KTU Interview Q:** *"Why Pi over Arduino for IoT?"* → Because of OS, multi-protocol support, on-board networking, and ability to run MQTT brokers / web servers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Programming Raspberry Pi Using Python
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECDT752 - INTRODUCTION TO INTERNET OF THINGS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Programming Raspberry Pi Using Python # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Raspberry Pi** is a series of small, single-board computers (SBCs) developed by the Raspberry Pi Foundation in the United Kingdom. It is built on a Broadcom system-on-chip (SoC) with an integrated ARM-compatible central processing unit (CPU), on-board GPU, and a **40-pin General Purpose Input/Output (GPIO)** header that allows direct electrical interfacing with external electronic components. **Programming the Raspberry Pi using Python** refers to the practice of using the high-level, interpreted, general-purpose **Python programming language** as the primary development environment for writing IoT applications. These applications read from sensors (inputs), drive actuators (outputs), communicate over networks (MQTT/HTTP), and implement edge-computing logic by leveraging specialized Python libraries such as **`RPi.GPIO`**, **`gpiozero`**, and **`pigpio`**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mandate:** This topic falls under *Module 4 – IoT Programming & Physical Computing*. The examiner expects students to demonstrate working knowledge of **GPIO pin numbering conventions (BOARD vs BCM)**, **digital I/O**, **PWM control**, and **Python library usage** for prototyping IoT nodes. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine the **Raspberry Pi as the "Brain" of a Robot**: - The **CPU/SoC** is the *thinking center* — it makes decisions. - The **GPIO Pins** are the *robot's hands and skin* — they touch the real world. Some hands **push buttons (Output)** to control LEDs, motors, and buzzers, while other hands **feel the environment (Input)** to detect button presses, temperature, or motion. - **Python** is the *language the brain speaks*. When you tell the brain in Python *"if the button is pressed, turn the LED on"*, the brain translates that into electrical signals on the GPIO pins. > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy:** Think of the Raspberry Pi like a **switches-and-sensors control panel** in a smart home. Python is the **script** that says: *"When motion sensor triggers (Input on Pin 17), turn on the porch light (Output on Pin 27) for 30 seconds, and send an SMS via cloud."* The Pi is the physical hardware that *makes the script touch reality*. --- ## 1.3 Key Technical Metrics & Standards | Metric / Standard | Value / Convention | |---|---| | GPIO Voltage Level | **3.3 V DC** (NOT 5V — damaging!) | | Maximum Current per Pin | **16 mA** (source) / **16 mA** (sink) | | Total Header Current | **50 mA** across all pins | | Default Python GPIO Library | `RPi.GPIO` (legacy) / `gpiozero` (modern) | | Pin Numbering Modes | **BOARD** (physical 1–40) and **BCM** (Broadcom SoC numbers) | | PWM Hardware Channels | Pi 4: **2 hardware PWM** + unlimited software PWM via `pigpio` | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner Trap:** Many students wrongly assume Raspberry Pi GPIO is 5V tolerant like Arduino. **The Pi GPIO is strictly 3.3V.** Applying 5V will permanently damage the SoC. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ## 2.1 Architecture of the Raspberry Pi GPIO Subsystem The Raspberry Pi's GPIO interface is governed by the **BCM2835/BCM2711** SoC (depending on model). From a software perspective, the architecture follows a layered abstraction: 1. **Hardware Layer (Physical Pins):** The 40-pin header exposes power (3.3V, 5V, GND) and a matrix of multifunction GPIO pins. Each pin can be multiplexed as a digital I/O, I²C, SPI, UART, or PWM channel. 2. **Kernel Layer (Linux `/dev/gpiomem`):** A memory-mapped device file grants user-space programs controlled access to the GPIO registers. 3. **Library Layer (Python Wrappers):** - `RPi.GPIO`: Direct register-level access via C extension. Fast but verbose. - `gpiozero`: Object-oriented abstraction built on top of `RPi.GPIO`. Beginner-friendly. **(Recommended by Raspberry Pi Foundation since 2016).** - `pigpio`: Daemon-based, supports remote GPIO and hardware-timed PWM. ## 2.2 Why Python for Raspberry Pi? - **Interpreted & Interactive:** No compile step — ideal for rapid prototyping. - **Rich Library Ecosystem:** `RPi.GPIO`, `gpiozero`, `requests`, `paho-mqtt`, `flask`, `opencv`, `numpy`. - **Cross-Platform Portability:** Code runs on Pi, Linux PC, macOS (with mock GPIO). - **First-Class Support:** Ships pre-installed on **Raspberry Pi OS** (formerly Raspbian). --- ## 2.3 Pin Numbering Conventions — The Critical Concept The Raspberry Pi offers **two pin-numbering modes**, and choosing the wrong one is the **#1 cause of "my LED won't turn on"**: | Mode | Identifier | Description | |---|---|---| | **BOARD** | `GPIO.BOARD` | Numbers pins by **physical position** on the header (1 to 40). Stays constant across Pi models. | | **BCM** | `GPIO.BCM` | Numbers pins by the **Broadcom SoC channel**. Varies between Pi models (e.g., GPIO 18 on Pi 3 vs Pi 4). | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield:** Always declare the mode at the top of your script using `GPIO.setmode(GPIO.BOARD)` or `GPIO.setmode(GPIO.BCM)`. Failing to do so throws a `RuntimeError: No access to /dev/mem`. --- ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — GPIO Programming Essentials | Concept | `RPi.GPIO` Syntax | `gpiozero` Syntax | Purpose | |---|---|---|---| | Mode Setup | `GPIO.setmode(GPIO.BCM)` | *(automatic)* | Pin numbering convention | | Set as Output | `GPIO.setup(17, GPIO.OUT)` | `led = LED(17)` | Configure pin to drive current | | Set as Input | `GPIO.setup(27, GPIO.IN)` | `btn = Button(27)` | Read external voltage level | | Pull-up Resistor | `GPIO.setup(27, GPIO.IN, pull_up_down=GPIO.PUD_UP)` | `Button(27, pull_up=True)` | Internal resistor to keep pin HIGH | | Digital Write HIGH | `GPIO.output(17, GPIO.HIGH)` | `led.on()` | Drive pin to 3.3V | | Digital Write LOW | `GPIO.output(17, GPIO.LOW)` | `led.off()` | Drive pin to 0V | | Digital Read | `val = GPIO.input(27)` | `if btn.is_pressed:` | Sample pin voltage | | PWM Init | `p = GPIO.PWM(18, 1000)` | `pwm = PWMLED(18)` | Pulse Width Modulation | | PWM Start | `p.start(50)` | `pwm.value = 0.5` | 50% duty cycle (half brightness) | | PWM Change | `p.ChangeDutyCycle(75)` | `pwm.value = 0.75` | Adjust brightness/speed | | PWM Stop | `p.stop()` | `pwm.close()` | Halt PWM signal | | Cleanup | `GPIO.cleanup()` | *(context manager)* | Reset pins to safe state | --- ## 2.5 PWM — Pulse Width Modulation on the Pi **PWM** simulates an analog output by rapidly switching a digital pin HIGH and LOW. The **duty cycle** $\delta$ is the percentage of time the signal is HIGH within one period $T$: $$\delta = \frac{T_{HIGH}}{T} \times 100\%$$ $$f_{PWM} = \frac{1}{T} \quad \text{(Hz)}$$ **Engineering Utility:** - **LED Dimming:** $\delta = 0\%$ → OFF, $\delta = 100\%$ → full brightness. - **DC Motor Speed Control:** Average voltage = $V_{CC} \times \delta / 100$. - **Servo Motor Angle Control:** Pulse width between **1 ms (0°)** and **2 ms (180°)** at **50 Hz**. > [!NOTE] > **Hardware vs Software PWM:** The Pi's SoC has only **2 hardware PWM channels** (GPIO 12, 13, 18, 19). For multi-channel PWM (e.g., driving an RGB LED strip or robot arm), use **`pigpio`** which generates timed pulses via DMA. ## 2.6 Real-World IoT Engineering Applications - **Smart Agriculture:** Pi + soil moisture sensor → Python script → MQTT publish to cloud dashboard. - **Home Automation:** Pi + relay module → Flask web server → phone-based light/fan control. - **Edge AI:** Pi + Camera Module + TensorFlow Lite → real-time object detection without cloud. - **Industrial Monitoring:** Pi + Modbus/RS-485 → Python `pymodbus` → SCADA integration. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Code Walkthroughs & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Program 1 — LED Blinking (The "Hello World" of IoT) ### Hardware Connection - LED anode → **330 Ω resistor** → **GPIO 17 (Pin 11)** - LED cathode → **GND (Pin 6)** > [!NOTE] > The **330 Ω resistor** limits current to $\approx 8\,\text{mA}$: $I = (3.3 - 2.0) / 330 \approx 7.9\,\text{mA}$, well within the **16 mA** safe limit. ### Code (using `RPi.GPIO`) ```python import RPi.GPIO as GPIO import time LED_PIN = 17 # BCM number for physical Pin 11 GPIO.setmode(GPIO.BCM) # Step 1: Use Broadcom numbering GPIO.setup(LED_PIN, GPIO.OUT) # Step 2: Configure pin as output try: while True: GPIO.output(LED_PIN, GPIO.HIGH) # Step 3: Drive pin to 3.3V print("LED ON") time.sleep(1) # Wait 1 second GPIO.output(LED_PIN, GPIO.LOW) # Step 4: Drive pin to 0V (GND) print("LED OFF") time.sleep(1) # Wait 1 second except KeyboardInterrupt: GPIO.cleanup() # Reset pins to safe state print("Program stopped safely.") ``` ### Code (using `gpiozero` — Modern, Cleaner) ```python from gpiozero import LED from signal import pause led = LED(17) # Auto-detects pin mode led.blink(on_time=1, off_time=1) # Built-in non-blocking blink pause() # Keep program alive ``` --- ## 3.2 Program 2 — Reading a Push Button (Digital Input) ### Connection - Button one leg → **GPIO 27 (Pin 13)** - Button other leg → **GND (Pin 9)** - Internal pull-up enabled in software (no external resistor needed) ### Code Logic Walkthrough ```python import RPi.GPIO as GPIO import time BUTTON_PIN = 27 GPIO.setmode(GPIO.BCM) GPIO.setup(BUTTON_PIN, GPIO.IN, pull_up_down=GPIO.PUD_UP) # PUD_UP keeps pin HIGH (3.3V) when button is OPEN. # Pressing button connects pin to GND → pin reads LOW. try: while True: input_state = GPIO.input(BUTTON_PIN) if input_state == GPIO.LOW: # Button pressed (active-low) print("Button Pressed") time.sleep(0.2) # Debounce delay (200 ms) else: print("Button Released") time.sleep(0.1) except KeyboardInterrupt: GPIO.cleanup() ``` ### `gpiozero` Equivalent ```python from gpiozero import Button from signal import pause button = Button(27, pull_up=True) def on_press(): print("Button Pressed") def on_release(): print("Button Released") button.when_pressed = on_press button.when_released = on_release pause() ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Debounce Explanation:** Mechanical push buttons "bounce" — the contacts physically vibrate for ~5–20 ms producing multiple rapid ON/OFF signals. A software delay (`time.sleep(0.05)`) or a capacitor across the button smooths this. KTU expects students to mention debounce. --- ## 3.3 Program 3 — PWM LED Brightness Control ### Code ```python import RPi.GPIO as GPIO import time PWM_PIN = 18 # Hardware PWM-capable pin GPIO.setmode(GPIO.BCM) GPIO.setup(PWM_PIN, GPIO.OUT) pwm = GPIO.PWM(PWM_PIN, 1000) # 1000 Hz frequency pwm.start(0) # Start at 0% duty cycle (LED off) try: while True: for duty in range(0, 101, 5): # 0% → 100% in steps of 5 pwm.ChangeDutyCycle(duty) print(f"Dim level: {duty}%") time.sleep(0.1) for duty in range(100, -1, -5): # 100% → 0% (fade out) pwm.ChangeDutyCycle(duty) time.sleep(0.1) except KeyboardInterrupt: pwm.stop() GPIO.cleanup() ``` ### Analytical Matrix — Brightness vs Duty Cycle The **average voltage** $V_{avg}$ across the LED is: $$V_{avg} = \delta \times V_{CC} = \delta \times 3.3\,\text{V}$$ | Duty Cycle $\delta$ | $V_{avg}$ | LED Brightness | |---|---|---| | 0% | 0.0 V | OFF | | 25% | 0.825 V | Dim | | 50% | 1.65 V | Medium | | 75% | 2.475 V | Bright | | 100% | 3.30 V | Full | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Insight:** The human eye responds logarithmically to light. A 50% duty cycle does **not** appear half as bright — it appears closer to 75% of perceived brightness. This is **Weber-Fechner Law** in action. --- ## 3.4 Program 4 — Interfacing a DHT11 Temperature & Humidity Sensor The **DHT11** is a single-wire digital sensor. Python uses the `Adafruit_DHT` library. ```python import Adafruit_DHT import time SENSOR = Adafruit_DHT.DHT11 GPIO_PIN = 4 while True: humidity, temperature = Adafruit_DHT.read_retry(SENSOR, GPIO_PIN) if humidity is not None and temperature is not None: print(f"Temp={temperature:0.1f}°C Humidity={humidity:0.1f}%") else: print("Sensor read failure. Check wiring.") time.sleep(2) ``` --- ## 3.5 Program 5 — Publishing Sensor Data to MQTT Broker (IoT Cloud Link) ```python import paho.mqtt.client as mqtt import Adafruit_DHT import time import json BROKER = "broker.hivemq.com" PORT = 1883 TOPIC = "ktu/iot/room1" SENSOR = Adafruit_DHT.DHT11 PIN = 4 client = mqtt.Client() client.connect(BROKER, PORT, 60) while True: h, t = Adafruit_DHT.read_retry(SENSOR, PIN) payload = json.dumps({"temp_C": t, "humidity": h}) client.publish(TOPIC, payload) print("Published:", payload) time.sleep(5) ``` --- ## 3.6 Program 6 — Flask Web Server to Control an LED (Web-of-Things) ```python from flask import Flask, render_template_string import RPi.GPIO as GPIO app = Flask(__name__) LED_PIN = 17 GPIO.setmode(GPIO.BCM) GPIO.setup(LED_PIN, GPIO.OUT) HTML = """

KTU IoT - LED Control

Status: {{status}}

""" @app.route('/') def index(): return render_template_string(HTML, status=read_state()) @app.route('/on') def on(): GPIO.output(LED_PIN, GPIO.HIGH) return index() @app.route('/off') def off(): GPIO.output(LED_PIN, GPIO.LOW) return index() def read_state(): return "ON" if GPIO.input(LED_PIN) else "OFF" if __name__ == '__main__': app.run(host='0.0.0.0', port=5000) ``` Access from any browser at `http://:5000/`. --- ## 3.7 Program 7 — Ultrasonic Distance Sensor (HC-SR04) The HC-SR04 uses **trigger** and **echo** pins. Distance is computed from the echo pulse width. ```python import RPi.GPIO as GPIO import time TRIG = 23 ECHO = 24 GPIO.setmode(GPIO.BCM) GPIO.setup(TRIG, GPIO.OUT) GPIO.setup(ECHO, GPIO.IN) def get_distance_cm(): GPIO.output(TRIG, GPIO.LOW) time.sleep(0.000002) GPIO.output(TRIG, GPIO.HIGH) time.sleep(0.00001) # 10 µs trigger pulse GPIO.output(TRIG, GPIO.LOW) while GPIO.input(ECHO) == 0: pulse_start = time.time() while GPIO.input(ECHO) == 1: pulse_end = time.time() pulse_duration = pulse_end - pulse_start distance = pulse_duration * 17150 # 343 m/s / 2 round-trip return round(distance, 2) try: while True: d = get_distance_cm() print(f"Distance: {d} cm") time.sleep(1) except KeyboardInterrupt: GPIO.cleanup() ``` ### Derivation of the Distance Formula $$d = \frac{v_{sound} \times t_{echo}}{2}$$ $$d = \frac{343\,\text{m/s} \times t_{echo}}{2} = 171.5 \times t_{echo}\,\text{(meters)}$$ $$d_{cm} = 17150 \times t_{echo}\,\text{(centimeters)}$$ > [!NOTE] > The division by **2** accounts for the **round-trip travel** of the ultrasonic wave: the pulse travels to the object **and** back to the sensor. Speed of sound $v_{sound} = 343\,\text{m/s}$ at $20\,°C$. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Raspberry Pi GPIO Header Pinout (Top 20 Pins) ```mermaid flowchart TB pwr3v3[3V3 Power Pin 1 17]:::pwr pwr5v[5V Power Pin 2 18]:::pwr gnd1[GND Pin 6 9 14 20]:::gnd gpio2[GPIO 2 SDA1 Pin 3]:::io gpio3[GPIO 3 SCL1 Pin 5]:::io gpio4[GPIO 4 Pin 7]:::io gpio17[GPIO 17 Pin 11]:::io gpio27[GPIO 27 Pin 13]:::io gpio22[GPIO 22 Pin 15]:::io gpio10[GPIO 10 Pin 19]:::io gpio9[GPIO 9 Pin 21]:::io gpio11[GPIO 11 Pin 23]:::io pwm18[GPIO 18 PWM0 Pin 12]:::pwm classDef pwr fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#900,color:#fff classDef gnd fill:#2d2d2d,stroke:#000,color:#fff classDef io fill:#4d96ff,stroke:#003,color:#fff classDef pwm fill:#ffd93d,stroke:#a80,color:#000 ``` --- ##
