```mermaid
        g2[ui gauge Air Temperature]
        g3[ui gauge Humidity]
        g4[ui chart 24h Trend Line]
    end

    subgraph tabControl["TAB 2: MANUAL CONTROL"]
        sw1[ui switch Pump Override]
        sl1[ui slider Moisture Threshold]
        fm1[ui form Update Crop Profile]
    end

    subgraph tabAlerts["TAB 3: ALERTS LOG"]
        txt1[ui text Recent Alerts List]
        ch1[ui chart Alert Frequency]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> This three-tab dashboard layout is the **industry-standard reference design** for KTU case study questions. Examiners expect students to mention at least **two tabs** with named widgets (gauge, chart, switch, slider) for full marks.

---

## 4.5 Decision Tree: Smart Irrigation Control

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([New MQTT Message Arrives]) --> parse[Parse JSON Payload]
    parse --> checkSoil{Soil VWC less than 35 percent}
    checkSoil -- No --> noIrrig[Set pump OFF]
    checkSoil -- Yes --> checkTemp{Air Temp less than 35 C}
    checkTemp -- No --> noIrrig
    checkTemp -- Yes --> checkRain{Humidity less than 85 percent}
    checkRain -- No --> noIrrig
    checkRain -- Yes --> irrigate[Set pump ON and Send Alert]
    noIrrig --> log[Update Dashboard and Persist]
    irrigate --> log
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Node-RED and list any four node categories used in IoT application development.
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 (Expected Pattern)]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Node-RED** is an open-source, flow-based visual programming tool built on Node.js, used for wiring hardware devices, APIs, and online services in IoT applications.

Four node categories:

1. **Input Nodes** — e.g., `mqtt in`, `http in`, `inject` (for sensor data ingestion).
2. **Output Nodes** — e.g., `mqtt out`, `debug`, `e-mail` (for actuation/alerting).
3. **Function Nodes** — e.g., `function`, `switch`, `change` (for processing and decision logic).
4. **Dashboard Nodes** — e.g., `ui_gauge`, `ui_chart`, `ui_switch` (for visualization).

*(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for correctly listing 4 categories with examples.)*

---

### Question 2: What is the role of an MQTT broker in a Node-RED-based agriculture system?
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 (Expected Pattern)]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> The **MQTT broker** (e.g., Mosquitto, HiveMQ) acts as a **central message router** in the publish-subscribe architecture. In an agriculture system:
> - ESP32 sensor nodes **publish** soil/temperature data to topics like `farm/zone1/soil`.
> - Node-RED **subscribes** to these topics using `mqtt in` nodes.
> - The broker **decouples** publishers from subscribers, enabling **asynchronous**, **scalable**, and **bandwidth-efficient** communication, which is critical for low-power field sensors.

*(1 mark for broker definition + 1 mark for pub-sub mechanism + 1 mark for agriculture relevance.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A: Case Study on Smart Irrigation using Node-RED

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 (Expected 14-Mark Pattern)]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Design a complete Node-RED flow for smart irrigation of a tomato greenhouse. Include sensor selection, MQTT topic structure, decision logic, and dashboard widgets.**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Sensor Selection (1.5 marks):**

| Sensor | Parameter Measured | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| **DHT22** | Air temperature, humidity | High accuracy (±0.5°C), digital output |
| **Capacitive Soil Sensor v1.2** | Soil moisture | Corrosion-resistant, analog output |
| **LDR (GL5528)** | Light intensity (PAR proxy) | Low-cost, sufficient for day/night detection |
| **Rain Gauge (Hydreon RG-11)** | Rainfall detection | Optical, no moving parts |

**2. MQTT Topic Hierarchy (1.5 marks):**

Following KTU/IBM IoT convention:

```
farm/<farm-id>/<zone-id>/<sensor-type>
```

Example:

```
farm/farm001/zoneA/soil
farm/farm001/zoneA/temp
farm/farm001/zoneA/humidity
farm/farm001/zoneA/light
```

**3. Decision Logic (2 marks):**

Implemented in a `function` node (as derived in Section 3.1):

```javascript
var vwc = msg.payload.vwc;
var temp = msg.payload.temp;
var rh = msg.payload.humidity;

if (vwc < 35 && temp < 35 && rh < 85) {
    msg.payload = "ON";
    msg.topic = "actuator/pump/zoneA";
    return [msg, null];     // output 1: pump trigger
} else {
    msg.payload = "OFF";
    return [null, msg];     // output 2: status update
}
```

**4. Dashboard Widgets (2 marks):**

- **Tab 1 - Live Monitoring**: `ui_gauge` for soil moisture (color zones: red<30%, yellow 30–60%, green>60%), `ui_chart` for 24-hour trend line, `ui_text` for last update timestamp.
- **Tab 2 - Manual Control**: `ui_switch` for pump override, `ui_slider` to dynamically adjust the moisture threshold (range 20–80%).
- **Tab 3 - Alerts Log**: `ui_text` node listing recent alerts and `ui_template` node for HTML-formatted history.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Explain how data persistence and alerting mechanisms are integrated into the above Node-RED flow. Discuss at least one storage backend and one alert channel.**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Data Persistence (3.5 marks):**

**Backend Selection — InfluxDB (Time-Series Database):**

InfluxDB is chosen because sensor data is inherently time-stamped and continuous. The `influxdb out` node is configured with:

- **Measurement name**: `soil_moisture`, `air_temperature`
- **Tag**: `zone = "zoneA"`, `crop = "tomato"`
- **Field**: `value = <numeric reading>`
- **Timestamp**: auto-generated from `msg.payload.timestamp` or current time

Sample Line Protocol payload generated by Node-RED:

```
soil_moisture,zone=zoneA,crop=tomato value=47.83 1714567200000000000
```

This data is later queried using **Flux** or **Grafana** for trend analysis and predictive modeling.

**Alternative Backends**:
- **MySQL** for relational storage (suitable when joining with farmer records).
- **CSV file** via `file` node for low-cost offline deployments.

**2. Alerting Mechanism (3.5 marks):**

**Email Alerts via `e-mail` Node:**

The Node-RED `e-mail` node is configured with SMTP credentials and triggers when:

$$
\text{VWC} < 20\% \;\;\text{for duration} > 30 \text{ minutes}
$$

This requires a **persistent context** (`flow.set` / `context.set`) to track the timestamp when VWC first dropped below 20%. On every new message:

```javascript
if (vwc < 20) {
    if (!flow.get("dry_since")) flow.set("dry_since", Date.now());
    var elapsed = Date.now() - flow.get("dry_since");
    if (elapsed > 30 * 60 * 1000) {
        msg.payload = "CRITICAL: Soil moisture below 20% for 30 minutes!";
        msg.topic = "ALERT: Greenhouse ZoneA Dry";
        return msg;
    }
} else {
    flow.set("dry_since", null);
    return null;
}
```

**Alternative Alert Channels**:
- **Telegram Bot** via `node-red-contrib-telegrambot` — sends instant mobile push.
- **Twilio SMS** — for farmers without internet but with cellular coverage.
- **Webhook → IFTTT** — for cross-platform smart home integration.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students commonly lose 2–3 marks by **not specifying the threshold values**, **MQTT topic structure**, or **explaining the difference between the two output branches of the `switch` node**. Always:
> - Draw the **flow with arrows and labels** (even if rough).
> - Mention **at least one numeric threshold** for the chosen crop.
> - Justify the choice of storage backend in **one sentence**.
> - Show **one line of JavaScript** inside the function node.

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice): Node-RED Architecture and Dashboard Design

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 (Expected 14-Mark Pattern)]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of Node-RED. Discuss its runtime components and message-passing model.**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Architecture (3 marks):**

Node-RED follows a **client-server architecture** with the following layers:

- **Client Layer**: Browser-based flow editor (HTML5 + D3.js).
- **Server Layer**: Node.js runtime hosting the flow editor backend and runtime engine.
- **Node Module Layer**: npm packages providing input/output/function nodes.
- **Message Bus**: An internal event-driven bus carrying `msg` objects.

**2. Runtime Components (2 marks):**

- **Flows.json** — JSON file storing all deployed flows.
- **Credentials File** — Encrypted storage for API keys.
- **Registry** — npm-based installation directory for nodes.
- **Settings.js** — Configuration for port (default 1880), auth, logging.

**3. Message-Passing Model (2 marks):**

Every node receives an incoming `msg` object, processes it (may modify `msg.payload` or properties), and emits zero, one, or more `msg` objects to downstream nodes. This is analogous to a **functional pipeline** with single-direction data flow and **non-blocking asynchronous execution**.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Design a Node-RED dashboard for monitoring a poly-house (multi-zone greenhouse) growing tomatoes, capsicum, and lettuce. Each zone should have independent threshold control.**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Tab Structure (2 marks):**

| Tab | Purpose |
|---|---|
| Tab 1: Overview | Summary gauges for all 3 zones |
| Tab 2: Tomato Zone | Crop-specific widgets + thresholds |
| Tab 3: Capsicum Zone | Crop-specific widgets + thresholds |
| Tab 4: Lettuce Zone | Crop-specific widgets + thresholds |
| Tab 5: System Config | Global settings, user management |

**2. Per-Zone Widget Set (3 marks):**

- **Group: Sensors** — Three `ui_gauge` widgets for soil moisture, temperature, humidity.
- **Group: Trends** — `ui_chart` showing 7-day trend.
- **Group: Actuators** — `ui_switch` for manual pump, `ui_led` for pump status indicator.
- **Group: Thresholds** — `ui_slider` (range 20–80%) to set VWC threshold dynamically, persisted via `flow.set`.

**3. Multi-Zone Independent Control Logic (2 marks):**

Each zone has its own flow tab within Node-RED, sharing a **common function node library** but with **separate `flow.context` variables**:

```javascript
// function node per zone
var zone = "tomato";
var threshold = flow.get(zone + "_threshold") || 35;   // default
if (msg.payload.vwc < threshold) {
    msg.payload = "ON";
    msg.topic = "actuator/pump/" + zone;
    return msg;
}
```

This modular approach allows the farmer to grow **three crops with different water needs** in one Node-RED instance, demonstrating **scalability** — a key KTU evaluation criterion.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU University Exam:**

- ✅ **Node-RED = Flow-based visual programming tool built on Node.js**, used for IoT prototyping.
- ✅ Default port: **1880** for editor, **1883** for MQTT broker.
- ✅ Core building blocks: **Input → Function → Output** nodes connected by **wires**.
- ✅ Every node communicates via the `msg` object; **`msg.payload` is the data contract**.
- ✅ MQTT uses **publish-subscribe** model with topic hierarchy like `farm/zone1/soil`.
- ✅ **DHT22** for temperature/humidity, **capacitive sensor** for soil moisture — never use resistive sensors long-term in agriculture (corrosion).
- ✅ Three-node decision flow for irrigation: **Soil Check → Temp Check → Humidity Check**.
- ✅ Threshold formula:
  $$
  I_{\text{trigger}} = (\text{VWC} < 35\%) \land (T < 35°C) \land (RH < 85\%)
  $$
- ✅ **InfluxDB** for time-series storage, **Grafana** for analytics dashboard, **Telegram/email** for alerts.
- ✅ **VWC Calculation**:
  $$
  \text{VWC}_{\%} = 100 \times \frac{V_{\text{dry}} - V_{\text{read}}}{V_{\text{dry}} - V_{\text{wet}}}
  $$
- ✅ **Dashboard tabs**: Live Monitoring / Manual Control / Alerts Log — examiners expect **at least 2 tabs** in 14-mark answers.
- ✅ **Common Pitfall**: Always mention **crop-specific thresholds** — generic "set threshold" answers lose marks.
- ✅ **Modular Flow Design**: One `function` node per zone with `flow.context` variables for independent control.
- ✅ Real-world impact: Smart irrigation saves **30–50% water**, increases yield **15–25%** (cite FAO in descriptive answers).
- ✅ Node-RED can run on **Raspberry Pi (edge)** or **cloud (IBM Cloud, AWS)** — examiners often ask about deployment.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: IoT in Smart City
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT752 - INTRODUCTION TO INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** IoT in Smart City

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Smart City (KTU Definition):** A Smart City is an urban ecosystem that leverages **Information and Communication Technologies (ICT)**, **Internet of Things (IoT)** sensor networks, **cloud-edge computing**, and **data analytics** to manage municipal assets, infrastructure, and services efficiently — with the primary objective of improving **quality of life (QoL)**, **operational efficiency**, and **environmental sustainability**.

In the KTU 2024 framework, an **IoT-enabled Smart City** is defined as a **cyber-physical urban system** in which heterogeneous physical objects (streetlights, traffic signals, water meters, garbage bins, parking slots, buildings, and vehicles) are instrumented with sensors/actuators and interconnected through wired/wireless communication networks to enable **real-time data acquisition**, **context-aware decision making**, and **closed-loop automation** across the urban value chain.

### 1.2 Core Smart City Verticals (KTU Syllabus Mapping)

| # | Vertical | Primary Function |
|---|----------|------------------|
| 1 | Smart Transportation & Traffic | Adaptive traffic control, intelligent parking, fleet tracking |
| 2 | Smart Energy / Smart Grid | Two-way metering, dynamic load balancing, renewable integration |
| 3 | Smart Water Management | AMR (Automatic Meter Reading), leakage detection, quality monitoring |
| 4 | Smart Waste Management | Fill-level sensing, dynamic route optimization |
| 5 | Smart Lighting | Daylight-responsive dimming, fault detection |
| 6 | Smart Buildings | HVAC automation, occupancy-driven energy control |
| 7 | Environmental Monitoring | AQI, noise, temperature, humidity sensing |
| 8 | Smart Governance & Citizen Services | E-governance, citizen grievance IoT portals |
| 9 | Smart Healthcare | Remote patient monitoring, ambulance tracking |
| 10 | Public Safety & Surveillance | Smart policing, gunshot detection, crowd analytics |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The City as a Living Organism":**
> Imagine a human body. The **sensors** are like **nerve endings** scattered across the body (skin) detecting touch, temperature, and pain. The **communication network** is the **nervous system** that carries signals to the **brain** (cloud data center). The **brain** processes the signals and sends instructions back through the same nervous system to **muscles** (actuators) which perform actions. A Smart City works exactly like this — distributed sensors collect data from every urban asset, transmit it through high-speed networks to centralized and edge computing platforms, and automated responses are sent back to actuators that adjust city operations in real time.

### 1.4 Key Performance Metrics (Mandatory Bold Highlighting)

The success of any IoT-driven smart city deployment is quantified using standard KPIs:

- **Latency:** ≤ **10 ms** for autonomous traffic control, ≤ **1 s** for utility monitoring
- **Device Density:** up to **1,000,000 devices/km²** (5G mMTC scenario)
- **Network Availability:** ≥ **99.999%** (five nines) for safety-critical services
- **Data Freshness:** ≤ **5 minutes** for environmental monitoring loops
- **Energy Efficiency:** ≤ **1 µJ/bit** for massive-scale LPWAN sensor nodes

### 1.5 Why IoT is the Backbone of Smart Cities

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A smart city **cannot exist** without IoT. The four pillars of urban intelligence — *Sense*, *Communicate*, *Analyze*, *Actuate* — are precisely the four functional layers of any IoT system. Therefore, **IoT is not a feature of smart cities; IoT is the foundational substrate** that converts passive concrete-and-steel infrastructure into an intelligent, responsive, and self-optimizing urban organism.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five-Layer IoT Reference Architecture for Smart Cities (KTU Mandatory)

The KTU 2024 syllabus adopts the **five-layer IoT architecture** mapped to urban deployments:

#### **Layer 1 — Perception / Sensing Layer (Device Layer)**
- **Role:** Physical data acquisition from urban infrastructure.
- **Components:** Temperature/humidity sensors (DHT22), gas sensors (MQ-135), ultrasonic fill-level sensors, inductive loops, piezoelectric traffic counters, smart meters, IP cameras, RFID tags, GPS modules.
- **Output:** Raw analog/digital signals → Microcontroller (Arduino, ESP32, STM32, Raspberry Pi).

#### **Layer 2 — Network / Communication Layer (Transmission Layer)**
- **Role:** Reliable data transport between field devices and processing infrastructure.
- **Technologies:**
  * **Short-range:** Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11), Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE), Zigbee (IEEE 802.15.4), RFID/NFC.
  * **Long-range LPWAN:** LoRaWAN, NB-IoT (LTE Cat-NB1/NB2), Sigfox.
  * **Cellular:** 4G LTE, 5G (eMBB, URLLC, mMTC).
  * **Wired backhaul:** Fiber-optic, Ethernet, PLC (Power Line Communication) for smart grid.

#### **Layer 3 — Middleware / Edge Computing Layer (Gateway Layer)**
- **Role:** Protocol translation (Modbus → MQTT), data aggregation, local analytics, security enforcement.
- **Components:** IoT gateways (e.g., Cisco IoT Gateway, AWS Greengrass, Azure IoT Edge), edge servers, fog nodes.
- **Function:** Reduces **bandwidth** by **80–90%** through local filtering and pre-processing.

#### **Layer 4 — Application / Service Layer**
- **Role:** Domain-specific smart-city services delivered to end-users via dashboards, mobile apps, or APIs.
- **Examples:** Traffic control software, SCADA dashboards for water utilities, citizen mobile apps for complaint lodging, billing portals for utilities.

#### **Layer 5 — Business / Management Layer**
- **Role:** Strategic decision-making based on aggregated KPIs, SLA monitoring, ROI tracking, policy enforcement.
- **Stakeholders:** Municipal corporation, urban planners, utility companies, citizens.

### 2.2 Key IoT Communication Protocols Used in Smart Cities

| Protocol | Transport | Header Size | Suitability in Smart City |
|----------|-----------|-------------|---------------------------|
| **MQTT** | TCP | 2 bytes | Lightweight pub/sub for telemetry (water, gas meters) |
| **CoAP** | UDP | 4 bytes | RESTful constrained-node communication |
| **HTTP/HTTPS** | TCP | ~200 bytes | Citizen-facing dashboards, firmware updates (OTA) |
| **AMQP** | TCP | 8 bytes | Enterprise-grade reliable messaging (grid control) |
| **DDS** | UDP/TCP | Variable | Real-time high-performance (autonomous traffic) |
| **Modbus RTU/TCP** | Serial/TCP | 8 bytes | Legacy SCADA integration (water treatment plants) |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet — Smart City IoT Engineering

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation Convention:** All symbols use SI units. Use $\vert x \vert$ (escaped) only when rendering absolute value inside table cells to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. All below formulas are valid in-line using `$ ... $` or display-mode using `$$ ... $$`.

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Engineering Utility |
|---|-------------------|---------------|---------------------|
| 1 | Total Smart-City Data Throughput | $D_{total} = N \cdot S \cdot f_s$ | N = nodes, S = sample size (bits), $f_s$ = sampling rate (Hz) |
| 2 | Shannon Channel Capacity | $C = B \cdot \log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right)$ | B = bandwidth (Hz), S/N = signal-to-noise ratio |
| 3 | Gateway Capacity (Max Served Nodes) | $N_{max} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{B \cdot \eta}{R_{per\_node}}\right\rfloor$ | $\eta$ = spectral efficiency factor (0.6–0.8) |
| 4 | Total Energy Budget per Node | $E_{total} = E_{sense} + E_{comm} + E_{proc} + E_{sleep}$ | Drives battery-life estimation |
| 5 | Battery Lifetime | $T_{bat} = \dfrac{C_{bat} \cdot V_{nom}}{I_{avg}}$ | $C_{bat}$ = capacity (Ah), $I_{avg}$ = average current |
| 6 | Network Coverage Area (Cellular) | $A_{cov} = \pi \cdot r^2 \cdot (1 - \delta_{overlap})$ | $\delta_{overlap}$ = overlap factor |
| 7 | End-to-End Latency | $L_{e2e} = t_{prop} + t_{trans} + t_{queue} + t_{proc} + t_{act}$ | Must satisfy application SLA |
| 8 | ROI of Smart-City Project | $ROI = \dfrac{(B_{total} - C_{total})}{C_{total}} \cdot 100\%$ | $B$ = benefits, $C$ = costs (5-yr horizon) |
| 9 | Air Quality Index (linear interpolation) | $I_{p} = \dfrac{I_{Hi} - I_{Lo}}{BP_{Hi} - BP_{Lo}} \cdot (C_{p} - BP_{Lo}) + I_{Lo}$ | Standard EPA breakpoint formula |
| 10 | Packet Delivery Ratio (PDR) | $PDR = \dfrac{P_{rx}}{P_{tx}} \cdot 100\%$ | Network reliability KPI |
| 11 | Smart Grid Load Forecast (Linear Regression) | $L(t) = \alpha + \beta \cdot T(t) + \gamma \cdot H(t)$ | $\alpha,\beta,\gamma$ = trained coefficients |
| 12 | Cost per Citizen per Year | $C_{capita} = \dfrac{C_{infra} + C_{ops}}{P_{pop}}$ | Used in municipal budget planning |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smart Transportation:** Adaptive traffic signals using **V2I (Vehicle-to-Infrastructure)** communication reduce average commute time by **15–25%**.
- **Smart Grid:** IoT-enabled AMI (Advanced Metering Infrastructure) reduces peak load by **10–15%** through dynamic pricing.
- **Smart Water:** Acoustic + flow sensors detect pipe leaks in real time, reducing **NRW (Non-Revenue Water)** from 30% to below 10%.
- **Smart Waste:** Fill-level sensors + route optimization algorithms reduce fuel consumption of waste-collection fleets by **~40%**.
- **Environmental Monitoring:** Distributed AQI stations enable hyper-local pollution forecasts with **<500 m** spatial resolution.

### 2.5 Logical Operational Flow (Why & How)

- **Why** is the Perception layer always distributed? → Because urban phenomena (traffic, pollution) are spatially heterogeneous; centralized sensing loses granularity.
- **How** does Edge computing reduce cloud cost? → By filtering out **80–90% redundant events** at the gateway before they reach the cloud, it slashes bandwidth bills and storage costs.
- **Why** is **MQTT preferred over HTTP** for sensor telemetry? → Because MQTT's **2-byte fixed header** (vs. HTTP's ~200 bytes overhead) makes it **100× more efficient** for low-bandwidth LPWAN smart-city deployments.
- **How** does the Business layer drive ROI? → By converting raw KPIs (e.g., "average commute reduced by 20%") into monetizable metrics (fuel saved, productivity gained, emissions reduced).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Smart-City Data Throughput Sizing

**Problem Statement (KTU-Numerical Type):**
A smart-city pilot deploys **N = 5,000 air-quality sensor nodes**. Each node samples $\text{PM}_{2.5}$, $\text{PM}_{10}$, $\text{NO}_2$, $\text{SO}_2$, temperature, and humidity (6 parameters). Each sample is encoded in **16 bits**. Sampling rate is **1 sample/minute**. Find the total daily data volume generated.

**Step 1 — Identify given parameters.**

$$N = 5000,\quad k = 6\ \text{parameters},\quad b = 16\ \text{bits/sample},\quad f_s = \dfrac{1}{60}\ \text{Hz}$$

**Step 2 — Per-node data rate.**

Bits per sample per node:

$$S_{node} = k \cdot b = 6 \cdot 16 = 96\ \text{bits/sample}$$

Per-node data rate:

$$R_{node} = S_{node} \cdot f_s = 96 \cdot \dfrac{1}{60} = 1.6\ \text{bits/second}$$

**Step 3 — Total aggregated throughput.**

$$R_{total} = N \cdot R_{node} = 5000 \cdot 1.6 = 8000\ \text{bps} = 8\ \text{kbps}$$

**Step 4 — Daily data volume.**

$$V_{day} = R_{total} \cdot 86400 = 8000 \cdot 86400 = 691{,}200{,}000\ \text{bits/day}$$

Convert to megabytes (1 MB = 8 × 10⁶ bits):

$$V_{day} = \dfrac{691{,}200{,}000}{8 \cdot 10^6} \approx 86.4\ \text{MB/day}$$

**Final Answer:** The smart-city pilot generates approximately **8 kbps** aggregate throughput and **86.4 MB** of data per day.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Battery Lifetime of a Smart-City Sensor Node

**Problem Statement:**
A streetlight controller node uses a battery rated at **C_bat = 10 Ah** at **V_nom = 3.7 V**. The current profile is: sensing draws **30 mA for 1 s every 60 s**, LoRaWAN transmission draws **120 mA for 0.5 s every 60 s**, processing draws **40 mA for 0.5 s every 60 s**, and sleep mode draws **0.05 mA continuously**. Find the **battery lifetime in years**.

**Step 1 — Compute average current for each activity over 60-second cycle.**

$$I_{sense,avg} = 30 \cdot \dfrac{1}{60} = 0.500\ \text{mA}$$

$$I_{tx,avg} = 120 \cdot \dfrac{0.5}{60} = 1.000\ \text{mA}$$

$$I_{proc,avg} = 40 \cdot \dfrac{0.5}{60} = 0.333\ \text{mA}$$

$$I_{sleep,avg} = 0.050\ \text{mA}\ (\text{continuous})$$

**Step 2 — Total average current.**

$$I_{avg} = 0.500 + 1.000 + 0.333 + 0.050 = 1.883\ \text{mA}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the battery lifetime formula.**

$$T_{bat} = \dfrac{C_{bat}}{I_{avg}} = \dfrac{10000\ \text{mAh}}{1.883\ \text{mA}} \approx 5310.7\ \text{hours}$$

Convert to years (1 year ≈ 8760 hours):

$$T_{bat} = \dfrac{5310.7}{8760} \approx 0.606\ \text{years} \approx 7.3\ \text{months}$$

**Final Answer:** The node's battery lasts approximately **0.606 years (≈ 7.3 months)**. Engineering takeaway: duty-cycle optimization or energy harvesting (solar) is mandatory for outdoor deployments.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Smart-City ROI Calculation (Justifying Project Approval)

**Problem Statement:**
A municipal corporation proposes an IoT-enabled smart-streetlight project with **10,000 LED streetlights** retrofitted with motion + daylight sensors. Total capital expenditure is **₹50 crore**, and annual O&M is **₹4 crore/year**. Annual energy savings = **₹14 crore/year**, reduced maintenance = **₹2 crore/year**, reduced crime (societal value) = **₹1 crore/year**. Compute the **5-year ROI**.

**Step 1 — Total 5-year costs.**

$$C_{total} = C_{capex} + 5 \cdot C_{o\&m} = 50 + 5 \cdot 4 = 50 + 20 = ₹70\ \text{crore}$$

**Step 2 — Total 5-year benefits.**

$$B_{total} = 5 \cdot (14 + 2 + 1) = 5 \cdot 17 = ₹85\ \text{crore}$$

**Step 3 — Net benefit.**

$$\Delta = B_{total} - C_{total} = 85 - 70 = ₹15\ \text{crore}$$

**Step 4 — ROI percentage.**

$$ROI = \dfrac{\Delta}{C_{total}} \cdot 100\% = \dfrac{15}{70} \cdot 100\% \approx 21.43\%$$

**Final Answer:** A positive **ROI ≈ 21.43%** over 5 years justifies the deployment; payback period ≈ 4.12 years.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Gateway Capacity (How many sensors per gateway?)

**Problem Statement:**
A LoRaWAN gateway operates at bandwidth **B = 125 kHz**, spectral efficiency factor **η = 0.7**, and each sensor requires a sustained **R = 250 bps**. What is the maximum number of sensors that can be served per gateway?

**Step 1 — Effective usable bit rate.**

$$C_{gw} = B \cdot \eta = 125{,}000 \cdot 0.7 = 87{,}500\ \text{bps}$$

**Step 2 — Maximum node count.**

$$N_{max} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{C_{gw}}{R}\right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \dfrac{87{,}500}{250}\right\rfloor = 350\ \text{nodes}$$

**Final Answer:** A single LoRaWAN gateway can service up to **350 sensors** at sustained load — guiding city planners on **gateway density planning**.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Major Smart City Vertical Deployments

| Vertical | Primary Sensor | Communication Tech | Latency Target | Typical Data Frequency | Annual Saving (Est.) |
|----------|---------------|---------------------|----------------|------------------------|----------------------|
| Smart Streetlighting | PIR + LDR | LoRaWAN / NB-IoT | < 5 s | Every change / 5 min | 40–60% energy |
| Smart Waste Management | Ultrasonic fill-level | NB-IoT | < 60 s | Every 30 min | 30–50% fuel |
| Smart Parking | Magnetic / ultrasonic | LoRaWAN / Zigbee | < 10 s | Every 30 s | 20–30% time saved |
| Smart Water (AMR) | Ultrasonic flow | NB-IoT | < 60 s | Hourly | 10–25% NRW reduction |
| Smart Grid (AMI) | Smart meter | PLC / RF mesh | < 1 s | 15 min | 5–15% peak load cut |
| Traffic Adaptive Control | Inductive loop / camera + AI | Fiber / 5G | < 100 ms | Real-time | 15–25% commute reduction |
| Air Quality Monitoring | Electrochemical gas sensor | LoRaWAN / 4G | < 60 s | Every 1–15 min | Health-cost reduction |
| Smart Buildings (HVAC) | Temp + CO₂ + occupancy | Wi-Fi / Zigbee / BLE | < 1 s | Every 1 min | 20–30% HVAC energy |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram 1 — Five-Layer Smart City IoT Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1["PERCEPTION LAYER: Air Quality Sensors"]
    P2["PERCEPTION LAYER: Smart Meters"]
    P3["PERCEPTION LAYER: Traffic Cameras"]
    P4["PERCEPTION LAYER: Fill Level Sensors"]
    P5["PERCEPTION LAYER: PIR Streetlight Sensors"]

    N1["NETWORK LAYER: LoRaWAN"]
    N2["NETWORK LAYER: NB-IoT"]
    N3["NETWORK LAYER: Wi-Fi and 5G"]
    N4["NETWORK LAYER: Fiber Backhaul"]

    M1["MIDDLEWARE LAYER: Edge Gateway with MQTT Broker"]
    M2["MIDDLEWARE LAYER: Local Analytics and Filtering"]
    M3["MIDDLEWARE LAYER: Security and Auth"]

    A1["APPLICATION LAYER: Traffic Control Dashboard"]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**  
*Define a Smart City. List any **four** IoT-enabled verticals of a smart city and state **one** sensor used in each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** A Smart City is an urban area that uses ICT and IoT-based sensor networks, integrated with cloud/edge computing and data analytics, to efficiently manage infrastructure and services, thereby improving citizens' quality of life and sustainability.

**Verticals (2 marks — 0.5 each):**

| Vertical | Sensor Used |
|----------|-------------|
| Smart Transportation | Inductive-loop vehicle detector |
| Smart Waste Management | Ultrasonic fill-level sensor |
| Smart Streetlighting | PIR motion sensor + LDR |
| Smart Water (AMR) | Ultrasonic flow meter |
| Air Quality Monitoring | Electrochemical NO₂/SO₂ sensor |

*(Student may write any four correctly paired vertical-sensor combinations.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]**  
*Explain the role of the **Edge Computing layer** in a Smart City IoT architecture. Why is it preferred over sending raw sensor data directly to the cloud?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Role of Edge Layer (1.5 marks):** The edge computing layer, situated between field sensors and the cloud, performs **local data aggregation, filtering, preprocessing, and protocol translation** (e.g., Modbus → MQTT). It hosts IoT gateways with embedded analytics capabilities (e.g., AWS Greengrass, Azure IoT Edge).

2. **Why preferred (1.5 marks):**
   - **Bandwidth savings:** Reduces upstream traffic by **80–90%** through local event filtering.
   - **Latency reduction:** Sub-second response for time-critical loops (traffic signals).
   - **Resilience:** Continues operation during cloud outages.
   - **Security:** Sensitive citizen data can be anonymized locally before cloud upload.
   - **Cost:** Lowers cloud storage and egress bandwidth bills.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A — (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Series I]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **five-layer IoT architecture** for a Smart City deployment. Explain the function of **each layer** with **two examples** of components/technologies per layer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A smart-city pilot deploys **8,000 air-quality sensors** sampling **5 parameters at 12-bit resolution** every **2 minutes**. Compute the aggregate data rate in **kbps** and the **daily data volume in GB**. State two engineering recommendations to handle this data volume efficiently. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Q.A (a)**

**Five-Layer IoT Architecture Diagram (3 marks):**

The student must draw/describe the layered architecture:

1. **Perception Layer (1 mark):** Physical data acquisition using sensors and actuators.
   - Examples: DHT22 (temp + humidity), MQ-135 (air quality), ultrasonic fill-level sensor, inductive-loop traffic counter, smart electricity meter, IP camera.

2. **Network/Communication Layer (1 mark):** Reliable data transport.
   - Examples: LoRaWAN (long-range, low-power), NB-IoT (licensed cellular), Wi-Fi/IEEE 802.11 (high bandwidth), Zigbee/IEEE 802.15.4 (mesh).

3. **Middleware/Edge Layer (1 mark):** Local processing and protocol translation.
   - Examples: IoT gateway (Raspberry Pi-based), AWS Greengrass edge runtime, Cisco IoT Gateway.

4. **Application Layer (1 mark):** Domain-specific service delivery.
   - Examples: Traffic control dashboard, SCADA water-utility interface, citizen mobile app for parking.

5. **Business Layer (1 mark):** Strategic decision-making and SLA monitoring.
   - Examples: Municipal KPI dashboard, ROI analytics engine, SLA monitoring tool.

*(Award full 7 marks if all five layers are clearly explained with two examples each, plus a labelled diagram.)*

---

#### **Model Solution — Q.A (b)**

**Step 1 — Given parameters.**

$$N = 8000,\quad k = 5,\quad b = 12\ \text{bits},\quad f_s = \dfrac{1}{120}\ \text{Hz}$$

**Step 2 — Per-sample size per node.**

$$S_{node} = k \cdot b = 5 \cdot 12 = 60\ \text{bits/sample}$$

**Step 3 — Per-node data rate.**

$$R_{node} = 60 \cdot \dfrac{1}{120} = 0.5\ \text{bits/sec}$$

**Step 4 — Aggregate data rate.**

$$R_{total} = N \cdot R_{node} = 8000 \cdot 0.5 = 4000\ \text{bps} = 4\ \text{kbps}$$

**Step 5 — Daily volume in bits.**

$$V_{day} = 4000 \cdot 86400 = 3.456 \times 10^{8}\ \text{bits/day}$$

**Step 6 — Convert to GB** (1 GB = 8 × 10⁹ bits):

$$V_{day} = \dfrac{3.456 \times 10^8}{8 \times 10^9} \approx 0.0432\ \text{GB/day} \approx 43.2\ \text{MB/day}$$

**Engineering Recommendations (2 marks):**

1. **Edge filtering:** Discard redundant readings within ±2% threshold at gateway — reduces cloud load by ~80%.
2. **Data compression:** Use delta-encoding or LZW compression before MQTT transmission.
3. **Tiered storage:** Hot data (last 24 h) on SSD; cold data archived to object storage (S3/Glacier).
4. **Adaptive sampling:** Increase sampling rate only during pollution-alarm events.

---

### **Question B — (14 Marks) — Internal Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Series II Alternative]**

**(a)** Explain **Smart Waste Management** as an IoT use case. Describe the system architecture, the sensors and communication technology used, and the algorithm for **dynamic route optimization**. Discuss **two measurable benefits**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A LoRaWAN gateway for a smart-streetlight deployment has **B = 250 kHz** bandwidth, spectral efficiency **η = 0.6**, and each lamp post requires **R = 200 bps**. The street has **300 lamp posts in a row**.  
**(i)** Compute the maximum nodes per gateway. **(ii)** How many gateways are required? **(iii)** If each lamp post consumes **40 W** when ON and **8 W** when dimmed, and the dimming schedule saves **6 hours/day**, compute the **annual energy saved (kWh)** if electricity costs **₹8/kWh**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Q.B (a)**

**System Architecture (3 marks):**
- **Sensing:** Ultrasonic fill-level sensors on each smart bin (measures waste height every 30 min).
- **Communication:** **NB-IoT** modules transmit fill-level + GPS coordinates via MQTT to cloud.
- **Edge/Gateway:** District-level gateways aggregate and forward to cloud.
- **Cloud Platform:** Stores time-series data (InfluxDB), runs analytics.
- **Application:** Web dashboard for municipal corporation + driver mobile app.

**Route Optimization Algorithm (2 marks):**
- Use **nearest-neighbor** or **ant-colony optimization (ACO)** on the live fill-level data.
- Only bins with fill > **70%** threshold are included in the day's collection route.
- Algorithm minimizes total distance $\sum d_{ij}$ subject to vehicle capacity constraint.

**Measurable Benefits (2 marks):**
1. **Fuel consumption reduction:** ~30–40% savings in diesel for waste trucks.
2. **Operational efficiency:** ~25% fewer trips → reduced CO₂ emissions and labor cost.

---

#### **Model Solution — Q.B (b)**

**(i) Maximum nodes per gateway.**

$$C_{gw} = B \cdot \eta = 250{,}000 \cdot 0.6 = 150{,}000\ \text{bps}$$

$$N_{max} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{150{,}000}{200}\right\rfloor = 750\ \text{nodes per gateway}$$

**(ii) Number of gateways required.**

$$G = \left\lceil \dfrac{300}{750}\right\rceil = 1\ \text{gateway}$$

**(iii) Annual energy savings.**

Power saved per lamp post:

$$\Delta P = 40 - 8 = 32\ \text{W}$$

Daily energy saved per lamp:

$$E_{day} = 32\ \text{W} \cdot 6\ \text{h} = 192\ \text{Wh/day}$$

Total daily savings for 300 lamps:

$$E_{total,day} = 300 \cdot 192 = 57{,}600\ \text{Wh} = 57.6\ \text{kWh/day}$$

Annual savings (365 days):

$$E_{year} = 57.6 \cdot 365 = 21{,}024\ \text{kWh/year}$$

Annual monetary saving:

$$\text{Cost saving} = 21{,}024 \cdot 8 = ₹1{,}68{,}192\ \text{per year}$$

**Final Answers:** (i) 750 nodes/gateway, (ii) 1 gateway required, (iii) **21,024 kWh/year saved** ≈ **₹1.68 lakh/year**.

---

### 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in Smart City IoT Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing "Smart City" with "Digital City":** Digital cities only digitize records; smart cities add **real-time sensing and closed-loop actuation**. Examiners award marks only if *sensor → actuator loop* is mentioned.
> 2. **Wrong unit conversions in numerics:** Mixing **bits** with **Bytes** or **kbps** with **KB/s** is the #1 reason for losing 2–3 marks. Always state your unit conversions explicitly.
> 3. **Skipping the Application layer in architecture diagrams:** Many students stop at Network layer. The full **five-layer architecture** is mandatory in KTU 2024.
> 4. **Forgetting to convert to years** in battery-life and ROI problems — answers in hours/days lose marks unless converted.
> 5. **Generic "IoT makes cities smart" answers:** Examiners want **specific protocols (MQTT/CoAP/LoRaWAN)**, **specific sensors (DHT22/MQ-135)**, and **specific verticals (AMI/ITS/SCADA)** — not vague generalities.
> 6. **Missing edge computing rationale:** Always justify *why* edge is needed (latency, bandwidth, privacy).

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ A **Smart City** is built on **IoT + Cloud + Data Analytics**, applied across **10 major verticals**.
- ✅ Mandatory **five-layer architecture**: Perception → Network → Middleware (Edge) → Application → Business.
- ✅ **MQTT** is the dominant pub/sub protocol for telemetry; **CoAP** for constrained REST; **HTTP** for dashboards.
- ✅ **LoRaWAN / NB-IoT** are the most-used LPWAN technologies for massive-scale city deployments.
- ✅ **Edge computing** reduces cloud bandwidth by ~80–90% and enables sub-second latency.
- ✅ Key KPIs: **Latency ≤ 10 ms (safety)**, **PDR ≥ 99%**, **Energy efficiency ≤ 1 µJ/bit**.
- ✅ Smart-grid AMI reduces peak load by **10–15%**; smart-waste reduces fleet fuel by **30–40%**.
- ✅ For numerical problems, always show **all unit conversions** explicitly — bits ↔ Bytes, hours ↔ years.
- ✅ Real-world benchmark cities: **Barcelona, Singapore, Songdo, Copenhagen, Dubai, Amsterdam**.
- ✅ **Security triad** — Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability — must be enforced at all five layers using **TLS 1.3, OAuth 2.0, mutual authentication**.
- ✅ **Interoperability** standards: **oneM2M**, **OCF**, **W3C WoT** — frequently asked in KTU 2024 descriptive questions.
- ✅ **Citizen privacy** in surveillance/waste/water use cases mandates **GDPR/Aadhaar-aligned data anonymization** at the edge layer.
